I own an older WD 1TB hard drive that is supposedly 2.5. However, every case I purchase does not fit. I realized later on that WD used to make their HDD's that are over 750GB a little bit bigger than typical HDD's. I did a little research and found out that normal hard drives are 9.5 mm thick while mine was 12.5 mm. Anyways, this case is perfect for you if you're having the same issues as me. However, since it fits all 2.5 hard drives, you'd expect it to be a little bigger than a normal case. If your HDD is 12.5 mm thick, go with this one. If it's 9.5 mm, there are cheaper and smaller cases out there.

*** UPDATE 5/13/2009: After using this great little switch very successfully for nearly two years to share a single printer between 2 PCs, I shelved it (in favor of a network USB hub), as my application had changed. Now, a few months later, I need the ability to occasionally share a KVM setup between my desktop PC and a laptop. Thanks to R. Al-Kazily's and Bruce Zentner's reviews, I'm going to pull this little gem back out of the closet and repurpose it for the KM portion of this application. (My video display has dual inputs, and auto/manual switching.) End of update ***I bought this switch to share a single USB2 printer (Canon Pro9000) between two PCs (Dell laptop and home-built desktop PC), because plugging and unplugging the USB cables at the back of the printer was getting to be a pain. I wanted the least expensive USB2 switch that could be controlled from the PCs, without loading any software on them.Both PCs are running Windows XP Pro; the desktop PC is connected directly to the switch, and the laptop PC is connected to the switch through a USB2 hub.This device simply works! Whether switching sources by depressing the button on the top of the switch, or double-clicking the ScrollLock key on either PC, the LED for the selected source will blink momentarily, then glow steadily when the switch has completed the transition. If it's *very* quiet, you can also hear the switch click over from once source to the other. No power adapter is supplied or required; the switch is powered by the USB ports of the connected PCs.On my laptop PC's keyboard, ScrollLock is accomplished by Fn+NumLock. Neither this nor the USB2 hub between the laptop and the switch cause any problems in the operation of the switch.My only complaint is that -- unlike some other USB switches (but just like my $425 printer!) -- no USB cable is included. It would have been nice if a short (say, 3 foot) cable had been included in the package; doing so should have only increased the purchase price $1.00 or so. But that's a minor quibble.Highly recommended.

The Mountainsmith Lumbar-Recycled Series Day TLS R Backpack, in Pinon Green, is an excellent lumbar pack when going for a long hike and even an overnighter if you are a true minimalist. I tend to like to use a backpack when hiking and have done so for years. Recently, I wanted to re-evaluate what I carry on hikes and not carry as much gear. When I carry a backpack I tend to fill it up with gear. After doing a lot of research I chose the Mountainsmith Day which holds 854 cubic inches of gear. This is big enough to carry all the essentials that I need to include some emergency items should the need arise. I was originally looking at theMaxpedition Sabercat Versipack, Khakibut it does not have water bottle pockets to hold water which I need since I am in a desert environment. I know I can add MOLLE attachments to the Sabercat to carry water but then weight distribution tends to get unbalanced and uncomfortable. So I ended up with the Mountainsmith Day. It should be noted that Mountainsmith did some upgrades and modifications to the 2012 Day which is slightly different from prior models. Modifications include:+ Waistbelt mesh pockets that can hold small items+ Wider shoulder strap pad+ Flat profile side bottle pockets+ Internal iPad sleeve that cannot be removed+ A Lumbar Control Point pad for increased ergonomic fitI really like the waist belt mesh pockets which make good use of space that would otherwise go unused. I do not use the shoulder strap and instead purchased theMountainsmith Strapetteswhich is a must have in my opinion. If you purchase this lumbar pack you must get the Strapettes. The Strapettes take the weight off your lower back and provide a much more comfortable harness carrying system by transferring the weight to your shoulders and hips; not your back. My pack weighs about 12.5 pounds which is too heavy to use without the Strapettes. I am carrying 96 ounces of water (2, 48 ounce bottles) which weighs 6 pounds.  If you are very tall the Strapettes will cause the Day to ride a little higher than it should on your back. If you decide to use the shoulder strap you can tuck the waist belt in the back panel to get it out of your way.The Mountainsmith Day has heavy duty YKK zippers which are very strong and durable. On each zipper is a bright yellow pull tab. I did not like the bright yellow color so I cut off the pull tabs and replaced them with pull tabs that I made from OD green paracord using a cobra stitch. I really like my modification as it tones down the yellow while the green paracord pull tabs match the pinon green color of the Day much better. I do not need a pull tab to be high visibility yellow. I also replaced the yellow shock cord with OD greenSGT KNOTS Shock Cord 3/16" - 100 Feet.Inside the main compartment is a very bright yellow lining. Included in the main compartment is a hanging pocket that has a zipper in case you need to secure items such as money that you do not want to lose. The main compartment is pretty spacious and has enough room to hold a lot of gear. The internal iPad sleeve does not take up a lot of room and does not impede your ability to carry gear if you decide not to use the sleeve for an iPad.The Delta Compression System load adjustment with two compression straps is a functional and ergonomic feature to keep the pack close to your body. This is an especially important feature if you do not use the Mountainsmith Strapettes to help support a heavy load. The compression straps prevent the load from sagging or pulling away from your body which can cause fatigue or injury to your lower back. I do experience some difficulty tightening the straps withNalgene Silo Tritan Wide Mouth 48 Oz Travel Bottlein the side pockets. I tighten the compression straps first and then add the water bottles to the pack. The water bottle pockets are very tight and it is difficult to get the water bottles in the pocket because of the tight fit. On one of the water bottles I use theBlue Desert SmarTube Hydration Systemso I do not have to stop to get the water bottle out during a hike. When I am finished with the water in one bottle I transfer this system to the other bottle.On the bottom of the Day are two  inch compression straps in which I have strapped anMPI All Weather Emergency Survival Blanketthat has 6Kelty Nobendium II Tent Stakesand 4Heavy Duty Bungee Cords Desert Tan 4 Packrolled into the blanket should I need an emergency shelter. I also carry some cordage in the main compartment of the pack. This is a very small, compact, and efficient shelter system that fits perfectly on the bottom of the Day.Overall I am very happy with the Mountainsmith Day for medium to long length day hikes. It is also nice to know I have enough gear to do an overnighter if necessary. It is durable, spacious, comfortable, and has the ability to carry two 32 or 48 ounce water bottles. Additionally, you can purchase theMountainsmith Strapetteswhich I feel is a must. The Day also comes in a variety of colors. You can also purchase a Mountainsmith Day Rain Cover but it is only available in the color yellow.The Mountainsmith Day is a product that I highly recommend to others. This is my go to pack when not using a backpack. I like the freedom it provides to my upper back and it is an awesome system.

This watch is boss!  I wanted a watch with a compass for when I am out hiking or scoping photography locations and want to know where the sun will be going.  The compass works great and is failry easy to calibrate.  To calibrate you just set the watch on top of something to keep it level and rotate it.  The band is very comfortable.  I have very small wrists and I have one notch left on the band when it's on.  I wear this watch 24x7, working in the yard, fishing, and to the office.  It is holding up to abuse pretty well.  I have a few scratches on the clasp from it getting banged on things, and I did get a tiny scratch on the face when I accidentally banged it into a metal table one day.  I really like the negative display and the indiglo light works just fine for me.  I do wish the light stayed on longer when you enable it, or that you could configure the timing, as it only stays on for two seconds.  When using the compass I often want the light to stay on longer.  If you are looking for a watch with a compass, or just a tough outdoors style watch, I think you'll be pretty happy with this.  I didn't want the G-Shock everyone has and I also didn't want to pay some of the G-Shock prices (they also didn't have one with a compass like this one has).  Oh, and the color of band is great.  It's almost an olive drab and looks really cool.

I hated this thing! Drills are weak and after one hole it stopped working. Yes I did use cutting oil! So I used my own drills. Then I tried to tap those holes, but with the included tap, that's IMPOSSIBLE, unless you drill all the way through the receiver and chamber, but then you will most likely ended up dead, having 7.62x54R exploding in your face. You would have to drill half way, and then use a totaly different tap, the "bottom tap" to finish your job. So I though about it and stopped, I decided to talk to my gun smith before proceeding.Second I went to mount the bolt handle, and after cutting old one off, I just welded the new one, didn't even try to drill and tap that. I have seen people weld those and it worked out great.So the next day I went to a gun smith and he said that instructions included are wrong. According to them you would drill on a very border or reciever and chamber, and that is very Dangerous! So I just had him install it for me the right way.Third, screws shaved off like they were plastic. I had to find different ones.So the bottom line is, if you wanna save money on installing new bolt handle and scope mount for Mosin Nagant, this product is not for you!!!  Be ready to pay 75-140 bucks for gun smith work, and have it done the RIGHT way.

I use this protector to protect my IPAD 4 (a 750 dollar investment), and it works well (well, at least the green light is on indicating my unit is protected.  There is a light on the left to tell you if the connector is not grounded (light goes on if not grounded), but my only real minor beef is I can't seem to hook up another unit in the "regular" socket in the lower center of the unit.  I actually can't find any electrical units that I have that have the three prong connection, but whenever I try to put a two prong connector in the thing, it won't take it or work.  So, for a Mac product that has that type of connection on the top, it works very well.  If you want to use this for two different connections, unless you have a three prong connector, it won't work (at least not for me).  So realize it has limited use.  Also the unit is kind of big, causing some difficulties if you are trying to plug something in on the other outlet (assuming you plug this into a standard outlet) as it tends to block the other outlet entrance.

Previously the only oil I have used on my hair - by personal choice and the recommendation of my stylist - was Moroccan Oil. Though my hair is very fine, it is also a bit damaged from being colored and tends to be a bit dry. That said, if I do not wash it at least every couple of days it can get very greasy. It's a pain, to say the least :) If you are curious as to what it looks like, I suggest that you look at the pictures on my public blog - [...]. I color my hair platinum blonde but *I do not bleach it*. The roots are dyed every 4-6 weeks, though I do try to keep the dye off the ends as it helps them stay somewhat healthy. I would say that I straighten my hair about 2-3 times a week at once and almost never use a blow dryer.I feel that this is important information because of the other reviews that have been posted for this product. The type of hair one has is of utmost importance when considering this product. If your hair is not at all chemically treated or dry of its own accord, I'm not certain you would need to use an oil at all, but even if your hair tends toward greasiness - especially when not washed - this oil still may work for you.First, it does have *a* smell, and while different from that of Moroccan Oil I did not find it to be any stronger or less pleasant or to last any longer than that of Moroccan Oil. Also, the dispenser is *way* better! It's always bugged me that Moroccan Oil comes in a regular little pouring bottle, as one has to be very careful not to pour too much. The little nozzle-type dispenser on CLEAR is far superior when it comes to controlling the amount that you pour into your hand.I used this oil on damp hair and dry hair. I didn't see much of a difference in how it worked based on how wet or dry my hair was, but I will say this - be careful when using it with other products/using it with a straightener. I used it on my dry hair, not thinking I would be straightening my hair that day...well, I changed my mind and did straighten my hair a little while later. In doing so I used my usual thermal protection spray (CHI 44 Iron Guard) and after spraying that on and using my straightener even the driest (dry as in least healthy) parts of my hair started feeling greasy :-/Finally, I have to say that price-point wise you are truly getting a bargain with this product. 3 oz for $5.56 here on Amazon, vs. Moroccan Oil costing $12-13 for .85 oz or about $40 for 3.4 oz on this site. I would say that I still slightly prefer my Moroccan Oil but as I can't always afford to spend so much on a teeny tiny bottle of the stuff, CLEAR is a great secondary option for me.

I've been using the Roku 2 XS to stream content, so I figured a comparison between that and the D-Link MovieNite Plus Streaming Media Player would provide perspective.Right off the bat, the D-Link is thirty bucks cheaper. But cheaper doesn't mean it is of lesser value. The difference is more of different value. I do like some of the things I can do that the Roku wont do.The number one cool feature was being able to link up my Android phone to the D-Link via the YouTube app, and choose videos on phone and stream right to my TV. I loaded up Freddiew's Video Game High School ep 1 after scanning the QR code with phone. The video loaded quickly and the high def quality was instantly apparent. It worked great. There is no YouTube on Roku, so this is a clear advantage of the D-Link MovieNite.Another advantage is the Kids section is available on Netflix, as it is at netflix.com.kids and on the Wii version of Netflix. This is great because it's easier to let my kids roam Netflix to see what they like without them clicking on some random movie full of naughty words and people getting disemboweled.The box itself is of decent design, a little smaller than a CD case, and a little thicker than a TV remote. It has a slit on the front to show the power light and allow for the remote's IR signal to reach it.The remote has good and bad points. It is IR based, as opposed to the wireless Roku remote, and isn't as responsive as I'd like and the cone of effectiveness is narrow. I can't sit at my computer beside the TV and use the remote. The remote does, however, have a Vudu, Netflix, Pandora, and YouTube buttons to easily launch those apps. Useful if you use those, but in the way if you don't. I have mixed feeling on the remote.An app it does not have is Amazon. If you buy or rent content on Amazon, there does not appear to be a way to access it with the D-Link MovieNite. As with so many streaming (or cable-cord cutting) options, there is no one ring to rule them all. It seems that every option is missing one or two components others have. A driving factor of which to buy is can you get the content you most want.I don't subscribe to Vudu or HuluPlus (coming soon to MovieNite at the time of this review) so I didn't test those options, but there is a Vudu 'app store' that appeared to be just free content, such as a Dexter podcast, or shows like Wreakreation Nation on Discover. This is content I don't believe is on the Roku.Overall, the YouTube streaming via phone is a killer feature, and the Netflix app works well. Lack of Amazon may be an issue for some. The remote isn't as good overall as the Roku, but both have an option to control via Android app (or iOS).If Netflix and YouTube are your main content delivery services, the D-Link is a cheaper and better way to go, but aside from the YouTube via phone, I find the Roku experience smoother and easier (plus you can play games on the Roku). Verify you can get the content you want before making a purchase.

Building a deer hunting SKS, so the compactness of this scope was of vital important.  It has that in spades; just under 10" long, using low-profile rings (link) (1" diameter, since it isn't listed in the product description) I was just able to fit it on my Choate scope mount as it isn't a pure Picatinny rail; it has a smooth section on which the rings wouldn't "seat".  The front end of the scope does peek over the front of the mount, but not far enough to be in range of ejected cases.Sighting in was pretty easy.  I started with a laser bore sighter to get a rough setting...realized my scope mount did have an angle to it, but the scope still dialed down just fine.  The covers for the adjustment dials came off easily, but were quite secure when replaced on the scope.  Note, you will need something flat to fit in the slot to turn the adjustment dials...pennies were great for this.  Site, as long as it's held securely in rings, didn't budge at all with subsequent shots.  This isn't a high-quality, precision scope (the glass wasn't the clearest I'd ever seen, and the lack of a focus adjustment is unfortunate), but it will hold its setting.If price is a huge issue (I bought at around $19), but you need a compact scope with some zoom, this is a fair choice.  You're probably not going to find a better $20 scope with *this* many features (and there aren't many here).  Durable, simple design, and it does what a scope ought to do.

Well, knock me over with a feather, but I got noticeable results with this Nourishing DNA Repair creme.THE EXPERIMENT:Okay, I'm a bit of a science geek so what I did was apply the creme to the area around just one eye.  I did this for two weeks.  At the end of that time I had my husband get out our jewelers loop which has a light source and asked him to pass judgement.  Because he's a guy, he never made note of which eye was treated and I consider him to be an unbiased judge.THE RESULTS:After studying both eye regions for an annoyingly long period of time, he announced that the left region --the one to which I had applied the creme -- had fewer fine lines.OTHER OBSERVATIONS AND NOTES:--It was strange but the first time I applied the creme my eyes burned. After that I had no similar reaction.  It was almost as if the creme needed to air out.  (I suggest you do air it out)--As anticipated, I noted that my skin felt dry and slightly irritated.  I say anticipated because I know that this is a common side effect of both over-the-counter and doctor-prescribed skin products which are anti-aging.  I counteracted this effect with the application of an oiless moisturizing lotion.--My skin is very very sensitive.  I could not, for example, use an Oil of Olay product that was similar because it irritated my face too much.  I did not have a problem using this vbeauté Eye Never Nourishing DNA Repair Eye Crème.--I noticed that a dark area --I refuse to say 'age spot'-- was lightened with the use of this product.IN GENERAL, I was pleased.  I found the creme to be mild enough for me, and I got noticeable results after only 2 weeks.  If I can come up with the cash, I plan to buy more when I finish this jar.Pam T~

This knife turned out to be larger and heavier than I anticipated. It is just over 12 in. long. The blade is 6 1/2 in. long, is full tang (all one slab of steel), 1/4 in. thick and coated in a charcoal finish. There are 5 grooves in the tang just behind the blade for a thumb grip. The slab grips are a heavily textured rubberlike material and retained with 4 hex head screws. The tang and grips have matching finger groves. There is a lanyard ring at the back of the tang. I was really expecting a somewhat smaller and lighter knife. For someone who wants the size and weight of this knife, this is the one. It is well made and looks good. The fit and finish are excellent. It does not say "China" anywhere I can find nor does it say "USA", so it leaves a question as to its country of origin. The markings on the blade are "SCHF9" and "Griffin Design". The blade is not hollow ground. The taper is wedge shaped. It is built for rugged use in all conditions. I do not hesitate to recommend it unless a smaller, lighter knife is desired. My scale weighs it at 15.4 oz. and 21.2 oz with the scabbard.The black scabbard is sturdy and well made but appears to be a polyester material instead of nylon. It came with a smaller holster strapped to the front of the sheath which can be used to carry a sharpener, a fire starter or a folding knife up to 4 in. long. The smaller holster is held on with 2 velcro type straps and can be removed. There is a strap with a snap on the back side of the scabbard which would allow it to be carried inverted on a pack or harness. The main belt loop is very sturdy and opens with a velcro type, over the top strap allowing the strap to be opened and looped over the belt (or a strap) without threading it from the end of the belt. The sheath is lined with a polymer liner to protect the sheath fabric from being cut when holstering the blade.There are two grommets at the bottom of the scabbard which allow a cord to be attached for a leg tie down. Mine came with a lightweight braided polyester tie down cord which I replaced with paracord.This is a well made, sturdy knife. The blade is well suited for chopping, slicing, or batoning (or stabbing). It is heavy enough to easily be used in the most extreme conditions I can imagine. It came reasonably sharp, but a fine sharpening is needed. Especially for the money, this is a fine, heavy duty knife.

I bought this to back up my data -- but this WD hard drive repeatedly fails on me.Periodically, for no apparent reason, my laptop no longer recognizes it. Usually I can tell when, because the hard drive starts faintly clicking.I use a Lenovo T510 laptop, with Windows 7 (64 bit).I tried PC Doctor, but while this program confirms that the WD hard drive is not working, PC Doctor can't fix it. It did recommend that I download new drivers, which I did, but that didn't fix the problem.The only thing that fixes the problem is rebooting.  Usually (not always) when I reboot, the laptop once again recognizes the hard drive.  But not for long.  Within a few hours, or days at most, I once again hear the hard drive softly clicking, and know that the laptop no longer sees it.The clicking implies a mechanical failure, yet when I reboot, and the hard drive again works, it no longer clicks.  Not until it once again fails.This is an unreliable hard drive.I haven't returned it, because it already has my data on it, and I don't trust delete programs (or even reformatting).Plus, I no longer have the original packaging.  The hard drive waited a month before failing for the first time.==================================I think I found a "fix it" for the problem.Someone advised that you should delete the WD Passport's programming, and that would solve the problem.  It didn't, at least not for me.But I've found that when my laptop refuses to recognize the WD Passport, if I disconnect its USB connection from the base of the Passport (I suppose you could disconnect it from the laptop as well), and then and reconnect it, then the laptop will once again recognize the Passport.  As least it's worked for me.Also, sometimes when I "shut down" my laptop, Windows 7 won't shut down unless I disconnect the Passport.

Purchased two of these adapters to use on a Sig Sauer 522 and Ruger 10/22. With the configuration of my Sig 522 with adapter and bipod, the overall system does add some weight to the rifle and the adapter isn't something that I would leave on the rifle for any length of time, does not look good. I mainly purchased the adapter so I can use the bipod and sight in my Bushnell TRS-25 red dot scope that I have attached to the Sig 522.Adapter was delivered in a small clear ziplock type bag with allen wrench and delivered sooner than projected.  I also looked at a CAA bipod adapter which was polymer and had a pushbutton vice allen head screw/bolt that would allow easier install/uninstall to rail.  However, the ProMag adapter was significantly cheaper, significantly cheaper!  I plan to purchase a ProMag barrel band with rails for my Ruger 10/22 for the same purpose of assisting in sighting a red dot scope.  For the price you really can't beat it and that's why I purchased two.  The adapter is functional and cheap.  I haven't gone to the range yet but hopefully soon.

This is a great soap product. That's what a "body wash" is, right? Soap?I've been using it for about a week now and I'm happy with how clean I've been. It's great for washing my face in the shower. I suppose it's OK for the rest of my body parts, but those parts don't see much daylight and don't necessarily get all that dirty.But I'm kind of ambivalent on the fragrance.My first thought was, hey, that's a very masculine fragrance. I don't typically wear heavy fragrances or colognes, and when I do, they are much more subtle. This fragrance is pretty bold though...bold like "I buy and sell guys like you all day long", kind of a bold smelling jerk now that I think about it. At first I was afraid this fragrance would come off to strong (like a teenage kid with Axe spray), but it doesn't really linger at all. By the time I get to work, I just smell like regular old me again.Then the next morning I'm shocked by the smell again. And since the fragrance doesn't linger long enough for me to smell like that throughout the day, I'm shocked by the same impression every morning: I'm showering with some other dude! Yikes. I mean, if I'm not going to smell like this product after I bathe and the product makes me feel like there's an invisible stranger in there with me, then why not just use a super hot chick fragrance and shower with an invisible female?I do some of my best thinking in the shower, but now the whole experience is super confusing and not at all productive. I feel super awake though. Awake and confused. Awake and confused and kind of curious about where that other dude went and why he was there to begin with. Surely that "I can buy and sell you" guy could find a more attractive guy to bathe with than me, right?But at least I'm clean. Awake and confused and ambiguously clean.

I used this with apple cider vinegar.  I used it with Bragg's apple cider vinegar.  I didn't know where to start from so I overmade my first batch with 1/3 cup of each in a glass bowl and mixed it with a a plastic butter spreader.  It mixes up green, it fizzed a little in the bowl.  I slathered it on my face, my face was clean when I started.  Prior to putting the mask on I washed my face with DHC deep oil cleanser.  I made sure to put lip balm on my lips and pull my hair back.  Upon initial contact there is no difference in feeling.  It takes about 15 minutes before you can feel the mask begin to tighten against your face and make your lips start to pucker in.  You won't be able to say much during this time.  I left the mask on for 30 minutes.  The first time out there was no discernable difference in my skin's appearance.  No difference in blackheads.  My skin was smoother, and probably for about 40 minutes my skin was redder because of all the of the increased stimulation.  It feels cleaner I suppose, but it isn't a miracle.  I moisturized with my Nuetrogena night cream afterward because my skin was begging for moisture.  Again, no difference in pores, no noticeable difference in blackheads.  My skin was smoother, felt cleaner.  I'd use it again.  But it's not a miracle.

I used the 6.3 light golden brown.Pros -Smells fantastic! I am a dyeing newbie but the ammonia from the other box dyes I used I'm sure fried some brain cells. So this product was a pleasant experience.Cons -The bottle has got to be redesigned. It is awkward to hold and not user friendly whatsoever. The design is a good concept on paper but not practical at all.Didn't really make my hair softer than before, even with the conditioner. Surprisingly, the other box dyes I used with the ammonia left my hair feeling better than this one - strange.Will I use this product again? I might give it another try.My hair saga is as follows:I have never dyed my hair before so I had the requisite virgin hair. It is a soft black and I really wanted the color lifted rather than bleached to a brown color.  I wanted to stay away from bleach because I did not want my hair to turn orange.So I chose the L'oreal Paris sun-kissed caramels UL51 hi-lift natural brown.Prior to dyeing, I didn't wash my hair for a day. I followed the instructions.  I wanted a subtle ombre effect so I only applied the dye from chin length to the ends. I left it on for 30 minutes. The effect was so subtle, I could barely tell anything had been done to my hair!Having gotten over my fear of dye, the next day I went on a dye rampage (not advisable given the fact that no professional has ever colored my hair - I just watched a lot of Youtube videos).I wanted to stay with the ash/cooler dyes to avoid the orange effect so I chose the L'oreal sun-kissed caramels UL61 hi-lift ash brown. I washed my hair first with a clarifying shampoo, blowdried then followed the directions. The color lifted at least two shades of brown, which was good.But I didn't stop there. I then washed my hair again with a clarifying shampoo, blowdried, then applied Garnier Olia 6.3 light golden brown. It made it darker. Not good.So I washed my hair again with a clarifying shampoo, blowdried, then applied another layer of the L'oreal sun-kissed caramels UL61 hi-lift ash brown. My hair was the right shade of subtle dark brown. I love the effect of my soft black hair's subtle gradation to the brown from roots to tips.If you're fearing for the damage to my hair, you're not alone. I figure 20+ years of no chemical processes and then 4 box dyes in 24 hours should balance each other out.  Anyway, I applied the Pantene overnight miracle to my hair and now my hair feels even softer than before.The next day my hair lightened even more so it is now a rich chocolate brown.Final verdict: If I had to do it all over again - I would have skipped L'oreal Paris sun-kissed caramels UL51 hi-lift natural brown and just used the L'Oreal sun-kissed caramels UL61 hi-lift ash brown the first time around.

In the main compartments I fit 2 emergency ponchos, 2 mylar emergency blankets,1 AA maglite, 1 pair wool socks, 1 pair gloves, water purification tablets, compass, topo map, 25 feet of 550 paracord, fire steel, 3 protein bars, 3 pairs of insta-heat hand warmers, and there is still room for more! In the exterior ancilarry compartment I fit an extra 10 rifle rounds and a small monocular with room to spare. In the bottle holder I fit a 32 oz. nalgene bottle without issue. People always say the same thing, "Old technology and you will have to replace bulbs". These people are either extremely stupid or extremely lazy. The bulb with housing costs $50 online and takes 5 minutes to replace, accessible from the back with just a few screws to remove. I did it for a friend who had the 73" model. Ours has been on, left on, etc for 2+ years and still on first bulb. I can buy a hell of a lot of bulbs before this TV costs me as much as the LED TVs that cost 3-4x more. The argument against is very dumb. Side by side the picture looked as good if not better than the LCD and LED TVs.If you are someone that is lazy and doesn't like to do things for yourself, then you will probably have to pay $300 to have bulb replaced and shouldn't buy this TV. If you can turn a screwdriver and removed a couple screws, this TV will last a long time.

I was excited about this unit due to ability to save track, get all stats online with Strava or Garmin Connect. I use it for road biking and mountain biking with a third party heart rate strap. For the most part, it is great for road biking. I did not try turn by turn directions yet but it looks like it works and is pretty accurate.However, for mountain biking it is grossly inaccurate. The distance measured can be 20-30% off (less), elevation profile looks pretty bad. No smooth track, weird steps going up or down. GPS accuracy is pretty bad too. For a three-lap mountain bike race where you go over the same trail 3-times, the track overlay can differ from each other up to 200ft. With this kind of accuracy there is no way to even consider loading a course to it and try to follow it. You can't find the trail with 200ft accuracy. My phone gps in my pocket overlays the track better than this. Apparently some of these issues can be improved by adding cadence and speed sensor, but that is exactly what I wanted to avoid by getting a gps bike computer so I can transfer it from bike to bike without any gizmos mounted to my wheel. Had I known these shortcomings, I would not have bought it.UPDATE 11/04/2013: I purchased the speed and cadence sensor for this for my mountain bike so improve accuracy. Who needs a cadence sensor for a mtn bike? You must have it if you want the distance measured right. And yes, it is THAT important if you are a racer. Well, my disappointment with this unit did not improve. Some distance accuracy improved but the GPS track is as far off as it was before. It can be off as far as 500ft. And, don't get me started on how stable the sensor is on my bike... It needs to be adjusted once a month, and can get out of alignment during an endurance race (zipties are not the way to go for a robust mounting system). It did that on me in a 6hr race 3hrs in. It beeped every 3 seconds auto-stopping for the next 3hrs! It will auto-stop for no reason even if you are bombing fast downhill. Extremely frustrating. I'm just a little short of taking a hammer to it and sending it back to Garmin. Stars reduced from 3 to 2. Only reason I keep it with 2 stars is because it mostly (not all the time) works for road biking.I don't understand all the rave reviews this thing is getting.

Seemed nice at the time, but I have had three arrays (different systems, equipment, locations) with a total of 13 drives.All but one drive has been replaced.  3 of the replacements have also been replaced.This is over the course of 4 years, so some of the replacements were purchased.The replacements for these started out as the same drive, ST32000542AS.Last year, a replacement was a ST2000DL003-9VT166 which failed within 1 year.At home, I've moved to WD RED drives, which have a much lower failure rate for me.I've lost a lot of faith in Seagate reliability, though I know "the good drive model" moves around between manufacturers as years progress.

I love the Iron Gym! I saw it on the internet and couldn't believe it worked. I got it home, set it up in about 5-10min and I was doing pull-ups in my house. I am trying to NOT use it (to rest my arms) but everytime I look at it I want to do at least one pull-up haha! A good thing to do is reinforce your door frames and/or panels with a few nails just incase. I have not had a problem yet with the doorframe ripping or anything though. The instructions that come with the Iron Gym have good tips for dieting and workout plans. I also use this for situps, pushups, and dips (although a bench or couch is a bit better). It's just amazing that this thing is strong enough to hold you up. I get nervous sometimes b/c it's just incredible. My arms have got bigger in just about a week (although individual results will vary).One thing I would have liked is the bar to be a tad bit stronger/sturdier. Don't get me wrong, it works as advertised and holds up to 300lbs but it's very light overall and I would have liked it to be a bit heavier. But that's just pulling something out of the air; no complaints from me. To those who have complained, try another doorway or buy the extender bar they have for it. If it's too long for your doorframe then too darn bad! People just love to complain. They go buy a lamp without a light bulb and complain that the lamp doesn't work! Good job Iron Gym (Pro Fit) and please make more items like these!

This is a really handsome case. The craftsmanship is top-notch and I don't have any complaints whatsoever when it comes to material quality and it's ability to protect the Surface Pro.However.... The Surface Pro seems to be a pretty tough nut to crack when it comes to creating a proper case.  The velco strap that holds the Surface Pro in place is not strong enough to keep it in place. The weight of the Surface Pro combined with the need to leave a large enough gap to accomodate the detachable keyboard proves to be too much of a problem for the strap.  The Surface Pro sags down into the case enough to completely render the home key useless and also make selecting icons on the task bar (or buttons located near the bottom of the screen) extremely difficult.I give this case 4 stars because of the high quality.  I can't recommend this case to people who do not travel.  I find myself stripping the case off as soon as I get home and leaving it off.  But while on travel (especially flying), this case is excellent because of its viewing angles and ability to protect the Surface from damage.

I own several knives, including Spydercos and Kershaws and Leathermen. This is a superb little knife. Although it lacks the G10 handle, the plastic handle model Dfly2 is 60% of the price of the G10 model, is well made and sturdy, and is extremely lightweight (moreso than the G10 for a handle). For a pocketknife, the blade is generous, stout, and sharp as a razor right out of the box. The thumb hole works well to flip it open, but because the knife does not have the heft of, say, the paramilitary, you will not be able to easily flick it open like lightening, if that's your bag. It does accept the zip tie on the thumb hole for a quick pocket open, if that's your penchant, baby.It's a wide and deep blade for it's small size, so you can use it for everything from coring an apple to filleting a fish to skewering a moose. It keeps a sharpness that is good for a pocketknife (but not as hard as Paramilitary, but then again, easier to sharpen, too). The pocket clip is the metal "paperclip" style, which is grippy but inconspicuous. This baby is so lightweight at only 1.2 ounces (the Kershaw ZT350 is 5.7 ounces, and the Spyderco Paramilitary II is 3.7 ounces--anvils compared to the Dragonfly!), you can't even feel it in your pocket. For an EDC in the pocket, there is none better.I think the price is steep for it's size, the handle material, and the blade stock. For the price, the manufacturer should have at least put on a SV30 blade (it's a small blade, how much could the difference be between this and the upgraded blade material?). You can price shop and see that other Spydercos like the Tenacious, Manix, etc. are, proportionally,not that ridiculously more pricey than the Dfly. So, you must decide over a more generous blade for a few more bucks, or a stealthy pocket rocket like the Dfly that you will never mind carrying because of it's low profile, great clip, and featherweight.Cheers!

I commute via bike, and as a result I'm on the road in all lighting conditions. I wanted a solution that would work for both my prescription plastic sunglasses and my wire frame regular eyeglasses, so I decided to give these universal Chums a try.The difficulty is that the ends of the sunglasses are way larger than my regular eyeglasses, but the Chums somehow manage to fit both. I just need to slip the ends on, and then a simple cinch of the buckle is all it takes. I no longer need to stop and fiddle with the positioning of my glasses periodically when I start sweating.This is really a must-have accessory if you're thinking of something like theBike Peddler Take A Look Cycling Eyeglass Mirror. Prior to using the mirror, my eyeglass slipping down my nose was frustrating but bearable. With the added weight of the mirror, my glasses were constantly in danger of falling clear off my face. When using the Chums, everything stays put exactly where it's supposed to be, and I have one less potential distraction on the road.

We've owned several camp cooksets from lightweight MSR nesting pans for hiking/light camping, through to our previous set of Tefal pots and pans relegated from our home kitchen and used car camping. We wanted a set big enough for a family of 4, lightweight/nesting for packability, non-stick for easy clean up and with a fry pan big enough for decent sized pancakes in the morning. This TexSport set does all of the above as it's plenty big enough for anything a family of 4 can throw at it. The fry pan is outstanding in the speed it heats up (grooved bottom design helps) and how evenly the heat is distributed. The set is functional and so far durable, but only time will tell how well the non-stick will hold up. I have high hopes, as the quantum coating has an excellent reputation and the non-stick performance of all pots and pans has been exemplary so far. Only thing that stops these being absolutely perfect is the potential for the folding design handles to swing back on themselves during cooking and melt over the heat. My wife is making some neoprene bands to hold these together for future trips and if the manufacturer was to include something like this they'd be worthy of even higher praise, but don't let this minor fault put you off as overall I prefer the folding handles design to having to use pot-grabbers. Set is lightweight but probably too large for backpacking- Texsport make a smaller set for this purpose and I've been so impressed by the larger set I'm tempted to buy one of these to replace the still very functional, but very much 'stick' as opposed to non-stick MSR stainless steel hiking set we possess. A really good product, very glad we bought them so far.

Other than bright shades of red in my younger years, this was my first time dying my hair any ... unnatural color. I decided to go for a little pre-midlife crisis while I'm still young enough to get away with purple hair. I only applied the color to the bottom of my hair, which was already a bit lighter from being dyed previously.  I didn't bleach my hair, and the color still took pretty well (even on a portion that had not been previously color treated). So, with this I was impressed.While it came out a pretty color, it unfortunately appeared a purplish red (bright magenta) in sunlight. It's pretty, just not the color I was hoping for. Indoors, however, it was definitely purple. As I didn't lift my color before using this dye, I can't complain too much. I was just happy it didn't turn out blue. Trying to get more of a purple color, I tried "intense purple" by Kiss which turned my hair redder. I totally lost any hint of the purple coloring to my hair.After this current color fades some, I may be re-buying purple Punky Colour to try again.*Update* I won't go into the long details of how I ended up with pink and orange hair thanks to a couple of purple dyes by Kiss, but I finally broke down and bleached my ends. This time I strand tested both Punky Colour Plum and Purple before applying. While Plum looks rather blue in the container, it came out a true purple. Even on bleached hair the Purple dye was still closer to magenta. But to be fair, I did not bleach my hair super light. Afraid of terrible damage, and the fact that keeping color in ultra light hair is a nightmare, I only took my color up to a dark blonde/light brown. This + Punky Color Plum solved my problem. Very pleased with the results!Even after a protein treatment, a couple of rounds of deep conditioner, shampooing twice, and wetting in the shower daily, my hair is still very purple. The only downside is the color bleeding onto everything! But, I won't complain too much, as I've finally got the purple I've wanted for over a decade. :-p

I've had my pn58c8000 for a week, as I write this, and I'm very happy with it.  (I didn't buy mine from Amazon, since Amazon won't ship it to Hawaii.)  Here is my take on a few issues that concerned me when I was deciding to buy:(1) Is the c8000 plasma enough better than the c7000 to justify its higher price?  I think the jury is still out.  The c8000 provides control of the Motion Judder Cancellation feature, while the c7000 doesn't, at least out of the box.  But a future firmware release may add that control for the c7000.  Most commentators dislike the MJC and just want to turn it off.  I like it, personally, but in any case, it's good to have control of it.  The Real Black Filter of the c8000 may or may not improve the apparent contrast of the picture, but it certainly doesn't hurt.(2) Will the TV buzz?  Samsung and other brands of plasma sometimes make an irritating buzzing or humming sound.  This pn58c8000 is my third Samsung plasma TV, and none of my sets have buzzed.  When my pn58c8000 was delivered, before I would sign a receipt, I listened carefully to it with the sound off, and I had my wife listen, also.  No buzz -- dead silence.  (But that doesn't mean your set won't buzz -- it might.)(3) Will bright patterns be burnt in to the screen?  Quite unlikely, I'd say.  It's never happened to me.  (I have had retained images on my previous plasma sets, but they go away after a few hours.)  What little danger there is can be avoided with just a little care.  Every few days, at least for the first few weeks, use the TV's menu selection for Screen Burn Protection, Scrolling.  The white edge that pans across the screen will make any retained image obvious, and if you see any, just let the pattern run on until it "shocks" the pixels to remove the image.  (I just now did that, for the third time since I got my set, and I saw no retained images at all.)(4) How does 3d look?  I don't yet have a 3d blu-ray player, so I can't tell you about 3d blu-rays.  I do subscribe to DirecTV, so I've watched several hours of their n3D channel programming, using the Samsung SSG-2200AR rechargeable glasses.  It looks good to me.  I do occasionally see some "ghosting" with screen graphics, when a faint image intended for the opposite eye is visible.  And I could wish for higher light output for sunny scenes.  (The 3d LED sets, I understand, are worse on the ghosting issue, but better at providing high light output.)(5) Does the 2d to 3d conversion work?  I was surprised at how well it works, but I don't actually use it very often, so far.(6) What about setting up the network?  Nothing to it, if you've got an ethernet cable connection to a well functioning network.  I simply plugged in the cable and turned on the set.  It was all automatic.  It took a while for the TV to download the standard applications from the Samsung internet servers.  I haven't taken the time yet to do more than play a few UTube videos.  Seems to work.  On the other hand, playing photos/music/videos from the DLNA servers on my local net does not seem to work at all well.  The TV can show menus of the available files, but so far it has refused to actually play anything for me.  (I do have two other working DLNA clients, a PS3 and two DirecTV boxes, so I don't think it's a problem with my installation.)(7) Do the picture controls need to be changed?  Yes, I think so.  Mine looked good when I first turned the TV on, but not great.  But there are plenty of user controls available (this is a strong point of Samsung TVs).  Here are the picture settings I'm using now: Mode Movie, Cell Light 15, Contrast 62, Brightness 72, Sharpness 15, Color 41, Tint 50/50, and Advanced Settings: Dynamic Contrast Low, Gamma +1, Flesh Tone -9, and Picture Options: Color Tone Warm2, Motion Judder Canceller Smooth, and 3D: Depth 7. Other controls I have at their default values.  It looks great, to me, but it might be a little dark toned for some. (Settings edited 7/25/10 to make colors more subdued.)(8) What settings to use for 3D? The shutter glasses cut out some light and impart an orangey tint, so to get a natural looking picture, you have to compensate, somehow. I'm not all that happy with the following settings, but it's the best I've been able to do.  Mode Standard, Cell Light 18, Contrast 76, Brightness 68, Sharpness 20, Color 43, Tint 54/46, and Advanced Settings: Dynamic Contrast High, Gamma +1, Flesh Tone -9,and Picture Options: Color Tone Cool, Motion Judder Canceller Smooth. (3D settings edited 8/1/10.)(9) Screen reflections?  I have my TV in a very light room, with multiple windows on three walls.  There is a window behind my chair, and I do see its reflection in the TV screen, during the day.  It's slightly diffused by the TV's screen coating.  It doesn't bother me, and my wife hasn't mentioned it, one way or the other.--Greg

Strictly as a DVD player -- its main function -- the Oppo DV-980H is superb.  It also has a much better audio subsystem than almost all other DVD players on the market. and can play just about any kind of CD (including Super Audio, or SACD) or DVD disk you put into it -- except Blu-Ray, for which you will need a separate player.  Its shortcomings are few and minor.The DV-980H is an upconverting DVD player; that is, it uses computer algorithms to convert the 480p output native to standard DVD video disks to the highest resolution your television set/display can show, up to 1080p.  While upconverting is becoming a common feature on many lesser players these days, the algorithms differ from brand to brand, and Oppo's upconversion is excellent.  Even older recordings such as "West Side Story" are rendered in a good pseudo 1080p.  Of course, the Oppo cannot supply data that is not there on the disk, but it fakes it really well.Not uniquely, but uncommonly, the Oppo DV-980 has a front panel USB input, which allows you to use your high-definition, large-screen, flat-panel television set to view the pictures you have taken with your digital camera.  The quality of the display is excellent, and quite likely larger than you are able to show on your computer monitor, although the display through the DVD player is limited to 1080 lines of vertical resolution.  However -- and this is one of the Oppo's minor imperfections -- navigation of your library of digital photos is unergonomic and sluggish.  The Oppo is a supplement to, and not a substitute for, your favorite computer-based image-sorting application.In some operations -- again, especially when viewing material from the USB input rather than from disk -- the response to the remote control is sluggish.  The front panel's navigation keys are more responsive, and are an adequate substitute in such situations, if you do not mind having to be within arm's length from the DVD player.All things considered, this is an excellent DVD player and Super Audio CD player for the primary functions for which you probably want to use it.  Its imperfections are in its minor and secondary functions.

To say that this is a "bad" mascara would be very untrue. However, because we all have differences in personal style, this mascara is incapable of making every single person who tries it out 100% happy. To me, it is just okay, but to someone who is my complete opposite on the style scale, this may be your favorite mascara. When it comes to my eye makeup, I usually prefer earthy, neutral eyeshadow paired with a very dramatic lash. I like lashes that are long, dark, and voluminous--borderlining on falsies, but not quite there. Maybelline Full 'N Soft just doesn't do that for me. In my opinion, this mascara is for women (or men--hey, I don't discriminate) who would like to look casual during business hours or for teens who are new to makeup and going for a natural look. As far as Maybelline's promise of fuller, softer, healthier, clump-free lashes; I would say that they get a 3/4 rating. While traditional mascaras make your lashes brittle and prone to flaking by the end of the day, Full 'N Soft made my lashes feel as if I weren't wearing any makeup at all--which may be part of the problem, depending on how you look at the situation. When I removed Full N'Soft, my lashes felt healthier than they normally would bare. Also, the mascara delivers on its promise of being clump-free. In a(n)(unsuccessful) attempt to create my more dramatic look, I applied no less than five good coats of this product. While I was silently hoping for at least a little clumpiness to bring the illusion of fullness, this mascara stubbornly refused to tack up on my lashes. Once again, you decide whether that is a positive or negative quality. But when it comes to "full", Maybelline Full 'N Soft just did not deliver to me. My lashes were only minimally more voluminous than when I first put on the mascara (I have moderately thin lashes), and it took 3+ coats to make me look and feel as if I were wearing any makeup at all. So for those of you who want only the most natural enhancement of your lashes to get you through the work day or through your first day of high school, I recommend this mascara. But for those of you (like me) who want lashes that would make even the most draggy of drag queens green with envy, I suggest you better listen to your mama and shop around.

Perhaps as a result, I ended up with a small piece (1/16 inch long) of some kind of material (maybe a small thread of some sort?) on the screen.  I missed it when applying the protector only because I did not have the screen on.I would recommend following the directions and keeping the screen with a light background so you can be sure nothing gets between the screen and the screen protector.  To me, that small piece of material is no big deal and it doesn't really bother me.Highly recommend this product.
I love this thing.  It's light, it's sharp, and it's versatile. The quality is very good too.  Works great attached to my pack.

Purchased to protect a healing scar from skin cancer removal.  Rather small tube for use over the whole body.  Would be very expensive for general use.  Otherwise a great quality product.  I like that the zinc oxide disappears shortly after application.

This is easily the best new idea I've ever seen for staying warm on the road outdoors in winter. Rather than having to wear heavy mittens that make it impossible to operate controls, these thick neoprene rubber overmitts fit over handlebars, allowing the rider to wear much thinner gloves that allow godd sensation and control operation, while still benefitting from the thick water and wind-proof protection of the bar mitts by merely grabbing the handlebar through their wide easy opening.At the other end, there are velcro strips to ensure needed control cables can pass through the mitts, while still blocking as much cold air as possible.Although Bar Mitts also come in various other shapes for bicyclists, I find this shape ideal for its easy insertion and removal of gloved hands from my recumbent bicycle.Highly recommended!

First of all, let me say that this Dollhouse Pink lipstick is a beautiful shade that enhances my medium coloring and reddish hair very nicely. I wear it with a pink blush and a touch of pinkish highlighter under my brows and a navy eye liner. Love it for a girley-girl mood!Secondly, if you purchase this Mega Last Lip Color understand that it is a very matt finish. It's not glossy. It doesn't glide on and look like a sheer touch of color. Once you put this on, it stays on -- almost to the end of your day! Try using a lip brush for a nice effect, or you might want to try putting on a touch of moisture on your lips before application or after for a shinier look.Third -- the price is amazing for a product like this. This was only a couple of dollars at a discount/variety store.In essence, Wet n Wild Mega Last Lip Color is a super product, if it meets your requirements for a matt-only lipcolor! If you are looking for a shiny gloss, look elsewhere!
I happen to like this shower gel and will continue to purchase it on a regular basis.  It is very emollient and smells good too.
The cable arrived on time and appears to be of good quality. Plugged it in and works great. Even came with covers for the ends.

This device has been perfect for me. I have it paired with a the Energy 5.1 Take Classic speakers (planning to upgrade to 7.1 eventually) and have been very impressed by everything. The sound quality is great for a normal, non-audiophile like me, and I clearly get the feeling of "surround sound" in movies and games. To me (again, as a non-audiophile), it sounds very close to being in a movie theater, and as that's the heart of the product, I think it does it's job very well. It's great with music and normal TV as well. I found it's large number of HD inputs very useful, and switching between everything is a breeze.I was surprised to see how much I enjoy the networking capabilities, as I can steam music directly from my computer library using the onboard software as long as they are both connected to the network. This has actually been a very nice feature for me, as it lets me easily use my nice new audio system without dragging cables from my computer to my TV.The only negative (and it's not really a negative) is that this thing is big. It's large in size and very heavy, to the point where I don't really want to put it on my wall-mounted shelving units (though I did have it there for a while and it was fine). This is probably just my paranoia, as I've never had a nice home theater system like this, but it's the only "bad" thing I can come up with. I haven't encountered any software issues or anything like that, so there's no negative for me here. I've had it up and running for about a year now, so I can safely say it's been a reliable product in my case.Again, I'm a novice at this home theater stuff, but I was very impressed with both the performance and the feature set of this device, and would definitely purchase it again

I bought this bar of soap because of all the information I've read about how dead sea mud (anything) helps with skin ailments.  I have excema (maybe rosacea) on my cheeks so I decided to take the chance.  I used it one night before retiring and the next day, it appeared that the redness had subsided and was less "angry." My forehead, once bumpy from the ailment, was now beginning to clear up and smooth out.  I now use it about every other day.  I'm a believer

To give a little background,I am an avid knife collector and have many of the cold steel machetes.This is by far the best looking,nicest machete I own.I have the 2 handed Katana,the 2 handed panga and several other and this,by first impressions just "feels right".Now this,at least by me,will not be used as a machete,but will be used for the zombie Apocalypse of course :) This 100%,in my opinion,a sword.Big,tough,sharp and nasty.When my fiancee seen it,even after seeing all my other weapons,said"good lord!!!" I liked this mouse, but it's not the best one I've ever had. The Microsoft model that I just picked up is actually a superior product overall. Although, the one thing present in the Logitech mouse and absent in the MS mouse is a super-high functioning scroll wheel. The great thing about having such a good scroll wheel is that with a Word file I can go between 100 percent to 200 percent size by slight degree; whereas, with my new one, I simply cannot do that. Another advantage is that this mouse has an on/off button which saves some juice and keeps you from buying as many 10 packs of AA batteries at the store. I do think that my MS optical is more sensitive and requires less wrist effort to move around, so, on aggregate, I'd say it's a better buy.
I love this leatherman, I carry it every day.

Having looked all over for the right case for my new Panasonic AG-HMC40 Pro cam corder I took a shot with this CaseCrown and couldn't be more pleased.The case simply fantastic as it allows for more room than I expected, is wonderfully thought out - there are pockets virtually everywhere, you can use the dividing walls to customize any configuration you like and it uses air cell technology.Air cell shock absorbing technology trumps convex foam any time - when you've invested 2 grand in a camera not to mention the add ons that's well worth some serious consideration.Most of the cases I looked at on line and in retail shops were in excess of $200 I would say a few may have been equal to all this case offers but many were sub par and lacking - or you would be paying mostly for the brand name and not the actual benefits of the case.Smart and high quality touches abound. The case is made of a tight nylon weave around a hard material. Both end pockets are deep and wide, perfect for light meters and such and the oversized Velcro flaps seal over the pocket preventing any rain from getting inside.There's a front and back outside compartment as well - perfect for folders, paper work and some accessories. The zippers are strong and secure and open, close and lock with ease. The center compartment is huge! It's more than enough room for the camera, lenses and any and all related accessories you might need.The included four panels make customizing the inner compartment to that  days needs effortless. The inside walls of the chamber are covered with Lycra Air Cell Technology protection so you know your equipment is safe even if it gets bumped around from time to time.The case closes using two plastic click locks and the center handle is well stitched and secure - a nice touch. Included is nice wide and adjustable shoulder strap with the underside layered wit air cells - making for comfortable and slip proof carrying . The carrying strap simply clicks onto a metal hoop at each end of the case - the fasteners swivel which will keep the strap from twisting around while walking - another welcome and unexpected touch.Just brilliant in design and the overall build quality is superb. It's great to see a product designed to do exactly what it's supposed to and do it well. I would recommend this case to anyone.

I got this case, so that I could store my Minolta Strobe-flash units and accessories.  It came as described, with the pick-foam and the optional dividers.I decided to use the pick-foam and set up my gear on top to map out where I wanted to remove the foam.  I found the process to remove the foam was slow, so I used a craft knife and carefully cut the foam and that did help a lot.  I had just enough space for all my stuff -- My 4 small flash-guns, my 2 large flash-guns, the wireless-optical transmitter, the cables and two adapters.The foam around the gear was nice and snug when I first made everything fit, but letting it sit up for a couple weeks on the built-in feet, I found the foam has sagged a bit.  I am wondering how much sag will occur over time, or if I need to lay the case flat for storage as in the going-to-open-case position.  If the deterioration continues, I might have to see if I can find a replacement foam that will not sag or is slightly more dense.The case itself if nice looking on the outside and inside, but my only concern is that the walls of the case are so thin, that when I carry the case, the walls bulge a little.  I figure this is a case for storage only, but if you're going to be travelling with it, and are going to be using this a LOT, I would not recommend it.  In my situation, since I AM using it merely for storage of old, but still usable equip, which I might use to augment my existing newer system via the adapters, the case is acceptable for my personal needs.  For pro-work, I would not even consider this case. I would rely on my tough, rugged and robust Pelican cases for that.So a star off for the crappy pick-foam, and another star off for the cheap-quality case.

I got these for my son based on the reviews, but not really expecting much.  Boy, was I wrong.  These earclips are so comfortable (don't know what the other reviewers were talking about) and light that they don't cause discomfort after wearing for an hour like my other headphones do.  The overear pad is very, very comfortable and the behind ear clips don't irritate at all.  I wear glasses and these don't cause any pressure points. I can't tolerate earbuds, and I've never found a comfortable pair that will stay in my ears, so overear pads are what I gravitate towards.  I like the size of the earclip style for transportability, but these are a bit larger than some.  Perhaps that's part of what lends itself to being so comfortable.And then there's the sound.  Holy Moly, what a difference.  This is the best sounding pair of lightweight headphones I've ever tried.  (B&Ws; and Bose overears are marginally better, but at 10x to 15x the cost, they darn well better be!) The bass is amazing for such a small thing, the highs are true, dialog is crystal clear, and the volume range is easily double what I've had in the past with these types of headphones...I play these at around half the volume setting of my other pairs. (parents, take note: these are for mature listeners who won't blow their eardrums with excessively loud settings.)I don't know what Koss did, but these are just plain great portable headphones.  The only way they could improve these is to offer these in a model with an in-line sound adjustment, and one with Noise-cancelling capability.  THAT would be an awesome lineup.I'd pay 3 times the amount for these headphones, they're that good.  At this price point you can afford to pick up a few of them.  I know I'm getting another set for myself because these make me want to throw away all my other headphones.

I had to take my cateye urban wireless bike computer back to my local bike shop. Those things never work for me. I picked up the trusty Velo 9 wired computer. All you have to do is set to either kilometers or miles per hour, tire size  and the time and your all set. COmes with  6 nylon zip  ties. You only need 4 for sure and maybe 5 if you want to tie down some slack on the fork to keep it away from the brakes. The 9 functions are: current/average/maximum speeds, Trip/total distance, elapsed time, clock, calorie consumption, carbon offset. The 4 features include: pace arrow, auto start/stop, zip tie mount compatible with flex tight bracket, and simple tire size. It basically covers everything you need. I mounted mine in the middle of the stem. You can mount it there or on the handlebars. Its an excellent wired bike computer for all your needs. Works all the time unlike those wireless models.

GREAT product.  The Blinky is extremely bright at the distance and gives a motorist a heads up that a biker is ahead of them. This is by far the best flash player you can buy... bar none.  I had the Cowon D2 for a number of years before my abuse finally outdid what the player could take.  So I bought this after having to use my iphone for a while.While the D2 had fantastic sound quality, I'll say that the J3 has even better quality.  The machbass and equalizer on the J3 is so clean that I can crank up the bass without getting distortion on most albums.  This is something I've never seen another mp3 player come close to, and it's very important to me as a bass-head.  The punch I can feel from these bass drums now is phenomenal.Compared to the D2, which had a resistive touch-screen, the J3 is MUCH more responsive with a much improved UI.  It's not quite as user friendly as it could be, but it's still much improved.  One of my biggest complaints of the D2 was how "clunky" it felt because of the UI, but the J3 is very slick.The only con I will mention is the proprietary USB cable.  Cowon has nearly always used mini-usb cables, and I loved them for that.  Now that it has a proprietary cable, it knocks just a tad off of my love for the J3 and Cowon in general.  But that still leaves it at a 9.5/10 in my mind, so it's a small issue.So, buy it... NOW

This is an excellent bookbag. I've had mine for nearly a year and haven't had any problems with the quality of it. The canvas material is highly durable and it hasn't torn anywhere yet. There's a pouch on the strap for phones or earplugs, one on the front "lid" at the top for other small things like CDs or thumb drives, a larger beneath it for maybe magazines. Inside is a well insulated compartment for your laptop -I bought a laptop sleeve too (also from Amazon) for added protection. There is also a compartment for your laptop cord for which Belkin supplies a matching cord patch made of canvas. This zips shut and slides right into the inner pouch. Against the laptop is a pouch for papers or folders, central is a large open space I put my school book in, and behind the "lid" are little slots for writing tools, business cards, maybe white out or erasers. At the top-back of the "lid" is a zipper pouch I keep my retractable mouse in (also from Amazon).My one and only dislike about this bag is that it only comes right-shouldered. I carry my bags left-shouldered so the Belkin sling was perfect until I started school and started having to carry the bag regularly. I'm not used to this and my right shoulder is killing me (as light as this bag is). I really wish Belkin made sling bags for lefties and righties. Other than that, great bag!

UPDATE #2In addition to purchasing the 50" SE50UY04, I went ahead and purchased the SE39UY04 as well. I put the 50" in the Living Room replacing the Samsung 40" and I'm now using the 39" Seiki as the monitor on my computerObservations of the 39":1 - It too suffered from the intermittent blank-out that was present on my 50" Seiki. Like the 50" Seiki, a firmware update corrected the issue and I can game/surf the net for hours with no problems*2 - Here's the reason for the asterisk, above. After about 4 or 5 hours of gaming/surfing/watching DVDs, the TV pops up a notification that it will auto-power-off in 60 seconds unless I press a button on the Remote (or on the TV itself) This option is not something I can disable and, when the notification box appears in the middle of an epic battle in Path of Exile, it can be annoying. I have contacted Seiki about the possibility of making this a selectable option in the menu via a firmware update but I have not heard back from them regarding this issue.3 - The 30" Seiki required a bit more tweaking to dial in the color to where I like it. Not that it was a "big deal", mind you, it's just something I thought I'd mention in case someone bought it and decided that, "out of the box" the colors were "off". There are plenty of online calibration sites available, and Windows itself has a monitor color calibration wizard that I used, then I tweaked it a little bit more.4 - Using the 39" as a computer monitor, versus the 50", is a better fit for me as I am not craning my neck to see the top of the screen anymore. My neck is far less fatigued after several hours of use than it was when I used the 50" Seiki.5 - Even though the remote control for the 50" is "serviceable". the remote control unit for the 39" Seiki is far better. It's got a better layout, different sized buttons so you can tell, by feel alone, which buttons you're trying to manipulateI cannot create a product review on the 39" Seiki. Amazon seems to think that this review of the 50" serves the same as a review for the 39" Seiki, so they won't let me post a review of the 39" Seiki since this review of the 50 Seiki exists...odd.UPDATE #1 When I purchased this unit and came here to post my review, there were 2 reviews here that gave the unit 3 stars and 1 star. For whatever reason, they are now gone. While I have nothing but praise for the item, those dissenting voices should be heard as well. so keep in mind there were 2 people who did not think it was a great TV.Personal note on those two reviews: The reviewers were idiots who failed to do their homework before their purchase and never even color calibrated the unit and then tried to compare it to their $5k+ top of the line, comes with a happy ending included plasma, but at least they spoke up and provided some entertainment value to the debateI purchased this unit several weeks ago and it came in last week. I bought it to replace the Dell U3011 30" LCD Monitor on my computer in order to gain some sorely needed real estate.I cannot say enough good things about it. It has so little light bleed that I had to hunt for it to find any at all. (rare for any TV in this price range)The colors are crisp clear and vibrant. Blacks were decent, not great. (You should access the Service Menu and turn down the backlight a little. It really helps)Picture Quality on my BluRays and BluRay Rips is great. Even 10-bit 720p content looks great on this panel. It's awesome to have a full web page open while watching a movie playing in a 39" diagonal window (yeah, I actually measured it with a tape measure)As a tool for improving productivity, it's great. I can have 3 large spreadsheets open along with MS Access and I'm not spending half my time minimizing and maximizing windows.On a lark, I installed Total Annihilation to see how it would handle a 16 year old computer game and the picture was amazing (TA will run at the panel's native resolution). I have also played 7-8 hours each in Skyrim, FONV, Max Payne 3, Bioshock1,2, and Inifinite and the picture quality is awesome.One note: Running the Unengine Valley benchmark at 3840x2160 resolution at Ultra Settings with 8xAA causes much distress to my Crossfire 7970s. They aren't happy at all at having to work that hard, so I had to drop 8xAA down to 2xAA.I wasn't sure if I could live with a 30Hz refresh at 3840x2160 but after a week of using it I placed my U3011 up for sale, and I haven't looked back.The initial version of the Firmware mine came with had an issue where the resolution reset itself at random intervals. The screen would go black and, in about 2-3 seconds would reappear as if nothing had happened. Turns out a Firmware upgrade resolved the issue, and from the time I reported the issue it only took 1 day for them to correct it. Seiki seems to be very responsive to the needs of their customers and I applaud them for working out a solution as quickly as they did. I'm still waiting on Samsung to respond to the email I sent them 9 months ago about a minor issue I have with the menu on my Smart TV...go figureThere are no bells and whistles on this unit. You get a panel, some speakers, and a fair amount of connectivity options. That's it. turns out, that's really all you need. Seiki isn't trying to cram 200 options down your throat that you'll never use and charge an additional $3k for them.I hope more people take advantage of what has been a surprisingly great TV deal so that it sends a clear message to other mfgrs that they need to concentrate on getting 4k2k rolled out for less than $25k per TV (Sony, WTF are you thinking?!?!?!)

I bought this mouse for an extra computer I have. At home and at work I use the same mouse: Logitech MX Revolution. I absolutely love it. But, I wanted to go really ergonomic this time and this mouse from Microsoft has a steeper angle which I liked.The problem with this mouse is that it's really dated in every possible way. It feels like a top-of-the-line mouse... from 10 years ago.- The wireless technology is extremely dated. Not only the receiver is huge, almost as big as a mouse itself, but its signal strength is extremely weak and unstable.- The scroll wheel feels dull and unresponsive. Any mouse from Logitech bought within the last 3 years has a better scroll wheel.- The optical technology is dated as well, nowhere close to the versatility of newer laser mice.Finally, the back/next buttons are located way too high for them to be reached with comfort. I actually had to look at the mouse in order to find them with my thumb when I wanted to use them.Honestly, I don't think Amazon or Microsoft should be selling this mouse anymore, especially with all those mice with tiny and efficient Bluetooth receivers and decent scrolling mechanisms.

Pull the router out of the box; plug in the network cable between my laptop and the router; supply the network SSID, ISP IP information, WEP key, and IP range for DHCP.  Unplug router from laptop, plug router into DSL "modem", slip wireless card into laptop.Network connection says the wireless connection is excellent, but the IP address it gave me is 0.0.0.0, so that's not right.  Try a few more times with "ipconfig release/renew, "repair connection", pulling and reinserting the card, still nada.Restore old wireless router (Apple Airport), browse over to netgear.com, get the latest firmware (5.03), install on new router, put new router back in network.Network connection says the wireless connection is excellent, and the IP address is right!  "yay"  But wait, now it's gone! "boo"  Now it's back! "yay"  Now it's gone! "boo".  Move laptop next to wireless router and the connection stabilizes but it takes 5-10 seconds to get to each admin page.  Local throughput on a file copy approached 50kb/sec for at least 30 seconds.  Web connections are hopeless.  Manual says to try a different channel.  Still awful.  Manual says to disable WEP.  Still awful.Send email to tech support with details of problems including the fact that I upgraded to 5.03.  They tell me to upgrade the firmware to 5.03.  I am getting a little upset and tell them I have already upgraded the firmware.  They give up and send me to phone support.  I call Netgear and explain my problems including the frustrating experience with email support.  Phone support tells me to upgrade the firmware to 5.03.  I sigh quietly and explain (one more time) that I have already upgraded the firmware.  Phone support sounds puzzled by this.  Phone support puts me on hold, and later tells me to change channels, I do so with no effect.  Phone support seems baffled, I ask if it could be that the product doesn't actually function as advertised?I give up on phone support and send the Netgear MR814v2 back to Amazon for a refund.
