I recently purchased the 2720 when my Magellan Rodadmate 700 went on the fritz.  (Turns out it was not really broken afterall, I had just stored it with something on top of the screen and it needed a day to recover).  After a week of comparing the two, I returned the 2720.I am generally a big fan of Garmin products.  Their handhelds and aviation units are world class.  The main reason I bought the 700 two years ago was that at the time it was the only unit with a built in HDD.  I was surprisingly pleased with it but now thought I would give the latest Garmin StreetPilot a shot.  I find it to be a good unit with some innovative features such as the real time traffic update option.  Still, it has some surprising shortcomings in the software realm.Lets start with the screen.  The good news is that the resolution and readability in all conditions is outstanding.  Definitely best in class.  COmpletely visible in sunlight while the night mode is nice and easy on the eyes.  The bad news is that it is physically a bit smaller than the Magellan.  This would not be a big deal were it not for the fact that 1/4 of that smaller screen is taken up by a speed indicator and estimated time of arrival on the right side.  This results in a map view that is MUCH smaller than that of the Roadmate.  Look at the screenshots of the two units, you will see that the Rodamate displays its information using a semi-transparent overlay at top or bottom of the screen and then switches to a split screen as you approach a turn.  Why Garmin wastes aquarter of the screen space is beyond me.  Maybe if they had a BIG screen like the Lowrance units but they do not.  This leads me to the next point, configurability.One of the strenghts of older Garmin units was that you could configure them in many different ways to your liking.  This is not really the case with the StreetPilot.  For example, on that right side of the screen it displays vehicle speed.  Unless my speedometer is broken this is useless information and I would prefer to replace it with something like distance to destination.  No such flexibility exists.  FYI, the Magellan displays BOTH estimated time to destination and distance simultaneously.I will say that the spoken street names work well.  Pronunciation is good, and prompts usually make sense.  The Magellan 700 does not offer this and just tells you things like, "Right turn in .2 miles".  The choice of routes and quality of maps is essentially equal.  The 2720 does show more detail on the maps.  Generally you will get routes that make sense although both units will sometimes send you on a less than optimal or logical course.  In most cases they will bothget you there.The Magellan wins over the 2720 is in terms of the navigation menus itself.  For example, the 2720 gives you a choice of fastest time or shortest time.  Magellan offers these as well as "Least Use of Highways", "Most Use of Highways".  You can configure the 2720 with similar preferences but you need to navigate through many menus to change these, and can not easily do so on a per trip basis.  Even with the existing choices, on the Magellan I can select a destination with say "Fastest Time".  If I do not like that route I hit "Back" and then canpick "shortest distance", two clicks total.  On the 2720 I must go all the way back to reselecting my destination, then routing choice, a process of navigating multiple menus.  On both units I can quickly switch screens from map to route listing (you will need to updgrade to the latest version of software on the Garmin to do this).  But even here, the Garmin initially shows me ony the next 2-3 upcoming turns, I have to push another button to see the complete list, an extra and unnecessary step.  The only interface plus for the Garmin is the inclusion of the remote.As mentioned, The 2720 is innovative in including the ability to add on real time traffic.  This does requires purchase of an additional $200 module and a subscription fee after the first 15 months.  It is a nice feature which Magellan should offer and which Garmin should build into the unit itself..  Do note that service only covers major roads in certain areas so value may vary.You will not be unhappy with the Garmin, I just happen to believe that the Roadmate is a better choice in terms of both simplicity out of the box and advanced features and configuration when you need them.  As the top of the line unit,  it really needs to offer much more than their less pricey units.  It almost seems that Garmin decided to save money by using a common interface software on all their units.  That is OK for someone who owns multiple Garmins.  However, the buyer of their premium unit expects lots of bells, whistles, and configuration options not found on the lesser units.  If you want a Garmin, you can get most of the features of the 2720 on their other models for less money.In my case I have returned the 2720.  It is good but underwhelming.  It just does not seem to offer enough over my 2 year old Magellan 700 let alone the newer 760 and 800 series.  This conclusion having been reached after using them side by side for the past week.

The card is dead, I mean it's unreadable. First time I'm gonna have to use warranty with Amazon. Thankfully, the card is so small it shouldn't cost much to ship it whatever I'm gonna have to ship it to from Latvia.*** EDIT ***From the moment I've contacted Patriot customer support, it took about a week to get an RMA#, which is essential to have your broken card switched for the working one. Then I sent the card (paid around 3 bucks) from Latvia to Holland, and in a little over two weeks the courier delivered a new card right at my address. It's the first time I'm having to deal with return of a broken product on international warranty basis. So, I think it all went very good. The customer service guys were polite and responsive. So, overall I am now happy with the fully functional card.Done a quick speed test on my fast notebook using USB3.0 card reader. Here are the results based on 3 passes of each read/write type of 1 Gb data chunk:Sequential Read :  84.604 MB/sSequential Write :  48.014 MB/sRandom Read 512KB :  74.025 MB/sRandom Write 512KB :  1.019 MB/sRandom Read 4KB (QD=1) :  6.764 MB/s [  1651.4 IOPS]Random Write 4KB (QD=1) :  0.822 MB/s [  200.7 IOPS]Random Read 4KB (QD=32) :  6.825 MB/s [  1666.3 IOPS]Random Write 4KB (QD=32) :  0.617 MB/s [  150.6 IOPS]Test : 1000 MB [D: 0.1% (0.1/119.4 GB)] (x3)Date : 2013/10/21 18:19:13OS : Windows 8  [6.2 Build 9200] (x64)Works just fine with my Canon T3i (600D) DSLR. Can't enjoy its full speed though due to T3i's 21 Mb/s write speed limitation. But I'm sure enjoying the awesome capacity of this card and ability to quickly copy my video stuff from it.

I pre-ordered this monitor when it was first announced. I've now had it for over two years and use it every day. A few comments:o The quality of the display itself is simply *perfect*. It's sharp, bright, and super-responsive. The colors only shift very slightly when the screen is tilted.o The real estate here is *massive*. It's a world of difference from my 24" low-end Acer display. Keep in mind that when you're running it at the highest resolution (which most people will!), your menu bar and other parts of the OS X system will look a fair bit smaller than they do on other displays. Those parts of OS X are a fixed pixel size... so smaller pixels means they'll be smaller on the screen, and there's no simple way to enlarge them.o Using it with Aperture / Lightroom in full-screen mode: it's only now that I really see how sharp photos out of my camera are. There's more pixels than I had before, and the pixels are sharper, and both of these help to make photos look amazing.o I used my Huey Pro to calibrate it out of the box. The before and after calibration profiles are virtually identical -- that is, colors are perfect, with no adjustment needed.o The display has a Thunderbolt port on the back. You can use this to daisychain additional Thunderbolt displays, if you have an extra $1K to spend. But you can *not* plug a regular DVI monitor into this port, even with a DVI-to-Thunderbolt adapter. The second screen is dark. The manual confirms that this doesn't work.o The power, USB hub, speakers, camera, etc. just work so easily to dock my laptop too. It's all automatic and easy, and *everything* except for power goes through the Thunderbolt cable. So nice! (Kind of weird though that the power cable comes out at 90 degrees, while the Thunderbolt cable comes straight out.)o The speakers sound very full... in fact, a little bit *too* much bass and a bit tubby. Could be because I'm used to listening to everything through my laptop's tinny speakers.o One moderately annoying thing: when the computer is not outputting any audio, the monitor's audio circuit is entirely off. But when it needs to play any sound (new mail chime, etc.), you can hear the audio amplifiers turn on (click), play the sound, and then there's about 15 seconds of very quiet static / white noise before the amplifiers turn off (click) and go back to silence. This is exactly how the Mac's internal speakers work, and perhaps all computer speaker systems. The noise is not very loud at all. But in a quiet house, I notice it. If it was there all the time, it'd be less annoying than how it pulses on and off, like it does now. Turning the volume down doesn't make any difference.o There's a very, very faint buzzing sound that comes from the lower right-hand corner of the screen. Sounds like a transformer. It's not audible if my laptop's fan is running. It is goes away if the brightness is turned down below 50% or so. It's very faint -- I only hear it if it's totally quiet. Much quieter than the amplifier noise above. Also, apparently there is a fan inside, but I've never once heard it.o The glare from the glass front panel is fairly annoying. The glare is actually worse on the black bezel than the main display: reflections against a black background are a lot easier to see than against the white. On the screen itself, especially on light-colored app windows, the glare's not bad. (My office has indirect light from the outside right behind me.)Still, I soon removed the glass panel and bezel from the display entirely. This improves the glare situation *immensely*.  The large piece of glass is held on to the front by strong magnets, but you can remove it by carefully putting a couple of fingernails under the edge, and pulling it off toward the front. Or use suction cups (google around for info). No adhesive, no screws -- just magnets, and it'll come off easily. This will then expose the actual LCD panel itself. The panel is still quite reflective (it has a glossy finish), but then there's just one layer of glass to reflect, rather than two, so you get half the glare or less, plus no multiple-reflections. It also gets rid of *all* of the reflections from the bezel of course -- which were more annoying to me than on the display itself. There are then some magnets and screws and other hardware exposed, but so be it. I've seen others who have custom-fit a matte bezel to cover the innards up again. With the inner panel exposed you need to be careful about not dinging it accidentally.But seriously Apple, please offer this in a non-glare version! The matte screen on my MacBook Pro is great to read all day, so I know you know how to do it!p.s. A thousand bucks for a monitor? Seems like a lot, but I figure I'll get at least five years out of this, which is less than a buck a day.** Update: My original version of this review was written after having it for two days. After two months of using it, it's still fabulous. The glare is less annoying than it was at first. The audio amplifiers clicking on and off are more annoying than I thought they'd be, so I usually send audio to my laptop instead of the Thunderbolt display. The huge size and beautiful color remain fantastic.** Update: After 18 months, still loving it. I should mention that I've also recently purchased a 27" iMac. Apple has redesigned how the LCD and glass integrate in the iMac -- the glass looks to be laminated right onto the LCD surface. It is definitely superior to how this Thunderbolt display is made. The new iMac -- with the glass -- has less glare than the Thunderbolt display with the glass removed. Also, Apple seems to have improved their noise gate circuit -- the iMac monitor does not exhibit the same behavior with the audio clicking on and off.Minor gripe: I wish this worked with the 'Mini DisplayPort' as well as the Thunderbolt interface. They are both Apple protocols and both use the same connector. Older MacBooks use the former, while the new ones use the latter. I have two laptops -- one old, one new -- and this monitor will only work with one of them.

I have not used this product but I am going through the cosmetics sold on Amazon randomly to check the accuracy of the claims and transparency of listing all ingredients.  I believe everyone needs to know what they are putting on and in their bodies, especially the eye area.When I went to review CoverGirl Naturelux Mousse Mascara, I first went to EWG.org Skin Deep database and found this product listed.  I am using the "Black" color in this review.I am happy that Covergirl (or the seller) opted to list all of the ingredients for this product.  Several products I have reviewed either did not disclose or were deceptive in describing their products contents.Unfortunately, this product is anything but natural (although the FDA has very low standards for the "natural" description so a lot of companies are allowed to get away with using this label incorrectly.  Just because it has Water and Beeswax as the 1st and 2nd ingredients does not make this entirely safe or inert).  EWG.org gave this product an overall rating of "4" on their toxicity scoring (moderate) from 1 being low hazard to 10 being high hazard.  The data on each ingredient is listed with their potential for health hazards and side effects.  Here are some of the concerns with this product.  For the sake of brevity, I am listing only those things with a higher "moderate" score (5-4).High concerns:  Multiple additive exposure sources, contamination concerns, irritation (skin, eyes, lungs).  Occupational hazards (in the manufacturing of the product) and persistence and bioaccumulation.Benzyl alcohol (5) - Potential for allergies/immunotoxicity, organ system toxicity (non-reproductive), Occupational hazards and use restrictions.  Data on this ingredient is rated as "fair" meaning there are enough studies to support these claims but there is not an overwhelming body of evidence to give the data a "robust" score (this simply means that there are studies cited, but not enough review has been completed to definitively say this is either benign or hazardous.  Existing studies support ongoing concerns regarding this ingredient)Alcohol Denatured (4) - Enhanced skin absorption, cancer, developmental/reproductive toxicity, Multiple additive exposure sources and organ system toxicity (non-reproductive).  Data on this ingredient is rated as "fair" meaning there are enough studies to support these claims but there is not an overwhelming body of evidence to give the data a "robust" score (this simply means that there are studies cited, but not enough review has been completed to definitively say this is either benign or hazardous.  Existing studies support ongoing concerns regarding this ingredient)Phenoxyethanol (4) - Allergies/immunotoxicity, Irritation (skin, eyes, lungs), Organ system toxicity (non-reproductive),  Occupational hazards, use restrictions .  Data on this ingredient is rated as "fair" meaning there are enough studies to support these claims.  Data on this ingredient is rated as "fair" meaning there are enough studies to support these claims but there is not an overwhelming body of evidence to give the data a "robust" score (this simply means that there are studies cited, but not enough review has been completed to definitively say this is either benign or hazardous.  Existing studies support ongoing concerns regarding this ingredient)On the website sciencelab.com I checked the MSDS (Material Safety Data Sheet) on the third ingredient, cetearyl alcohol.  It lists this as an irritant for the skin and eyes.  So why would CoverGirl use this as their 3rd ingredient on mascara?I then checked "Shellac".  The MSDS sheet lists the following:  "Potential chronic health effects- hazardous in case of skin contact (irritant), of eye contact (irritant)."  With mascara commonly used daily, why would this be the 5th ingredient if it is a potential irritant?These ingredients will not affect everyone in the same way due to the unique biological makeup of each person (no pun intended).  However, there are people who are acutely sensitive to these and other chemical ingredients and it is important to check up on these prior to application and make your own decision on what you want to do.

I bought this pair as a replacement for a pair of HD 555's I bought and was EXTREMELY pleased with, as they got a short and need to be repaired. Luckily for you and me, Sennheiser's have a 2 year warranty with every set of cans, even this very economically priced one. That means if anything goes wrong with these headphones that you paid a measly $30-40 for in the next 2 years, you can send them in to be repaired for free. Sennheiser is all about quality, and it shows with every set of headphones they make.The HD 415's are not only very cheap, but for the price they are pretty damn good. they're nowhere as amazing as my HD 555's, but you're still going to hear a lot of detail that you just won't hear with ANY OTHER pair of headphones in this price range. It doesn't matter if it's Sony of Philips or what have you, in this price range you're NOT going to get any better than THIS PAIR OF HEADPHONES.It is true the Bass is a little muffled. It reminds me somewhat of Koss headphones of the past, which I enjoyed thoroughly because although the bass always seemed to be muffled with Koss, it did have a habit of bumping a bit, and that is a lot like what the HD 415's do. They won't offer the crystal clear clarity of Bass that the HD-500 series does, but if you're not a TOTAL audiophile or if you're strapped for cash like I was, these are a decent set of headphones for Bass.As for everything else, it does it great. I'd say the quality of the stereo is about 70-75% of what my HD 555's were, so for less than half the price you're getting a pretty good deal.Most amazing to me about Sennheiser is their ability to make a pair of headphones that is just.....the most comfortable thing you will ever put on your head. Yes, MORE comfortable than a nice worn in old baseball hat.This pair in particular I was amazed by, as the price didn't make it look as if the comfort would be very good at all, and if you look at the picture the actual cans look to be made from cheap and uncomfortable material.If that's what's stopping you, I urge you...BUY IT....TRY IT...this is one of the most COMFORTABLE pairs of headphones I have ever bought. The other one was my HD 555's. The first time I put these babies on though, I was SHOCKED. I literally couldn't stop smiling and laughing giddily as I realized that these headphones GENTLY CARESS YOUR EARS like a nice warm hug from a loved one. They could easily double as earmuffs for those in cold areas (such as myself) as they keep your ears nice and warm and fluffy. I'd say one could wear them for about 8-10 hours before becoming bothered at all.The only issue I have with this pair of headphones is that the cable is somewhat thin and not as protected as Sennheiser's HD-500 series, and as such you might have to be a little careful with them. However, the cable is also very long, so your only task is not to trip, you clumsy fool.

Functionally, this case does what it is advertised to do, but like any product it has its good and bad features.Pros:  * A good solid feel with a cover that will protect your Kindle from things that might drop on it  * A Bunge Cord to help hold the cover closed when not in use and to help identify the front of the cover before opening, thus helping to eliminate cracked cases on the Kindle * Self-contained reading light that draws its power from the Kindle battery instead of requiring separate batteries.  That really was a stroke of genius in the design, and it is why I gave this cover a 4-star rating instead of 3-stars.Cons:  *Weight * difficulty is pulling the light out of the cover to its operational position, and * uneven lighting of the Kindle reading area.The lighted cover is very attractive, but it's a shame that the designers of this cover didn't learn anything about weight from the cover first offered for the K2.  This thing doubles the weight of the K3, and in a purse, that's a lot of extra baggage.  It is my opinion that a similar cover could have been fabricated from a lighter material.The light produced for reading is somewhat unevenly spread across the reading area.  Some folks have decided that is unacceptable.  My feeling is that while it is less than ideal, it is a better solution than having to carry around a separate clip-on lamp that requires the use of separate batteries.  I cannot pull the light from its housing without lifting the Kindle slightly on its hinges so I can slide my thumb under it to get a better grip on the light.  That requires extra caution on my part to avoid lifting the Kindle too high and damaging/cracking its back.There is one more thing I should mention.  I can't prove this but I suspect that this case is responsible for the occassional reboots of my kindle and the kindles of other owners.  The cause may be that friction from the occassional rocking of the kindle on the hooks that hold it in place sets up a static electrical charge which, when it discharges, momentarilly interrupts the power to the kindle resulting in a shutdown and reboot.  As I said, I can't prove it; but what I do know is that when my kindle is not in this case, it operates normally, and when it is in this case, it reboots once or twice a week for no apparent reason at all; and that's with Whispernet turned off, so I know it's not trying to download anything.Ordinarily I would prefer a full wrap-around zippered case like the M-Edge Latitude to help keep the dust and other small pollutants found in a purse from contaminating the kindle, but the built-in light is just too handy to ignore.  For that reason alone, I have gone with this case.

My husband put this Proform treadmill together after it arrived.  He doesn't consider himself mechanically inclined.  In fact, I usually put things together, but this treadmill was 200 pounds.  It took him from 9:30 to about 2:30, including a lunch break and a trip to the hardware store to buy four missing screws.  He didn't seem to have any trouble besides the screws.  I occasionally had to help him hold it or move it around, so it's almost a one person (with muscles) job.This treadmill is much quieter than my last treadmill.  I heard this awful noise when my husband was running and thought maybe a part was groaning, but it turns out it was him wheezing.  I'd never heard him wheeze while running before.  He said he always wheezes while running, but I never heard it because the other machine was so loud.The cushioning on this treadmill is very nice.  The belt is comfortably wide.  It doesn't slip, which was the reason I didn't use my last treadmill.The support bars on the side don't extend back very far.  I thought that might be a problem.  Even though I don't use those bars, I feel secure when they're there.  It turned out not to be an issue for me.The front support bar takes the pulse.  This pulse monitor works better than the one at the gym.  I get a reading every time I use it.The treadmill supports iFit, but I don't use that.  It also has the following onboard workouts:* Calories: 200 Cal (20 minutes, 5 mph, 0% incline), 300 Cal (30 minutes, 4 mph, 5% incline), 400 Cal (40 minutes, 4.5 mph, 8% incline), 500 Cal (40 minutes, 4.6 mph, 8% incline), 600 Cal (40 minutes, 6.7 mph, 2.5% incline)* Timed: 20 minute hill climb, 30 minute tempo hill run, 50 minute speed run, 60 minute tempo hill, 60 minute hills* Distance: 3K run, 5K run, 10K run, 10 mile run, half marathon* ManualI'd like to do the onboard workouts, but they are not customizable.  For instance, I cannot do the 200 Cal workout if I only want to walk 3 mph.  Because of this, I use the manual mode.The digital monitor is very nice.  It displays our trip around the track, along with Calories burned, distance run, distance climbed, heart rate.  There's an onboard speaker system, which I hook to my Kindle Fire.  My last treadmill didn't have this and I'm very happy this one does.  The speakers work just fine, no static or sound distortion.  The tray is a little deep for my Fire.  I put a piece of foam there so my Fire will sit higher.  There are two cubbies (one on each side of the console) to hold water bottles and knickknacks.  The only thing missing is an onboard fan.This is a great treadmill.  I'm happy with my purchase.

I purchased these earbuds about a month or so ago and compared them to my Sony $30 pair.  As far as comfort goes, these are about the same in comfort level as my Sony and the other earbuds out there in the lower to mid-tear catagory.  These stick out more so don't expect to be able to sleep in them as they will fall out as soon as you start to roll over.  The noise-reduction is very good though comparatively, but the Sony earbuds are not billed as such so I can't really compare in this area.  I do like the noise-reduction of these headphones though.  Quality of the product- I have had the right side of the J-labs already go in and out a few times, which is quite concerning since I only have about 15 hours on them.  I really don't like the jack that goes into the Ipod because it sticks straight out, whereas the Sony and most others have an L-jack that makes way more sense.  Sound quality- Jlabs are a bit muffled, and I don't get the clear treble and bass that I get with the Sony.  So overall I would say it might be a better idea to find some higher-quality earbuds than the J-labs. Good feel, relatively easy installation, excellent instructions.I easily installed it without using either of the two spares. I had to peel up and reposition it once, and this did not result in any problems. The protector installed is nicely transparent. The feel is about as good as plastic can be. It's not glass, but it's pretty close. At first it was sticky, but that turned out to be a thin layer of adhesive left over from the outer layer of film, which wore off in about a day. (I suppose it would have come off quicker if I'd actually cleaned it off.)The edge of the film is visible, as it does not cover the curved edges of the screen. When swiping in from the right or left side of the screen, this provides a rough edge. I got used to it, but it does mar the experience slightly. The case on my phone hides the effect, because the *case* instead gets in the way when I swipe in from the edges. So you'll probably only notice if you have a very slimline case (or no case, if you are very brave).The included screen cleaning cloth is excellent.This is the first time I've installed a screen protector without any dust on the first try, possibly because of the included screen cleaning cloth, so I didn't have a chance to test the dust removal tape.I forget it's there.  It's a good protector.[updated]After two months, I still love it. It feels like glass and seems to be resisting scratches quite well!

I headed out this morning on my 13 mile ride with the temp below 40, windchill a few degrees lower. At first I kept this jacket in my panniers, thinking that with its extreme thinness, it wouldn't be much help. Yet despite wearing an Under Armour long-sleeved base layer, a T-shirt over that, and a fleece over that, I was still cold. It was all that wind! It cut right through the fleece of course.I pulled over after a mile and figured I'd put on this Pearl Izumi jacket, just to see if it helped. My goodness! It entirely blocked the wind, and from then on the ride was perfectly comfortable. I even had to unzip it halfway after several miles as I was getting quite toasty.I have also used this jacket on cool spring/summer/autumn nights (50-60ish degrees) over a simple T-shirt, and it kept blocked the wind and kept me warm.I'm 6'1, about 210, and the XL fits well and is loose enough to wear over several other layers. Highly recommended!

I`ve enjoyed using theTargus Versavu 360for over a year, but it's starting to show its age.  Knowing I'll eventually need a new case, I've made an attempt to find a better alternative.  The Belkin Cinema leather folio may be my best replacement option.As the name suggests, the Belkin Cinema leather folio, made in China, is designed for watching videos and movies.  Simply open the cover to wake your iPad and set at your preferred viewing angle.  When finished, close the cover and the hidden AutoWake magnets turns off the iPad.Securing and removing the iPad from the Belkin Cinema leather folio is accomplished using a barely visible rubber frame with sturdy protective corners.  Simply rest the iPad in the interior corners, and snap the outer corners into place.  Reverse the process when the iPad needs to be removed from the case.  The power button is accessible, but somewhat blocked by a protective corner.  Thanks to the AutoWake magnet, this is only a concern if needing to turn the iPad off while left in the viewing position.The leather exterior utilizes both grained and smooth leather to create a luxurious and professional appearing cover resembling a high end note book.  A magnetic clasp securely closes the cover and stows out of the way during use.  The back side includes an oversized camera hole and a decorative tab showing the light gray soft interior lining underneath.The decorative tab is used to tuck the cover into, when locking it into position for typing.  Just flip the cover back and slip the end underneath the decorative tab.  The soft interior lining creates a non-slip surface and the iPad is situated at a comfortable typing incline.When comparing the Belkin Cinema leather folio to the Targus Versavu 360, both cases have an attractive professional appearance, and protect the iPad.  The Targus Versavu 360 doesn't include hidden AutoWake magnets, uses indentations to determine the viewing angle, and is secured closed using an elastic band, while the Belkin Cinema leather folio is far more stable.  The only feature the Belkin Cinema leather folio doesn't include is the 360 degree rotation.The more I use the Belkin Cinema leather folio, the more I like it.  I would prefer having the rotation, but I rarely use this feature.  Otherwise, the Belkin Cinema leather folio outshines the Targus Versavu 360 in every other way.  I currently plan to leave my iPad in the Belkin Cinema leather folio.  If you don't need 360 degree rotation, the Belkin Cinema leather folio is definitely worthy of your consideration.PROS:Attractive professional appearing coverLeather exterior with soft interior liningProtects iPad 2 and 3 from dings and scratchesStable and comfortable typing angle1 year limited warrantyCONS:Doesn't rotate

I bought this with a little trepidation...  I wasn't sure it would work for my  F250 w/ a double-din stereo.  In the end, it did.  I used the screws provided w/ the stereo to mount the side plates to the stereo.  I then had to trim the hole in the dash as it is only a "1 1/2 din" hole.  Once I was sure the stereo would fit (installed on my work bench then removed), I reinstalled my dash in the truck.  Connecting the wiring harness to the stereo and pushing the stereo in LOCKS the stereo into the dash (plastic locking tabe on the side plates).  If I ever have to pull my dash, I HOPE I can get it out as the locking tabs are impossible to reach now...  Once installed, I cut the center cross-bar out of the trim ring, and it fit perfectly as a "gap filler".All this said, if you are going this route, do some research on the Ford truck forums for different methods of installation.  As I mentioned, not sure I'll be able to pull the dash out w/ the stereo locked into the stereo hole...  We'll see...

Finally took the Edge 510 out for a ride and put her through her paces.  I upgraded from a 500. About 30 miles out we dropped 2 of them and they were able to pull up their phones and see where we were in front of them.  I was nervous that the new screen would be difficult to read in the middle of the day.  That was not the case.  It is darker than the 500 but very readable, polarized sunglasses may make it more difficult.  I did find that when switching screens you have to swipe and though this was very cool having sweaty fingers junks up your screen real quick.Accuracy was very good, we were out in open country though so can't speak for city riding but can't imagine that would be a problem. I came from a Galaxy S3 phone which is usable for my poor eyesight, although I found many games have fonts that are too small to read.I now use my Nexus 7 (my S3 plan allows wifi tethering), and I love it. In apps where I use a large font, the bigger screen seems to provide about double the viewing area of my phone. Many games are now enjoyable, although some are still unplayable (the screen resolution is quite high, so games that fix a font size based on the resolution do not have noticeably bigger text on the Nexus 7 than the S3).Unlike the iPad mini, the Nexus 7 is tolerably comfortable for holding in one hand for extended periods (it's a little narrower and not as slippery as the iPad mini).I also enjoy using a stylus and "Note Anytime" to take a few terse notes during meetings -- for more involved writing tasks I would still use paper due to the imprecision and small writing area the 7" screen provides.Note the Magnification accessibility feature in Android 4.3 works, although I found it interfered with game control, making game control sluggish. So I generally keep the setting turned off, but it's nice that I can toggle it on when I need it. I look forward to future improvement (especially a different activation than a triple-tap!).

I've been searching for a USB 3.0 hub that is compatible with both the MacBook Pro with Retina display and with the Logitech wireless mouse / keyboard.  This is a great quality hub for the price but it has the same problems that other USB 3.0 hubs have with regard to Logitech and Apple OS X Mountain Lion.  I like the fact that it has a 4 amp power supply that has a separate cord that plugs into your power strip or UPS.  This is much better than a power supply that plugs directly into the outlet.  I also like that the ports are on the sides of the hub.  This makes the USB cords much easier to manage.  The hub is small and has blue leds that tell which ports are active and a red power led.  The problem is that it will not work with the wireless receiver that some Logitech keyboards and mice use.  This is a common problem that is not specific to this hub.  I don't know of any USB 3.0 hub that is compatible with the receiver.  The other problem is that it is not compatible with external USB 3.0 hard disks when you are using Mac OS X Mountain Lion.  The disks will disconnect when the MacBook comes out of sleep mode and the MacBook will display an error message.  You then have to unplug the hard disk and plug it back in to get it to re-mount.  This same error is produced with USB 3.0 media readers.  This problem is not related to the hub.  It is an Apple OS X Mountain Lion issue!  Hopefully Apple will correct their operating system and their USB 3.0 port support.  Other USB 3.0 hubs also have this problem.  Plugable has tested this hub and their website states that the hub will operate properly coming out of sleep mode with USB 3.0 hard disks if you are using Mac OS X Lion.  I have not tested this because Apple will not allow you to boot from an external hard disk attached to their USB 3.0 ports.  I also hope that Apple will fix this remote boot issue!  The hub works perfectly if you have external hard disks that are USB 2.0.  This is also true for a USB 2.0 media reader.  The disks, flash drives and media readers all work properly coming out of sleep mode using Mountain Lion if they are all USB 2.0 type devices.  My solution to the Logitech problem was to plug a USB 2.0 hub into the Plugable USB 3.0 hub.  This works!  Using the Plugable USB 3.0 four port hub and a Belkin USB 2.0 four port hub gives me four ports of each type.  This includes the two on the MacBook.  Plugging the USB 2.0 hub into the Plugable hub also frees up the other USB 3.0 port on the MacBook.  This lets me plug the Apple DVD superdrive directly into the MacBook.  This is a must because the Apple DVD drive will only work if it is plugged into the MacBook directly.  It will not work if you plug it into a hub of either type.  It seems that Uspeed also sells a hub that looks identical to the Plugable hub but they charge $4 less.  The difference seems to be with the power supply.  The Uspeed power supply plugs directly into the power strip and the current rating isn't listed in the description.  It's worth the extra money to me to have a power supply with a cord.  Overall, this is a great hub for the money.  It has the same spec. as the 4 port D-link (DUB-1340) hub for about half the price.  I returned the D-link hub because it had all of the same issues.  The Uspeed hub has the same Logitech compatibility issue according to their product description.  I gave the Plugable 3.0 hub 5 stars because the problems that I've listed do not have anything to do with the hub and the Plugable website has been very helpful.

BACKGROUNDSighting 223/5.56 mm can be a chore.  This method is a useful too for getting onto paper at your desired distance.  It helps save some ammo instead of using trial and error.METHODBEFORE DOING ANYTHING: ENSURE THAT THE GUN IS NOT LOADED WITH LIVE AMMO WHEN USING THE BORESIGHTER.  Sighting a new rifle is simple in concept.  I use a bipod for stability in a seating position.  I use a Leupold Spotting Scope to visualize the target and the laser dot.  Load the Boresighter into the magazine, and load it into the chamber.  Make the appropriate adjustments on the sights, then remove the Boresighter.  Remove the batteries from the Boresighter and then store it.  Ensure that there are no barrel obstructions before using live ammunition.  Then, use your selected live ammunition to determine where your sights impact upon the target.Another use:  After shooting, I will use this Boresighter to confirm that the sights have maintained zero by cowitnessing the Aimpoint T1, iron sights and the laser Boresighter.  It can also be useful to determine the point of impact at closer combat distances, if necessary.Tips:Always be careful when handling live ammunition.  Always remember to remove the Boresighter after you have used it.Ensure that there are no obstructions when you transition to live fire.Use the same ammo that you intend to use all of the time.Be mindful of wind and temperature conditions.Be mindful that the ballistics of a warm barrel will vary from a cold barrel.IMPRESSIONThe Boresighter works as intended.  There is no power switch.  Just remove the batteries.  It takes only a few minutes to use this tool.  Battery life should not be an issue.Highly recommended.

I was about to order a GTX 650 without doing any research but read a review that suggested I take a look at this card and I'm glad I did. I was upgrading from a GTX 275 (it had been a while) and like one other reviewer here pointed out, nVidia cards run HOT. My new build was crashing constantly because the video card was overheating even under minimal load.So I went over to Tom's Hardware to look at benchmarks for this card. At the time of this writing, the GTX 650 is the closest competitor at a similar price-point and the HD 7770 outperformed it by leaps and bounds in EVERY benchmark, more than 6 games at least. In fact, it's not even fair to compare the two because the GTX 650 performs closer to the two step-down models of this card, the HD 7750 and HD 7730. On top of that, the HD 7770 is running about 15 degrees cooler than my GTX 275. The form factor is also impressively small, about half the size of my old nVidia card... and it only requires one PCI power connection in comparison to two on my old nVidia.I couldn't be happier with this purchase. I'm able to play games for the first time on the highest settings with really smooth FPS and no loss of frames, and it's nice to finally see some games in their full, graphic glory. It's also nice to not have to worry about additional cooling had I gone the nVidia route. If you're trying to decide between this and the GTX 650, it's pretty much a no-brainer.As for noise, my PC has gone from ultra silent to very silent after adding this card. One other reviewer complained that this card was loud, but I would say that the noise level is a complete non-issue.It's been at least 10 years since the last time I used a Radeon in a build, mainly because a few generations ago the nVidia cards in the same price range were marginally better from a price-performance aspect. I'm glad I looked at Radeon this time around because Radeon cards have always been a good value for the performance, and this card is no exception.

I am using FIOS with a standard issue Actiontec MI424 W/R Rev. D modem /router. It was barely adequate with my TX1000 HP tablet using Wireless G. The problem started when I got my Macbook (Summer 2009 Edition). The FIOS router is at the 2nd floor and I would go crazy trying to get a reliable signal that won't drop. Moreover, it was ridiculous that my Mac was getting full bars about 4 feet from the FIOS router but would not be able to access the internet. I did my research and I was leaning towards the higher Netgear Gigabit Rangemax Dual Band Wireless-N Router (WNDR3700) but was out of stock at Bestbuy. I reviewed the specs and this one is $50 cheaper and basically the same configuration minus the usb port and Gigabit capability, this one has 8 antennas.Setup could not be any painless. I used this as an annex router to the FIOS stock modem/router and the results were very impressive for my purpose at least. Now I have 3 wireless signals, (1) FIOS Actiontec Wireless-G (2) 5 Ghz band Wireless-N (3) 2.4 Ghz band. My Mac uses the 5 Ghz as it is better for the Wireless-N configuration and interference free. I realized it was probably my Panasonic DECT 6.0 cordless phone that was messing up my signal. Now I am able to view online HD videos on my Mac flawlessly from the entertainment room downstairs. The other 2.4 Ghz signals suit the smaller handheld devices like the iPhone etc. I ran a FIOS speedtest on my Mac and it is just a hair below 20/5 - just beautiful!All in all very satisfied with this Netgear device. How other people need technical support for such a very straightforward installation is beyond me.

My little one started showing the signs of eczema at about 15 months.  I tried some of the standard products at the store, like Aveeno as well as California Baby.  While I do love California Baby products, the Babyganics Bye Bye Dry has been the most consistent in keeping her from having breakouts.  I've been using it for about a year now and I use it on her daily. As many reviewers have noted, this TV has AV sync problems. Some reviewers have also noted that some hd channels contain video garbage, yellow lines and blinks, outside the broadcast frame. The latter can be fixed by switching the screen size from standard to fill; this crops some of the hd image (a little to much for my taste, but not too much more than a panasonic plasma automatically does). Also, only some hd channels don't fit in the standard frame; comcast hd movie channels look great in standard.The AV sync and bungled screen-fit are problems that Westinghouse should have fixed. However (and please correct me if I am wrong), if you do have this TV or find one in the $300 or under range, it helps to know that the AV sync problems apply only to analog broadcast channels; digital channels and hd channels pulled in through basic cable sync up fine. So, by Feb 2009, with the shift to all digital broadcasting, the analog AV sync problem on this TV will be moot.Some reviewers have noted that DVD players also have AV sync problems; this is true if you are using composite cables to hook up your player. However, I have found that with component cables the AV sync problem goes away, so try switching your cables if you have sync problems with your dvd player.I have this TV hooked up to a 5.1 sound system w/ an optical cable (optical out on this TV is a nice perk); hd broadcasts in dolby digital look and sound great. The sound using optical out is much better (and better synced) than the internal speakers. So if you are stuck w/ this TV (like me) or find one for a bargain, you can work around the sync and video problems.

I purchased this as I wanted to try this type of hiking pole.  I have used similar poles, although with different handle types.  The flashlight was an added benefit, but not a big selling point for me.The adjustment of the pole is stiff.  It is very stiff when adusting, starts out like it doesnt want to move, then when it moves I always seem to pull it past the stop points exposing the springs and locking mechanisms.  Once adjusted, it stays in position well though.The shock absorbing works, but feels gritty.  Turning the shock absorbing feature on and off seems challenging as well, but maybe its just me.  When off, there is still about 1/2" of movement; when on, it has 3-4" of movement and feels 'gritty' when compressing.The handle is comfortable, the foam section on the pole ripped almost immediately though.  Though I will admit I didnt see much use for that anyway.The flashlight functioned, although I did not find it useful and would personally prefer a headlamp or regular flashlight over the one in the handle.  The on/off function was touchy as well.Overall, as an economy hiking pole, or a 'tryout' pole to see if you like using them, this is a good deal.  Virtually all of the features are better on the more expensive poles, but if you dont want to spend the money, or dont want to waste money on something you may not use, this is a good deal.One good point over many other poles is that this one is shorter when collapsed than many other similarly priced poles, and some of the high-end versions.  This is important when packing the pole through areas where you are not using it.For short walks, easy hikes, or just a trial to see if using a hiking pole is for you, this is a good choice.  If youre planning a multi-day hike or plan on a lot of use, Id suggest you spend the additional money on a better pole.

Looking at the product images and information, I was expecting a substantial clipper and kit.  What I discovered was that this kit, in this price range, is at best average and arguably less than average in many areas.The product arrived packaged very well. The entire kit contents come inside the provided average quality bag, which is really too big to travel with and too flimsy to survive any real travel anyway.  Inside the bag, all the pieces are individually wrapped.  The attachments are all of average quality and are all plastic.  Most importantly, the build quality of the clippers is just OK (for home use), at best. I seriously doubt it would survive more than an average fall or two onto a hard floor.  The blade lever is all plastic and feels like it will break if I push too hard and I think it will be the first component to break either 1) after regular use or 2) via a drop onto a hard floor.  The body, although looking heavy duty in the product image and even upon close personal inspection, is constructed in plastic and very lightweight; you know it the minute you touch the product.  The blades of the Conair however did a pretty decent job in my tests.  But, this is offset by the flimsy attachments that, while usable, are clearly not of good quality in my opinion.  They snap into place now, but it appears the plastic retaining tabs will wear faster than expected (due to plastic used).In use, the Conair does not vibrate that much and is generally easy to use.  The clippers do not vibrate as much as other heavy duty clippers I have used and does a fine job on my beard and head. While the Conair is much lighter than previous clippers I have used, I personally prefer more substantial clippers, even if they vibrate more. These clippers had no problem plowing through very thick hair. I would recommend you use the included oil liberally however (the case with most clippers).Bottom line, these are at best average clippers.  In this price range (as of date of this review), you may want to consider other models if you are looking for a more substantial kit, especially for travel. If you are in the military and plan on traveling with these, I would definitely recommend a more substantial clipper, preferably with a metal construction and particularly, a metal blade lever.

This is a very inexpensive charger. It claims to be for MP3 players but it would not work with the Sylvania SMP4029 MP3 Player, until I modified the internal circuit.  I removed the label to expose the two screws that hold the case together.  Pin 1 is +5 volts and pin 4 is ground.  I cut pin 2, leaving it open. Then I soldered a short wire from pin 3 to pin 4.  I put it back together and now it works with the Sylvania.

I really wanted to believe in the promised features of this router.  I stream a lot of online video from popular movie services.  I am not much of an online gamer though.  The media streaming benefits of this router and its ability to classify and shape traffic are what caught my interest.If you have used the previous generation of DLink routers, the web interface will be familiar yet different.  This router features a new streamlined configuration screen: dark colors, greys and blacks, with blue highlights, but isotherwise very similar to Dlink's setup over past few years. I found the interface to be slow to render and you can see the non-css version before the css is applied.It comes pre-configured with ssids, so all the user has to do is plug it in, connect it up, and go. I initially chose to reconfigure it all as an access point and add it to my existing network.  When I used this as an access point (disabled dhcp, upnp, and firewall), it worked fine, but I only saw traffic measurements after connected to the wan (my cable modem) and used it as my main router.Once I set it up as my main router, I started seeing the traffic measurements, traffic shaping, and pretty charts and graphs!In the network map, it would be nice to name devices because names aren't detected sometimes.  The network map doesn't detect other switches, access points, or other routers (if you configure them as access points).  The network map is logical, not physical in this regard.I noticed a 40% dropoff in speed tests conducted with a famous speed testing app on my Galaxy S4. Was this attributable to traffic shaping?For a device that obscures from the user a lot of advanced settings found in most high-end routers, in the goal of shaping the traffic for you, I found it tedious having to adjust priorities of every device on my network.  I thought this device was supposed to allocate bandwidth where needed without user intervention. With this in mind, I think I prefer the "fair share" method of allocating bandwidth that my other router uses.Some random notes on its functionality and specs:It doesn't support fastest 802.11AC.  It only supports 867mbps.  The top speed I obtained on the 5ghz band set to AC only was 60mbps.  This was using my cell phone, which supports AC and was tested with a popular speed testing app.This router supports b only, g only, n only, and mixed combinations of them on 2.4 band.  It also supports a only, n only, ac only, and mixed combinations of them on 5 ghz band.It arrived with firmware 1.01, which is the only version so far.It detected my TV as a Vizio Smart Device.It detected my PS3.It detected my iMac as Mac OS X and my Windows laptops as Windows until it discovered their PC names.Sometimes devices were displayed as Linux when they were actually Android phones. It is sometimes difficult to tell which device is which. For example, it detected two devices as "Apple."  I had to look at the MAC addresses (aka hardware addresses) to determine what was my Apple TV and what Was my iPad.  It correctly identified my iPhone as iPhone.  Presumably, over time, all of the proper names of each device would display.Printed documentation is lacking, as is a detailed explanation of Streamboost.The web interface times you out way too fast, making you login multiple times in one sitting just to configure some options.It is missing a feature I first saw on one of my old DLink routers: Guest Networks.Usage data (and presumably any optimizations) are lost if the router reboots for any reason. Your data does't persist.On reboots, it seems to take a while for the router to discover the names of your devices, such as your pc's Windos name.  In the meantime, you are present with the MAC address or IP address of the device.Range is better than on the Asus N66u, which is my main router.Overall, this router tries to hide technical details and gives you pretty graphs and clean interface instead.  I found myself wanting to go back to my old router, which I eventually did.

I admit that I like to carry all of my equipment with me whenever I go out.  Fortunately I am big enough to carry a heavy bag without too much problem.  I have had this bag for about six months and I have to say that it is the best camera bag I have ever used.  I currently am carrying a Nikon d300, a Nikon d700 (attached to), Nikon 17-55mm; Tokina 11-16mm, Nikon 35mm, Nikon 50mm, Nikon 70-300mm, Nikon SB700 flash unit and the Nikon 18-55mm kit lens that I use for close up photography.  This makes for a heavy bag, but that's my choice.  All the items are easily accessible.  The zipper on the top of the bag allowing access from the outside without having to open the entire bag up is great.  It is true that this is not a rigid zipper.  You do have to swing the bag to the front of your body to open effectively.  However, I don't find that much of a problem.  When I am out I am using wearing one camera with a Black Rapid strap with the other camera inside the bag, lens attached ready to pull out.  Since I can use both hands to accomplish this, I have had no problem shooting with both cameras when necessary.  I have read some reviews that complain about the bag not being secure because the cover flap does not zipper down and can puff out in a way that theoretically could give someone access to your camera.  In other words, the complaint is that the bag is not as secure as it could be.  I have had no trouble with this aspect of the bag.  In fact, despite the fact that I have the bag loaded with gear, the top easily covers the edges of the bag, leaving nothing in plain site to outsiders.  I use the side pockets for cables and such.  This bag does not have the capability of carrying a laptop, but I didn't buy it for that purpose.  The bag is a little bulky when loaded down.  I have done several walking tours with this bag and have had no problem.  I don't know how I would feel if I were doing some serious hiking and climbing, but I'm not. The front flap of the bag is roomy enough to carry small note pads, etc.  I use mine to carry additional CF and SD cards.  There are two nice sized pockets on the front of the bag under the over flap that is big enough to carry additional drives.  The shoulder strap is padded and comfortable and does not dig into you even if you are wearing a T-shirt.  There are a million ways you can configure this bag to meet your needs. If you are carrying more than one camera body and more than one lens around this is the camera bag for you.

I like these grips a lot on my specialized rock hopper. I wanted more grip and control and these fit my hands nicely. My only complaint is the outer material on the grip is a bit to hard. I prefer not to wear gloves but on these grips you almost have to. I have about 300 miles on the grips now and after 100 or so I got in the groove and appreciate them a bit more. I love the design just not the outer plastic/rubber, the inner palm material is perfect.

I'd picked up some other grippers and thought I was doing pretty swell hitting 100 reps per hand on each leg of my commute. But I didn't really feel any stronger. So I decided to try the Trainer. But really, would that be tough enough. Nah, let's got for the .5. Yeah, that should work.There's nothing more emasculating than failing miserably at squeezing a gripper. No wait, there is one other - ripping your hands apart while trying.The laser machined grips are like razor tinged barb wire to pasty white office worker hands.It's tough, but the key is gradually increasing the number of reps so you don't wear holes in your hands on these grips of terror. I've been doing this since last week and I'm already seeing a difference in the number of reps and general strength in my forearms. And it's a wonderful feeling when you successfully squeeze those twin talons of terror. I'm looking forward to upgrading to a stronger model in a few months.
Bottom line this is the best stuff I have used on my guns. It is a great cleaner and protector. I highly recommend this for any serious gun owner.

This is my every day carry knife that will meet any daily challenge that I can throw at it. This case/keyboard combo is a compact and smart idea, but because of the magnet that holds the keyboard in the case, it is considerably heavier than one wants for a tablet of this size. If you bought a small tablet for it's portability, it makes little sense to get this combo which adds significant weight. I purchased this for traveling, and now I am debating returning it. The keyboard seems very convenient and comes with a mini-charge cable. Its keys are responsive, but cramped, and if you're a fast typist like me, the placement of some of the functions will unnerve you. The response of the keyboard is effortless though. I have another keyboard for my iPad, which has better placed keys, but a laggy response. The fit of the tablet in the case is perfect. So tight, I could barely get it off when I realized I hadn't removed some of the tablet's original plastic protective coverings. But again, the case is ridiculously heavy, so what's the use? I'll probably end up returning it. 2 stars for the idea, but minus 3 because it doesn't meet my needs.

I heard on the radio where billionaire and Dallas Mavericks owner Mark Cuban (52 years old) was asked how he had suck good skin, and he remarked that a girlfriend once told him to use "foot cream" on his face. It got me to thinking about using Shea butter, which my wife used to use to smooth the rough patches on the bottom of her feet.When I first got it I could hardly figure out how to use it. It's unlike any cream in that it's literally a solid substance. You have to sort of swirl your finger around and pull off small chunk and put in on the areas of your face you want to cover, such as the forehead or crow's feet.Here's what I found: after a minute or so it "melts" on your face and you can rub it around a bit. What shocked me was its longevity. With most moisturizers, and I've probably used dozens, they soak in nearly immediately. Not the Shea butter. You can feel it working on your face all day. It's not greasy, has no smell and isn't bothersome (I'm a guy so I don't use makeup and don't know how that would work).And it lasts all day. I really like the way it works as opposed to moisturizers you use in the morning or at night on dry areas and seem to disappear, accomplishing nothing. This goes for some very expensive products as well.In summary, I highly recommend this product for the face, the elbows and feet--anywhere that gets dry. It lasts longer than anything else I've ever used.

While I have been wanting to try bar shampoo for a long time, I was skeptical about this product. Now I am really glad that I tried it. It leaves my hair soft and clean and I love using it. I have very thick, dry hair almost down to my waist that I wash daily. This bar lathers nicely with the help of coconut oil as opposed to sulfates. The smell was odd to me at first but it isn't bad. It's kind of what I would expect an "old fashioned" soap to smell like. I leave the bar on a ventilated rack when not in use. I would not recommend leaving this in a soap dish as it will get slimy and shrink faster. They sell holders for this soap, so I ordered the plastic case and will review it after it arrives.I do use a separate conditioner with this soap on most days (Organix Coconut Milk) as my hair does get tangled even though I wash it carefully. I use a cider vinegar rinse 2x a week and my hair has no tangles after using it.This is perfect for any one going "low-poo." I would also recommend this to people who want to be able to bring shampoo with them while traveling but do not want to deal with plastic bottles. It is also great for people trying to get away from sulfates, alcohol, and other damaging chemicals. Some people may go through a transition period when first using this and may experience some waxy, oily buildup on the scalp. Try to give this product at least a week to get used to. I personally only had the buildup for a day or two. I liked this soap so much that I ordered the herbal version and can't wait to try that one out.My only complaint about this shampoo is that it is not good for washing out hair grease (coconut oil/shea butter mix). If you use coconut oil to protect your hair, you may want to have a regular non sulfate shampoo on hand to wash it out.

My first unit was a failure, removing the blue insulator would result in a dim glow but no flash. It was returned after a cap was tried (same fail) and the replacement unit has been working very well for hundreds and hundres of shots now. I'm so glad I accepted a replacement and didn't go for a return!!!It is absolutely a good training aid for me. I've put round stickers on the walls at 10 and 20 feet, and fire both single and double handed, strong and weak hand. I can tell immediately if/when I flinch or have poor grip and finger placement, the spot becomes a smear or streak. There's a feeling of comfort or control or something that I get that really puts the laser spot on. I'm learning to recognize it and trying to learn to turn it on at will. I don't know what it is but is makes a big difference in my steady accurate hold and fire.Being able to exercise with this with a variety of pistols helps me be accustomed to the trigger pulls and learn what my best grip is. I try to use one per session. I'll "shoot" during commercials so there's alternating concentration and relaxation, not unlike the league shooting at the range. I'm slowly increasing speed with accuracy and seeing improvement.All this without expending a single bullet. It does translate to the range, I keep my targets so I can confirm my improvements over time. This Laserlyte makes all the difference with the immediate feedback it gives. With the price of ammo these days (post Sandy Hook) this has more than paid for itself, and so worthwhile in the learning and improvements I've made. If I lost this, I would replace it, quickly.

I love knives. In my dorm I have a USMC Kabaar knife for when I go hiking. But when I am on the ranch on campus and have to deal with ropes, twine and tension string, I knew that I needed a blade that wasn't my shoe size. It had to:1) open extrememly fast2) be sharp3) clip onto my belt4) be very good quality and not make me feel nervous to use it.This knife did just that! I saw the reviews and saw the negatives for the grip, and I agree. I don't like the grip material on the handle. It's the same material used on skateboards. But one thing to notice is that that type of material is on the part of the knife that faces the oustide (when clipped to a belt or pants). The other side of the handle is a similar look but its hard plastic instead. That's the only flaw with the knife though. I absolutely cannot stand the skateboard material on the outside face of the handle. I don't want to put my shirt over it because I don't want it to sand and damage my shirt. I am praying that the sandpaper side also doesn't ruin the inside of my jean pocket as I like to clip my knives in those tiny pockets of my jeans. So far though, I don't notice any damage to the pockets of my pants so that's a good sign. If you are really worried about this you can always sand that side a bit and make it smoother. But the purpose of the handle is to make sure you have grip even if your hands are greasy or muddy. But it's not that big of a deal- if you wear it on the outside or clip it on a belt you should have no problems.One thing I really like about this knife is how fast you can open it. It has a safety feature that prevents the knife from opening by accident (a safety that can be switched off). I like that because after so much bumping and rubbing against animals, I can't have this open up and slice me or one of them. So it's great in that respect.Though a minor positive, the knife came out of the box EXTREMELY sharp. I was able to take a piece of paper and without any "sawing" just slice it down the page like butter. Never had a knife that sharp out of the box before!Now about the blade itself. I personally like the 1/2 serrated and 1/2 fine edge combination. Like I said, when dealing with rope or leather, I need something with teeth. I also am very very appreciative of how sturdy the blade is when extended out. This is something rare in many switchblade/folding knives. With many you will get a lot of wiggle with the blade when it is extended. Not with this knife. I can't even wiggle it a millimeter. I like that! I also like that the blade opens not from a switch on the side, but a tiny lever on the top of the handle (for lack of a better term). You can see it in the pics. I hold the handle in my palm and use my index finger to pull on that little stub on top and the knife opens EXTREMLY fast with a thwack! The blade is then locked. You don't have to worry about closing it on your fingers by accident. There is a tensioned metal slide in the handle that locks the blade into place. When you want to close the knife you can easily push against it with your thumb and then close the blade with your index finger. Very simple.All in all, This knife is excellent price and quality wise. It is extremely light weight and does not feel overbalanced! So happy with this knife! I can tell this will last me many years (with a bit of oil and sharpening from time to time). Size of the knife is very ideal for small/concealment and ranchwork. Anything bigger and you're gonna get funny looks from people. That's my 2nd Smith and Wesson Knife. I am pleasantly surprised and look at this brand with much more respect now.If you want a quality knife sharpener as well that is inexpensive, made in the US and has great quality for your lovely knife, please check this product out too! I have used it with amazing results for my other knives (utility, kitchen, survival knives)!!!AccuSharp Knife Sharpener With Replacement Blades**5/11/2012**I am still using this blade for everything! Yes, I still need to sharpen it from time to time, but the blade is holding up really well and I have come to rely on it for everything in my day to day activities.

I specifically wanted a simple, compact camera for snapshots and to take pictures of merchandise for online auctions.  I'm pleased with having chosen the Z20fd.  There is neither a telescoping lens nor an automated lens cover to malfunction.  The manually-sliding front panel (shown in the open position in the product picture) functions as a lens and flash cover and is the camera's power switch.  There is a large viewing screen and operating sounds can be silenced for unobtrusive use.  There are plenty of operating options including an auction setting where multiple images can be included in a single frame.  A memory card must be purchased separately (but the camera can be used out-of-the-box with approximately 45MB built-in memory).  A separate wall charger for the lithium-ion battery is included; I bought an extra cell to keep handy for a quick switch when camera power runs low; without a spare, the camera is out of action while the battery is removed for charging.  All things considered, I'm very happy with this little camera!

I have a terrible back and have tried every therapy known to humankind (I exaggerate).I have one of those inversion tables but when I use it I feel like I'm going to have a stroke with all the blood rushing to my head. You put this device on, stick it in a door, lay back and crank down on the ratcheting device. You can feel your spine extend. Tow bad things happened to me while using this that were my fault. First, my back was "out" and I was in great pain. I was using this device and it was really helping to relieve my pain. Then I sneezed. I thought I was going to pass out from the pain. So, if your back hurts when using this device, don't sneeze. Second, I neglected to tell my wife I was on the other side of the door using the stretcher. She opened the door and the device let go and that really hurt, too. So be sure to use a door you have control over. I use this 2-3 times a week and it's something that really works for me. It's much better than hanging upside down. It's a brilliant device and very easy to use. Thanks folks!

Update February 2011: I originally gave this four stars but have since come to be completely addicted to this lotion. This occurred after I gave my mother some of it. This repeater / extender was easily setup and the online guide was very easy to follow.  Instructions were simple and it seemed to work fine out of the box.  I placed the SR20000G in the opposite corner of my house from my wireless Medialink router (within 70% signal as instructed).I downloaded an APP for my iPhone and tested speeds for the unit to compare to the wireless Medialink router (using comcast service).  My findings...Comcast service advertised speeds (upload/download): 4Mbps / 20Mbps.Medialink router (Measured by iPhone App): 4Mbps / 18Mbps.Amped SR20000G (Measured by iPhone App): 2Mbps / 10Mbps.I tested many times and never got a higher download speed on the Amped SR20000G.Then I tested outside my home and the signal for the SR20000G was quite poor / worse than my Medialink router.  I read on other reviews that the firmware may need updated (most recent firmware is July 2012).  I checked and the unit already had the updated firmware.So unfortunately this unit really didn't help extend the wireless service in my home and the speeds are quite inferior to my current wireless router...  I would not buy again.UPDATE: 11/19/2012.  I'VE CONTINUED TO USE THE SR20000G WITH HOPES THAT I WOULD SEE BETTER SPEEDS...  I've moved it to different locations... I've tested speeds with different devices... BUT I'M AFRAID TO SAY THAT I'M LEFT WITH THE SAME FINDINGS!  My WIRELESS ROUTER is still giving me speeds up to 5Mbps UP / 20Mbps DOWN and the AMPED SR20000G is still doing terrible.  Max has been 2Mbps UP / 10Mbps DOWN, but usually is more like 1.5Mbps UP / 5Mbps DOWN.  Unacceptable.. unfortunately I'm returning it...  Oh well, it was worth a shot!IF YOU HAVE ANY QUESTIONS FOR ME, FEEL FREE TO LEAVE A REPLY TO THIS REVIEW AND I'LL GLADLY ANSWER!  HOPEFULLY MY EXPERIENCE HAS BEEN HELPFUL FOR YOU!

I've been looking into rugged diving watches for a few years.  I've got a Casio solar watch which I like for knockin' around, but it's not all that handsome.  I recently looked at the Luminox watches and after reading reviews and seeing that pricing was around $300 for decent looking models, I decided to look elsewhere.  G-shock watches have the same look and function for much less.  In fact, I would think the Casio is better made (based on reviews), and tougher.  When you get this watch you will be surpried at how nice it looks.  A great knockin' around watch for outdoorsman/casual wear.  This watch will serve you well in rugged applications whether you're a diver, hunter, military or whatever you do which tears up your gear.  It's also very comfortable and lightweight, which I really like.  It's a big watch, akin to a small Tonka tractor tire on your wrist (and looks like it too), but it suites the look, feel and use well.  Your long sleeve cuffs won't make it over this watch, but you can manage to do it if you actually pull them over.  Some may not like the thick case about 1/2 inch.  I like it, and know that the thickness is to protect the watch (also has a scratch resistance crystal).The only negatives I have on this watch are the light/illumination... while it does work and serve it's purpose, for such a fine watch, it falls way short.  I suppose by design, they couldn't use the indiglo-type light; this light is like a small yellow-orange flashlight at the bottom of the face.  It sucks, but you can see the hands, and digital display via the light. Without the light, and using the watch in the dark, the markers are not illuminated? (neither are the hands?)  I still give this watch 5 stars for it's look, feel, and over-built abilities.  200 meters of water resistance is too much for most anyone, and the case seems like this watch could stand being dropped from skyscraper and still work just fine.  I will updated my use of this watch after more wear.  The band, like most plastic watches might wear out after some years, so we'll see.  I hope not.  This watch is also a dark-grey color, not black as the appearance suggests.  It's a handsome grey, and I really like it and I've already gotten compliments.  This is a very attractive, rugged mans-man watch.  I highly recommend.  There are Utube vids on similar watches..

This device has an identical pin-out as FDDI's TTL-232R-3V3 USB cable...[...]The 6-pin single-inline 0.1" pitch connector is constructed as follows:pin 1: black GNDpin 2: brown CTS (input to USB)pin 3: red VCC (+3.3V power from USB, 75 mA max.)pin 4: orange TX (output from USB)pin 5: yellow RX (input to USB)pin 6: green RTS  (output from USB)What I was shipped does not have a clear over-mold as shown on the Amazon Website.  The plastic molding on my USB connector is quite dark---almost black.  Nevertheless the green TX LED and the red RX LED are quite visible.I bought three of these to use under MS Windows 7 and Linux.  I'm going to buy more.  For example. this device is useful for monitoring the console serial port on a Beglebone Black and/or Raspberry Pi.  The latter requires an "adapter" cable.The price of this device is better than the equivalent cable from FDDI and the function/quality is identical.  In fact, because the cable diameter is not as thick, I like it better than FTDI's.Bruce D. LightnerLa Jolla,CA

I really like the Solo Stove Titan. It is well constructed and appears to be very durable, it is basically a big Solo Stove. Frankly I can't say enough good about the stove. It is easy to light, easier to feed than the original Solo Stove, burns very clean (for a biomass stove) with very little smoke. Since getting the Titan and Solo Pot 1800 combo my wife and I have managed to use it to cook 3 meals in the back yard and on  a 3 day snowshoe backpacking outing. The stove worked great. I really like the larger fire box of the Titan with a little practice I was able to regulate the flame to the extent that cook with high heat, or simmer with the use of a Scorch Buster. Since the stove is so efficient, rigging a simple pot hanger it is even easier to regulate the heat by just adjusting the height of the pot in relation to the stove. Thus far we have used the Titan with the Solo Pot 1800, the original Solo pot, old fashion coffee peculator, 9" cast aluminum deep Dutch Oven, GSI hard anodized aluminum Wok, Mors Bush Pot, Large Banks FryBake pan, SS Mucket Bake Packer and a Scorch Buster with good results. Some of the dishes we tried were bacon & eggs, pancakes, old fashion rolled oats, stews, chicken and dumplings, spaghetti and meatballs, porky pine balls, stir fries, chocolate cake with peanut butter frosting, and pizza. All from scratch, not freeze dried boil some water and soak. Over the weekend we used the Titan for all of the cooking and melting snow for potable for 3 adults.  While for solo summer backpacking if I were concerned with pack weight I would probably carry the original Solo stove, Mors Bush pot and Small FryBake pan. But with a buddy, in the canoe, kayak, car camping or winter camping I will most likely carry the Titan. I would definitely recommend the Solo Stove Titan to a friend or relative. The Solo Stove Titan is durable, versatile  efficient and a product I would rate at 5+ stars..The Solo Pot 1800Well I guess I should start by admitting that I am 65 years old, a grumpy old cuss and have been camping and cooking outdoors for as far back as I can remember. When I look for outdoor gear I want safety, durability, versatility, functionality and ergonomics. I wont be surprised if most folks disagree with my evaluation of the Solo Pot 1800, but I have to be honest if I am going to review something.In my opinion the Solo Pot 1800 pot container portion (the container minus the bail, handles, and cover) is an excellent product. The volume is sufficient for more than one person, well constructed, durable and functional. The pot has a pour spout, the volume scale is embossed in liters and ounces so it wont scrub off. The pot is stainless steel and heavy enough to do more that just boil water, though I wouldn't object to the bottom being a bit thicker. It is not coated with nonstick gunk so it can be scrubbed out even with sand if one has to and no eating flakes of nonstick coating. Unfortunately the handle type would be better on a sauce pan (height less than the diameter) rather than this style pot and it is in the wrong location. The handle would be much better if it were a butterfly or D shaped handle directly opposite (180 degrees) the pour spout. The cover is very loose, it keeps the bugs and ashes out but has to be removed or held when pouring from the pot. The bail significantly too short. The cover is difficult to remove or put back on when the bail is up and is a real pain to stir the pot with the bail up. The bail needs at least twice as much vertical clearance between the bail and cover. I also have some long term reservations over the "paint can" style bail attachments. Over time if the bail starts to loose some of its spring, it could easily become a serious safely issue.Over all the I like the Solo Stove Titan & Solo Pot 1800, especially since the stove nests in the pot. We will continue to use it with the Solo Stove Titan & Solo Pot 1800 unless I found a better replacement pot that would nest with the stove. I would recommend the stove and pot to a friend or relative but would point out what I consider to be weakness in the Solo Pot 1800.
