They have a lid, and keep things hot or cold, are very light and basically throw-a-ways.  They break easy, and I've often just disposed of them after use at a picnic or other outdoor event.  This is somewhat better, but it's far from magic keeping things hot or cold.  Folding it into shape leaves a few air gaps as well so, I doubt that it would work quite as well as said here, but I didn't have the event to try it out.  Where it does excel is the fold up and store away feature so you can keep it handy somewhere.Like most foam containers this is light, and should be a good insulator.  I can't fathom the price however, I can't see this standing up to heavy use and abuse. If you were a frequent picnic goer, or transport a lot of food for a catering business, I doubt it would hold up.  It is not likely to replace an igloo or coleman cooler in the long run.  At the right price, (perhaps about half) it would be something I think people would want for the convienience, sort of a middle ground thing for boats or RV's.  I cannot recommend this at the price they want, but if your need fits, it's at least something to take a look at, there might be a sale!

The performance of these hearing protector muffs is amazing.  The amplification of ordinary sound is so clear, crisp, and perfect, that it is literally possible to forget that you have these muffs on!  These muffs make ordinary conversation possible while wearing them, and yet they cut off the amplification instantly when a gunshot pushes the decibel level past the cut-off point.  Using these Peltors really enhances the experience of shooting with friends, as it is quite feasible to engage in conversation while keeping one's hearing protected.  This is hard to believe, but when you engage the amplifier via the on-board button, it is as though the ear muffs magically just vanished!  The sound does not sound electronic--it sounds like ordinary hearing.The build quality of the Peltor MT15h7F model is very high.  The components are all very high quality and rugged.  The fit around the ears is on a par with expensive stereo headphones, and very comfortable.  The activation buttons for the sound amplification, and the volume control, are all big rugged buttons that are built to take use and abuse.These hearing protectors are not cheap, but they are worth the money.  To protect your hearing and enhance the shooting experience, you cannot do better.  Highly recommended.  RJB.

The Energy RC-Micro 5.1 surround sound speakers pack awesome sound in small and rather stylish design.  I am not an audiophile, but I used to have large Infinity speakers that sat on the floor, and these Energy speakers have sound quality far superior to them.The powered bass rocks the house and can deliver plenty enough bass to fill my 13 x 20 foot room and more.  The surround speakers have a uniquely crisp sound so that your home feels as though it was part of the original video and music making experience.Although these speakers can belt out over 100 watts per channel without a problem, I wouldn't recommend these speakers for the person who wants to listen to their music at deafening levels.  The bass can deliver but the unique qualities of the surround speakers are somewhat lost at maximum power . . .  or my ears can't adequately process all of the noise.I find these speakers excellent for surround  sound TV and movie watching, excellent for music at normal listening levels (where the neighbors in the next house are not complaining), and good for the times when you want to make neighbors listen to your music.

I have owned this set for a couple  of months now and I am quite happy with it. It has optical out so all my equipment is hooked up via HDMI and then an optical cable carries the audio signal to my speakers. The Cinemotion technology (filling in additional frames to make the motion look smoother and fluid) makes the HD videos even more beautiful. And it is 3D capable at a good price. The delivery was also timely.Now couple of things that I want to warn the potential buyers about: The TV has an odd shape in the rear. I am usingOmniMount Power40 Articulating Motorized Wall Mount for 23" to 46" Displays (Black)to mount my TV to the wall and I did manage to mount it... only after two weeks of trying to figure out which parts of the mount fit this TV best. There is a bulge towards the bottom rear part of the TV that makes it harder for the Omnimount to be mounted properly - I had to buy custom size screws for the mount. Still, I would say that most mounts will fit as it follows the VESA specifications.Now this is a real Con: After I installed new HDMI cables, there was no signal on the screen. I thought the cables were bad, so I tested them on another TV and they worked. I knew the equipment wasn't broken either so it must be the TV. After a little research it turns out that this TV (and some other Bravia models) tend to turn off their HDMI connection once its disconnected. You would have to turn the TV completely off (powered off/ unplugged), and then turn it back on and everything should be working. I am using three of the four HDMI ports and my idle port does not work at the time. I am pretty sure if I turn off the TV for 5 mins and then turn it back on, the port will start working again but I have no need for it at the time. Yet, this is a serious defect in the product. I am not the only one who experienced this issue - quite a few people online had the same problem. Other than that, everything else is Solid.

I received the adapter and installed it on my 2007 Toyota Corolla three weeks ago, with help of some tutorials I found on Youtube, and the following tools:pry tool (metal, though plastic is better),standard phillips screwdriver (to remove middle panel),ratchet, ratchet extension, 10 mm socket (to remove radio),magnetic pickup tool (collects loose screws),and some packing tape (to secure wire slack).Once the radio was out, it was as simple as plugging it into the backside of my car radio and pressing "DISC."For wiring, I chose not to drill a hole into the middle box below the fan controls as many others have done; instead, I placed the main adapter / transceiver snugly between the plastic shell and carpet near the bottom right fan, and taped any excess wiring to the underside of the plastic shell as well. The 30-pin wire would then come around and up from there to connect to my phone, which is mounted horizontally on the dashboard via aniOttie HLCRIO104 Easy Flex 2 Windshield Dashboard Car/Desk Mount Holder for iPhone 4S/5/5S/5C.With the 30-pin connector connected to an iPhone, volume adjustments occur directly via the car radio knob. FF, RW, track skipping can be done either via "seek track" button on car stereo or from the iPhone itself. Also, charging occurs only at ON/ACC -- stops when the car is OFF.Sound quality is excellent (compared to the SwissGear FM radio transmitter I was using previously) regardless of whether 30-pin or aux connections are used, but I always use the 30-pin to get the benefit of charging and car controls.Overall, this was a definitely a good investment of money and labor; I recommend this as the most cost-efficient way to add aux functionality to any 4-10 year old Toyota.

Bought this when I was in college, after three years of hot TX climate and neglect (I left it baking in the car all those years)...it still works. The battery just croaked today so that made me remember to give it a review.Initial plot speed:12.5 mile trip takes 6 seconds to plot. 214 mile trip takes about 20 seconds.Replot Speed:Fast, 3-5 seconds replotting was good enough for me whenever I took the wrong turn.Signal Strength:Normally 6 satellites and 4 out of 5 barsVolume:Great, clear and audible, easily adjusted in two touches. Default voice not a man, just how I like it.Battery life:When it was new it lasted about 3 hours. Leave it in the car too long and that fully charged capacity loses about half its life.Bluetooth:Never had to use it, so cannot give insight here. Car dealerships now setup bluetooth with your phone when you buy any recent vehicle models.Map Updates:Free, corrections made by other users are trust-based. I hope other GO 920 users know how to use map correction and are motivated to do so, because I sure am not :)Dependability:98% of the time it gets me to where I want to go, 2% of the time the address is so new that it is not in the maps loaded in the device.Likes:Toll Road options - always use toll, never use, and ask me. New arrival time (voice) - tells me ETA in hours and minutes, this ETA is recalculated OTF every 15 min or so. Handy because then I know to call in if I am expected to be late and can then give an ETA.Dislikes:Windshield grip strength of suction cup is poor. Depending on temperature changes via AC or weather, the suction cup will come loose and cause the device to come tumbling down. This happens more often during the rainy season because outside/inside temperatures change along with spontaneous showers. While the GO 920 waterfalls down from the dash, my Valentine One remains on the windshield.Map correction. I would have to mark a location and come back later to make corrections. However, the UI for correcting the map is not intuitive, so I do not utilize it. I am sure it is in the manual somewhere...though who wants to read that these days. Speaking of getting with the times, GO 920 does not have life time maps or traffic like some other newer models from TomTom's competitors. Hard to justify the price-to-date (349) compared to other units on the market now.Ordered a Garmin 1450LMT from Amazon for my new car and will compare it to the GO 920 in 1-3 years or whenever the 1450LMT battery dies, whichever comes first.

I really enjoy owning and using this mat. Typically made mil spec gear. This is a US ARMY issue rucksack backpack with all the quality mil spec snaps, buckles, straps, stitching and webbing which are are high-end. This bag is cavernous. There are more pockets and molle straps than I care to count. YouTube has several video reviews which detail the different uses for this bag. Make no mistake, if you want a big bag, this is it.The plastic frame is very light, well-formed, designed to carry the weight of the fabric and fits as it should, allowing the brunt of the weight to be distributed on the hips where it belongs. The padded straps on both shoulder and waist are thick and wide, very comfortable to handle.I purchased this bag for our Rim-to-Rim hike across the Grand Canyon next year. It will provide a stable platform for our gear, sleeping bag, tent, cooking gear, stove, fuel, food, tools, ammo, etc. I've done plenty of research both online and at the brick and mortar stores in search of the 'perfect' bag. The sleeping bag area can be closed-off from the main compartment keeping the sleeping gear clean and accessible. Normally, the sleeping bag is lashed to the bottom of the bag exposing it to the elements. There is also a wide rain shield which covers the top opening.A recent movie with Willem Defoe hunting the last Tasmanian Devil cat in New Zealand, (If I recall correctly) equipped him with a bag which was just the ticket I was looking for. I searched online for the brand, but after identifying it was unable to locate one. That lead to the military bags. A quick search on Amazon brought me to this one. Some checking on its merits and I was convinced.If you're looking for a LARGE bag which will hold what you need for when you need most of your gear (this is NOT a day pack) then this solid, well-built, well-desingned rucksack may be the answer. The seller was honest with the description of the bag having some wear, some dirt, and even one of our troop's name written on the side!If I lost this one - I wouldn't hesitate to get another!

After installing the 4 sticks of RAM my computer would not boot up. It did the typical series of beeps which indicate bad memory.I ran Memtest+ on each stick one at a time.  Of the 4 sticks, one failed the test after the first pass.  Contacted the seller "Xtreme Ram" and they responded promptly to go ahead and return the single stick for a replacement.  I did so (on my dime) about 2 weeks ago and have not heard back.The seller "as well as myself" are located in CA so I don't imagine the replacement should take too long.  After all, the original order only took two days.I am currently running the 3 good sticks of RAM in my PC (6GB) and everything is fine.I will update this review when/if I receive my replacement RAM.  If I don't hear back by this Friday, I will file a claim with Amazon.*UPDATE*Received the new stick of RAM. Threw it in with the other 3 sticks (totaling 8GB). Booted up fine but then Firefox, VLC, and IE started crashing after minutes. Then I got the memory dump blue screen.  Forget it, I'm just going to return the whole order.Put my  old memory back in (4 sticks at 1GB each) and the computer runs flawlessly again. The old memory is a slightly slower speed and I checked the MOBO compatibility and this new memory should have worked.  Oh well...Will report back if the refund/return goes smoothly.*Update 4/17/2013Seller refunded all money with zero problems. Never did figure out why 3 sticks of memory worked but not 4. I ran out of patience on an already decent performing machine.

This product is best to use after you've had a skin peel or microdermabration. You have to wait until all the skin on your face has peeled and then you can use it. It's incredible how much junk it sucked from my pores after a skin peel.If you don't have the money or time to have a skin peel or microdermabration you can scrub you face in the shower and then use it while your face is still wet. It won't unclog as many pores but it's still fairly good. Then I just use a metal pore extractor for any clogged pores it left behind since I don't want to use the metal pore extractor for all of my face.There are three rules I always follow when using this product: 1) always use after a shower 2) always keep my face wet 3) always after I've expoliated my faceBy expoliating, there's less blocking the suction.If I don't do these three things this product is pretty much useless.  Oh, and I don't advice using the big suction cup. The small one is better.

This was an upgrade from an old Vista machine so you can imagine how much faster it is. I spent a lot of time researching exactly what I wanted and it came down to this and the 1350 model. At the time this was on sale and over $300 less so it was an easy choice. My main worry was Windows 8 but I actually like it quite a bit. Plus it still supports my old copy of Office Pro 2003 so that was a plus. It didn't take long to get used to the the "tiles" and "apps" and I no longer look for the Start button.The computer itself is very fast. To start with, the boot time is about 20 seconds. Not sure if that is just how Windows 8 is or the Solid State drive that is used for the cache. I've done quite a bit with photo editing (Adobe Elements 11) and loading and rendering time is very impressive. There are some features I might not use but for what's inside the box, I would highly recommend the HP h9-1330!Update: So after a few weeks I'm finding more and more to like about this computer. It's VERY quiet and stays very cool. I've also had no issues installing any software or hardware even though Windows 8 isn't "supposed" to be compatible. There is also I feature that HP has called "Connected Remote" which turns my Android devices into remote controls for Windows Media Player. I didn't think I would use this but now use it quite a bit. Lastly (for now) I upgraded my monitor to take advantage of the updated graphics card and decided to use the dual monitor set up (although you can hook up to three). This has also come in much more handy then I ever thought. Just keeps getting better!5 Months later...Still love this machine. ZERO problems. No noise, no heat, no issues, just runs like a champ. Plus I see it cost $150 more then when I bought it so I'm even happier. Have installed a lot of old software and devices (and even more new) and all work perfectly. The only "issue" I had was it came out of the box with some weird update settings that would just restart the computer with no notice but that was any easy fix. The specs speak for themselves but as a user I can tell you, you won't be disappointed. Almost forgot for those that want to know. Scores 7.1-8.2 on the " Windows Experience Index".
It's really easy to use, small and compact, held everything that I needed to free up space on my computer. It's relieving to finally have all of my important stuff secure.

Lots of connection options on the back and side.5. Has a built in DVD player (not the best, but good enough for kids movies).6. Very light weight.CONS:1. Slow. The tv is very slow to responde to some types of changes. For instance, turning on the tv takes quite a while. Also switching inputs takes a long time too.2. This tv uses a non-standard remote. I am white as can be and I burn as soon as I walk out into the sun!  I just do not tan.  At all.  I go from white to red, to a faded version of a tan and back to white. I read the directions, but they didn't "sink" completely into my head because I was so anxious to try it--I ended up spraying the product directly onto my skin and THEN using the mit to rub it in!!!!  It actually didn't turn out too bad, as I only did small sections at a time.  I do now use it by spraying the solution directly onto the mit THEN rubbing it in to my skin and it works great.This product is awesome.  It has saved me so much money (from getting air brush tans) and I can apply it anytime in the privacy of my own home.  It doesn't streak and I love that it has the applicator mit. First off I wanted to give this product a fair shake.  I ordered the product thru Amazon about the same time I ordered my Nexus 7 from Google.  The Supcase came in due course and I have to say it is a good looking cover similar to a cover I got for my iPad but smaller, of course and costing much less.  I was happy to have a cover, although I am not rough with my electronic toys.  I easily slipped my Nexus 7 into the case and it fit wonderfully.  After using it for about 15 minutes I noticed that the Nexus was turning off almost instantly after turning it on and finally the Nexus would not stay on at all.  Finally after trying various scenarios, I took the Nexus 7 out of the case and problem solved!  Well, I thought this Supcase was not working at all!  I then took an Exacto knife and cut the magnets out and put the Nexus 7 back in the case and voila! success!  I still get the advantage of the Nexus coming on automatically and it doesn't go off after 20 seconds and works great!I would have to say do not buy the Supcase.  It may work for some but I found that the product does not work properly, at least in my case.

I have long, thick hair, and I use this for big, looser waves. Anything larger than this, and my hair won't hold the curl. After reading reviews of this product for the last few years, as desirable as it seemed, I was discouraged about buying it. There were complaints about almost everything.  A week ago I decided to take the plunge, after trying it on and checking whether there was a differences between the older and the newer reviews.I am delighted and enchanted. I have been using consumer oriented GPS products for nearly 10 years, and this by far is the best. I was staggered at the speed  with which it receives satellite signals. In midtown Manhattan it took fewer than 20 seconds, and in suburban neighborhood where I live, fewer than 10.  I'm used to waiting close to two minutes on my current best-selling  product. Secondly, as compared to my Polar or Garmin,  the user interface is extraordinarily simple. To start the GPS one holds down a red button on the side of the watch for three or four seconds and shortly thereafter, the GPS signal is received. Navigation beyond that point is simple and intuitive. There is not as wide a variety of options as some of the running watches have, but at twice the cost. But for someone who is an exercise walker Or runner and not a competitive racer this is a fine piece of equipment.I was a bit concerned about the size, but now that I am wearing the watch it is fine. The large digits indicating time, may be exaggerated, but are not unattractive. The information available when running can be modified easily and is readable without any eyestrain at all.  is easy to illuminate the  face  of the instrument.I may have no complaints now and it is conceivable that in the course of time all the horror stories that are on the web may be realized. However, I have no reason to believe that this will happen.  I wear the Nike Fuel on my other wrist and feel indebted to Nike for the cleverness, attractiveness and functionality of its products.

"L'Oreal Paris Age Perfect BB Cream Instant Radiance, Light/Medium" looked like it would be perfect for ivory or very fair skin tones.  The soft, pale petal pink color disappeared nicely when applied as a test to my inner wrist.  Then, I applied this BB Cream to my face!  Almost immediately the color changed to a sickly, yellow-orange that was similar to the color produced by self-tanning products in the early 1970's."L'Oreal Paris Age Perfect BB Cream Instant Radiance, Light/Medium" was a major disappointment.  Although it was not too heavy and applied smoothly, the resulting color was atrocious.  The jaundiced look is less than desirable.  The fragrance of "L'Oreal Paris Age Perfect BB Cream Instant Radiance, Light/Medium" is quite strong.  That might have been tolerable, if the product had not resulted in a sickly appearance.  Further, based on the packaging, it does not appear this product is hypo-allergic.I cannot recommend "L'Oreal Paris Age Perfect BB Cream Instant Radiance, Light/Medium".  The radical color change when applied to skin is unacceptable.

This knife is a beast! All you have to do is hold it in your hand and you know this is not something you have to worry about breaking. With a minimum amount of care this hunk of steel should out live me.I've owned my knife for about a year and find myself pricing it on Amazon while considering it as a gift for a family member.  I have put this blade through the paces time and time again.  I've pried with it, I've dug with it, I've cut sod with it, I've cut cord with it, I've tapped nails back in with it, I've batoned logs, I've split kindling, I've done rough carving with it, I've even made box forts in the living room with it - this knife does it all!  Obviously there are tasks other blades would be more suited for but the best knife for the job is often the one you have handy and this a good all purpose knife.I continue to be very happy with the kydex sheath.  Curiosity about the kydex sheath was one of the reasons I purchased the knife - I've always been a leather kinda guy when it comes to sheaths.  The sheath has far exceeded my expectations.  Many miles later it still snaps firmly into place and holds strong.  I prefer the top of a knife to hang even with my belt or below and the screws give you the option to raise or lower the knife in relation to the belt loop which is a nice little bonus.  Another bonus is the snap and velcro in the belt loop allowing you to remove the sheath from your belt w/o having to unfasten your belt - not a feature I would have shopped for but more handy that I would have thought.Over time the black coating on the blade has worn through in places but after the hard use that I have put on the blade it would be unreasonable to expect it to stay intact.  The blade holds an edge fairly well through hard use but leaves a little to be desired in this area.  However to be fair I would not be treating the edge this way if I expected it to be a fine detail blade.In summary this is a great knife for the money and I would buy it again in a second if I lost mine.

Specs: ASUS N71JQ-A1 (Intel Core i7 Q 720  @1.60GHz, 8GB DDR3-1333 RAM, Video ATI 5730 1GB; Three eSATA HDDs running @7200rpm, Seagate 750GB Momentus XT HDD1, Seagate Momentus 750GB HDD2, Seagate Momentus 750GB HDD3; Bluray Combo DVD; Intel Centrino Advanced-N 6200 network card; Windows 7 x64 Ultimate Service Pack 1 and up-to-date. No hardware or software issues.This laptop is a screamer but it has its limitations. It only has one USB 3.0 port and the eSATA port is not powered which only makes functional for devices that provide for their own power. Conveniently, there is a USB 2.0 port purposely located right next to the eSATA port so this Micro SATA cable adapter worked perfectly. I really needed another high speed port for my other Seagate GoFlex external HDD wich can use USB 3.0/Firewire/eSATA protocols via adapters one can buy separately but it "complaints" when connected to a USB 2.0. This Micro eSATA cable adapter terminates with a female powered eSATA connector where the GoFlex powered eSATA STAE103 cable connects to. I also use it to connect 2.5" HDD using the "Shared eSATA Cable for 2.5" SATA Laptop Hard Drive". I've always kept 2.5" HDDs when I upgrade to bigger and faster ones and use them to backup stuff in zip files. Amazon sells these inexpensive plastic boxes "IO Crest 2.5-Inch IDE/SATA HDD Storage Box SY-ACC25014" that can store two drives each. You will be amazed how much data one can store in these little drives.
They get the job done and they work fantastic I have no issues with using these. I will definitely buy more in the future.

This power strip is a very nice, and very basic device that offers very good value.  I am impressed that it uses an intelligently designed plug as shown in the accompanying photo from Emily Goodin.  The outlet layout allows for 2 power blocks to be plugged in without obstructing other outlets.The overall appearance is functional, but obviously not expensive.  Does it actually provide protection against power surges?  I don't know, and hope never to find out.The one design element I do not like is that the tiny red light to indicate it is powered on is not visible unless one is looking down from almost directly overhead.  This increases the risk that it will needlessly be left on, resulting in a phantom load.  I much prefer a power strip that has the indicator light incorporated in the switch, or at least has an indicator light that protrudes so it may be seen from across the room.

This looks great, and has a very heavy-duty feel to it.Each outlet bank can be set to switch on and off (or not) based on the front switch or a remote 12 volt trigger.  It comes with a power cable which, when plugged into a switched outlet, will switch the other outlets on and off.  So, if you plug this into the switched outlet on your receiver, for example, you can make the outlets of your choice turn off and on with the receiver.  They can also be set to turn on after a delay, to allow the amp (for example) to power up before turning on the source device.  This is a pretty powerful capability.It has a couple of outlets that are for high current devices, like power amps, which is nice to see.  I have a high-end receiver plugged into one of them (160 Watts x 7 channels) with no issues.It isn't perfect, however.  The front panel looks like brushed aluminum, but is really just painted plastic, so know that going in.  Also, after about a year some of the LCD segments on mine no longer light, probably due to a bad solder joint on the display.  This could just be a problem with my unit, but it does make me a bit concerned about their quality control and makes me a bit hesitant to buy a second one.The display is hard to read from below, too, so you'll want to put this on a lower shelf.Overall, a good unit for the price, and looks great too.

Resistive touchscreens are difficult after using capacitive screen for a long time. Rtouchscreens are touch-sensitive computer displays composed of two flexible sheets coated with a resistive material and separated by an air gap or microdots. Because the touchscreen responds to pressure on its surface, contact can be made with a finger or any other pointing device. When using  bare finger operation, the resistive screen's poorer responsiveness to light touches has caused it to lose market share to capacitive screens these days.  It is definitely hard to use after I used for few hours. 512 MB RAM is a deal breaker. No Google Play is a deal breaker.But the price is unbeatable. As a throwaway tablet just to browse web this may be good. But you can get quality ASUS or Samsung Tab 2 for few more bucks. Well, not few more double. I would suggest investing little more and buying a quality one rather than suck to the low performing capacitive one. Coby themselves have a resistive one, which I am going to try next week.

The Harmony 650 does a great job eliminating the need for multiple remote controls, but clunky software and inconsistent performance hinder an otherwise great product.Setting up the Harmony is a lot like installing Windows PC software 10 years ago. There are a ton of menus to click though to get everything set up. But it does offer access to virtually any device you can control with a remote control, so it's worth bearing through it. It also gives you the option of controlling sets of devices per activity, so you can set up an activity profile for watching TV, another for streaming media, and yet another for playing video games. Just select the activity you want, and it'll turn those devices on, in theory. It can be annoying when for whatever reason, it does't turn on all the devices. You then have to go through another menu on the remote control to troubleshoot the issue. It's a painstaking process that a lot of people won't have patience for.Once the Harmony is set up,  can map your volume control to your amp and your channel control to your cable box. And when it works, it works great. And while it has cleaned up the clutter, I'd love to see a remote control that's easier to set up that you wouldn't have to troubleshoot as often.

First of all, this is an awesome design and it opens beer and soda bottles quite easily while also holding my keys, mini light, and swiss army knife.  I have been using this carabiner for about 8 months and it has served well with only one issue: one of the rotating clips popped loose.  I must have dropped it and the capped end that is sotterred on the pivot point must have broken off.  Why is this a problem? Well, sometimes the clip will get pushed loose and open the gate and leave it open because it gets caught on the frame.  Obviously, this led to my keys falling off in a parking lot.  Lukily, I heard them hit the ground and investigated the issue.To solve this problem, I keep nothing on that particular gate and use it to just clip on my clothing when needed.  However, since I am someone who dropps his cell phone and keys frequently, it is only a matter of time before the other breaks.  Once this happens, I will no longer use the biner due to the fact that it will not be able to secure some of my most important small items.Another noteworthy issue is size.  It is on the larger side and is quite long and hard to fit into my pocket comfortably sometimes. Do I recommend this product?  Um, not really.  If you are gentle with it and watch it then you may be able to avoid this issue.  If you are someone like me who tends to need bulletproof items, I would get a regular climbing carabiner for your keys and attach a bottle opener to it.  My favorite is the Metolius FS Mini.  It is small, lightweight, and has a great wired clip.  It is definitely built to last and is rugged as it was designed for rock climbing.

Needed to dremel cut the weld on the original muzzle nut to install this break.  small pin on end of gun, top side of barrel that depresses into the gun.  Must depress this pin to fully screw any new muzzle break onto barrel.  pin returns to fully outright position when muzzle is fully screwed in.  Two pin cutouts on muzzle break allow for change in which side the break is on (left or right).  Fully screwed in, the muzzle break has a little bit of play, but does not seem to affect the performance or accuracy.  would recommend to anyone who needs a well built, reliable muzzle break that does not cover the muzzle flash (which I personally like). The Canon 70-300mm IS USM is a great telephoto lens for those who occasionally do telephoto work.The Canon is very nicely made and feels good in the hand, but it's no "L" series lens.  The barrel is not weather-sealed and is constructed of plastic, but the mount is metal.  Overall, construction quality is similar to the 50mm f/1.4 lens.  The zoom and focus rings both operate smoothly and have generous travel.  There are two modes for the image stabilizer (normal and panning), and a lock button to prevent lens creep when the lens is pointed down at wide angle.  When attached to my Canon 7D it feels balanced and easy to shoot with, but can become a bit burdensome if carried for a long day of shooting.The lens has an ultrasonic motor, and focuses fairly quickly and quietly.  It may hunt a little at the telephoto end, but rarely takes more than a second to find focus on my Canon 7D.  When focusing the end of the lens extends and rotates.  The lens does not support full-time manual focus.  The image stabilizer is quite effective and I was able to take shots at 70mm all the way down to 1/10", and towards the telephoto end down to 1/40".Photos from the 70-300 lens are similar in quality to those from the EF 28-135 f/3.5-5.6 IS USM lens, which is to say that they exhibit good sharpness, but don't have the biting, crisp look of Canon's L-Series lenses.  I don't use telephoto focal lens very often, so this lens works perfectly for me.  If you, however, do a lot of telephoto work then you may want to invest in something like the 70-200 f/4 L lens.  I shot with apertures ranging from wide open down to f/22 and found that the lens' sweet spot is around f/8.  The lens does not lose much sharpness in the corners, and stays consistent throughout its zoom range.  I did not notice any vignetting on my 7D and purple fringing was minimal.  Out of focus areas at wide apertures were smooth and not distracting.  Color is great, but contrast is a bit low.All in all... a good telephoto lens at a great price.

I'm very fair skinned and have noticed that as I've gotten older,  my freckles seem to morph together and try to look like age spots.  Which I think is attention seeking behavior.  Well,  they won because I paid attention and decided to find something to fade these freckle gangs and maybe send them back on the path to just looking cute and friendly.The stuff on the market is targeted for people with dark skin.  It was waaaaay too harsh and smells gross.  I've used Burt's Bees products before and liked them so I was OK trying this out.It's not a lotion,  it's pretty watery but goes on smooth and easy.  It does have a slight odor that dissipates almost immediately.  After three weeks of use, the targeted freckle-gang broke up and are mere shadows of their former selves.Like with any fade cream,  the spots will come back immediately when you are exposed to the sun for more than 10 minutes.  But,  use a good sunscreen and moisturizer and the Burt's fade potion and it's all good.  The best part is that my skin doesn't have that dull,  blech winter look to it.  It's bright and smooth,  so this is now a staple in my skin care squad.

I have a whole slew of wonderful, coloriscious old Minolta, tack sharp Kiron and stellar Canon glass from the 60's- 90's that I often thought about putting up on fleabay, and I am SO glad I was too lazy. My old 40 1.8 is back in action and as gorgeous now as it was back in the film days. my 58 1.2 is resurrected as well, and just as magical as I remembered it to be. The old glass on an a7R lends a pronounced 'feel' to the image, and I'm quickly getting to the point where I can tell which lens I used after the fact just by the character of the picture. Not all the old lenses have sufficient resolution to really push the sensor, but many have such exceptional color and charming anomalies that they may, finally, get worn out. To replace my whole bag of old glass with modern new iterations would be astronomically expensive. For a few dollars, you can get the charm of the old and implement it with the tech of today and make images that are TRULY distinctive and spectacular and maybe more importantly, FUN again. This adapter fits VERY well, mounts easily and snugs down solidly with no wobble or gaps. I'll eventually just buy more and leave them on the lenses to cut swap time, and be very happy to spend a few dollars to reincarnate all my old friends.

I bought this keyboard because I tried it first in school, and I liked they way the keys don't need to be pushed down very far, much like a laptop keyboard, which is what I'm used to. It's easy to fix, clean and is pretty sturdy (I've dropped it once or twice). It looks decent, seems to resist scratches, and is light. the USB ports in either side of the bottom are pretty useful, and the keyboard itself comes with an extension cable, which is nice.Now the bad parts. First of all, Apple does not support this keyboard AT ALL for use on PCs. Most of the keys are the same, but there are some differences that you'll have to get used to. The ALT and Windows (the apple key for you ignorant mac users) keys are switched, the "Clear" button is Num Lock, there is no Print Screen button or equivalent, but there are F13-F19. As far as I can tell, they can't be reprogrammed for anything with any applications that I can find. Apple doesn't make any drivers to get the keys to work, which is just bad marketing, but then again, the only good thing Apple does do is advertising, however untruthful, manipulative and misleading. The USB extension cord that comes with the keyboard has a little nib that prevents you from using it on any other device, which is kind of a dick move on Apple's part.I gave the keyboard five stars because I've gotten over the shortcomings, taped over the stupid "Return", "Apple" and "Eject"(Escape) buttons with electrical tape, and the whole thing looks pretty cool now. The Caps Lock key now has Caps Lock filed off and Billy Mays Mode professionally printed on it, so the overall makeover kind of makes up for how flamboyant and unprofessional it looks.

I've tried several iPad keyboards and I'm not sure I like any of them (including the "just ok"Adesso Compagno3 Bluetooth Scissor-Switch Keyboard with Carrying Case), but the "iHome Type Slim Bluetooth Keyboard" is probably the most underwhelming of the bunch.  To use the keyboard and have your iPad properly fit in the plastic holders, you are restricted to using a landscape view of your iPad so it doesn't really support you swiveling into portrait mode.  Considering some pages require portrait, this is obviously not ideal.  Also I found that the Bluetooth connection from the iPad to the keyboard did not work properly.  I had to spend ALOT of time getting it to connect in which is less than ideal since all other Bluetooth items have connected into my iPad very easily. Also, to prop up the iPad to view while you type, you have to unclip the case along a side of the iPad, then later clip it back in when you are done.  I got it to pop out alright when I wanted to type, but when I was finished the clips would not always snap in tightly.  Very annoying!So although it's compact, lighter than many of the competing brands, it's handy to have the keyboard with the iPad (if you can get it to work), and the design looks sleek and professional, the construction is way too flimsy for me.  Because of the over-reliance on cheap plastic for the clips, cheaper plastic covering the sides of the keyboard, and thin cardboard under the keypad, I wouldn't count on it lasting too long.Overall, I was not impressed and the price was pretty hefty in my opinion for what you get.

I jumped on the bandwagon and bought the Tangle Teezer after seeing other natural haired women rave about it. I have type 4a/b hair and thought I would give it a go.Starting out, it was a curly girl's dream. The Tangle Teezer did what it said. Since my hair is starting to gain some length, I don't look forward to detangling days. It can take almost 2 hours to detangle my hair. The Tangle Teezer cut this time down to 15 minutes. First, I wet my hair, added conditioner and waited for the slip. I took the tangle teezer and glided it through my hair, starting from the bottom up and working my way up to the root. My hair felt thoroughly detangled, almost as if I had a blowout. It also clumped my curls together. I was thrilled........that is, until six weeks later. I had been wearing my hair in protective styles for three months, using the TT in between. For three weeks, I began using the TT more frequently and began wearing my hair out in twist outs.One weekend, I decided to put my hair back up in a protective styles (small twists). Little did I know what type of damage I was doing to my hair. As I parted my hair, I began finding mini "locks" in my hair, where the hair was matted. I've never had matted hair before just using a wide-tooth comb and my fingers.I guess, that although the TT detangled portions of my hair, it also clumped small portions of it together, which caused the matting. This gave me the illusion of completely detangled hair. I couldn't detangle the matts, and ended up having to cut out them.Second, I noticed that when I put my hair back in twists...my ends were wrecked. Usually, my ends don't frizz at the ends, when in twists. When dry..it just lays flat. When I wet them, they have these nice little spiral curls at the ends. But, after using the TT my ends were frizzy, indicating damage. I was devastated, since I had worn protective styles and had grown nearly two inches in length. Sadly, I also found an increased number of SS knots. I ended up having to cut an inch off my hair to minimize the damage.The only thing I did differently in my hair routine was using the Tangle Teezer. So, bye bye TT! I prefer using my beloved wide-tooth shower comb again. Since I've stopped using the TT, my hair hasn't been experiencing the "mini locking" and my ends are slowly being nursed back to health.I'm not saying that the TT doesn't work. It does, just beware, because the risks might outweigh the benefits of using it.

I have owned for 20 years a similar Sennheiser HD and I have always put them on happily. I loved the way they sounded. This HD 380 Pro does the same thing. I love putting them on. They are great in the office too, the closed design makes sound stay inside.I am an electrical engineer by my schooling and have experimented my fare share with good quality Hi-Fi products. I feel however, that our ears are so imperfect, uneven and specific to each of us that any claim about how one pair in the $100+ price range is better than another simply becomes personal preference. So, when I mention fantastic sound stage, clearness, dept, color, warmness they are only my own experiences and have little to do with how other people experience it. I have recently received a gift of Bose AE2s which are even more expensive than the 380-Pro. I have compared them with as much as possible A/B testing but having difference impedance they produce different sound volumes which then make it impossible to differentiate. Louder will likely sound better in the $100+ price range.I also want to mention that the coiled cable makes it easier to work with it, I end up tangling up the cables of the AE2s. I am not sure if people know this, but the coiled cable has jacks on both end and can be replaced with a snap. The pads can also be replaced. The carrying case is also useful and I like how they headphones fold in and lay flat. The included larger jack nicely completes the package.

Let me start by saying this is my first Holographic sight. I have used a buddy's Aimpoint so I do have a somewhat high end sight to compare to. The Sightmark and the Aimpoint have the same 3 moa reticle. The Aimpoint dot was a little sharper than the Sightmark. That was the difference, a little sharper for 5 times the price of the Sightmark. Now on to the Sightmark. It has 4 reticles and seven brightness settings. Setting 4 or 5 is more than enough in the noon sun. The unit is very sturdy built and clamps solid to a picatinny rail. It comes with cleaning cloth, rubber dust cover and an allen wrench that fits both the rail mount screws and the windage an elevation screws. I zeroed at 25 yards with a laser bore sight and only took three clicks on the elevation screw to put it on the money at 50 yards. The sight adjusts with very positive clicks. This sight is mounted on a 9MM  carbine and although it is a minimal recoil weapon, the sight once zeroed has not budged in the 120 rounds I fired today. Even when a different reticle was used it held zero. The height of the sight  is perfect for the UTG back up flip sights I bought here at Amazon. I was able to co witness peep, post and dot perfectly. If your eyesight is not what it use to be, try the both eye opened bright dot shooting that will put you back on target. The choice is yours, buy the Sightmark or spend 5 times as much on the Aimpoint for a dot that is just a little sharper. Or you can spend that extra cash on ammo and  enjoy using a quality holographic sight that cost less than $50.

Alaskans fish a LOT. I've used ONE non-stainless steel version of this BERKLEY product since 1985, nicknamed The Alien after the sci-fi movie character. :-) I have used it extensively on my saltwater boat removing the hooks from deeply hooked halibut, lingcod, rockfish, Pacific cod, pollock, irish lords, sharks, skates(ugh) and 3 salmon species without getting a scratch on me and preventing a lot of injuries on fish I intended to release. These are NOT for the dry fly and nymph fly rodder as they are built large and very strong with a large jaw set for managing substantial sized hooks and putting a lot of gripping pressure on the hook for removal. Hooks smaller than #6 may be damaged due to the gripping power of this tool. Although I've used mine in and around saltwater for all these years a little preventative care of rinsing off the salt after use with a bit of freshwater from your drinking bottle has allowed this tool to work now for 27 years.In the freshwater category, I have used mine extensively ice fishing and open water fishing for Northern Pike and after regretting NOT bringing it along on a few fishing trips, I never leave home without it after that. Pike, as many anglers know, have tough jaws and sharp slicing teeth that can rival a shark for the damage they can do to hands and fingers. In my opinion this hook remover is ideal for those toothy critters in freshwater fisheries too. Not to mention using it on any fish you need to take the hook out of. Because of the gripping power on the hook, you can exert a LOT of force to get hooks out of bony or very tough tissue and cartilage. And this one has had plenty of workout removing hooks from large king salmon, sockeye and silver salmon all summer long.When this BERKLEY SS version turned up on Amazon for under $6 it was a no brainer to get this rust resistant tool for the saltwater boat and permanently transfer my "antique" hook remover to my freshwater fishing tackle box. In fact on my next Amazon order I'll add another one of these to my shopping list for a good friend who is a saltwater charter boat captain as a special "tip" on my next trip with him.P.S. I am reviewing the durability and performance of the BERKLEY brand product. There are other imitators you will see on the market, however I do not speak for those brands and their capabilities. BERKLEY has proven itself and for the price why gamble and take a chance with any other?

The Seagate Slim is a very good, and slim drive.  Unlike the Buffalo, the included Seagate software actually works on my iMac, which is refreshing, though not vital.  I have owned both internal and external Seagate drives for many years and all have performed well.  I'm new to Buffalo, thus cannot yet comment about long term durability of my Buffalo Ultra Slim.  However, I give the nod to Buffalo in terms of build quality.  The Buffalo has an aluminium body all the way around, both top and bottom, with a plastic "bumper" to protect the edges.  The Seagate Slim is only aluminium on the top 25% with the rest being black plastic.  The Buffalo, by my measurement, is also 3mm thinner than the Seagate.  Both come with a 2 year warranty.The Buffalo Ultra Slim just feels more sturdy and better built.  The Seagate Slim is a very good drive, and Seagate as a whole has been very good to me over the years.  You can't go wrong with either way.  But because of the better build quality, I'd give the slight nod to the Buffalo Ultra Slim at this price point.  Using 75% plastic for the body, Seagate has cut corners that Buffalo did not.  Here's to hoping Seagate does not just rest on its reputation and not put forth the effort to keep up with the likes of Buffalo.

Although I have a beloved PS3, I decided that if I was going to upgrade to a 1080 HD TV, I may as well get a proper Blu-Ray player as well.  I do normally only buy new, but I bought this refurbished unit on a whim, mainly because of the general glowing reviews it has received.  And I got a great product at a discounted price!A few notes:● A file format for self-made discs may be supported, but if they require an additional codec, they still won't play.  I discovered this with a set of old Anime discs that need an extra audio codec.  All well, just means I have to finally file convert them.● If you leave a disc inside the player, it has a somewhat nuisance of a "feature" that when I turn on the TV, the player after a moment detects it, turns itself on and forces the input to swap to its HDMI channel.  Kind of cool, but when I turn on my PS3 and then get pushed over to the Blu-Ray player because I had left a disc inside, it is a tad irritating. But in the grand scheme of things, really minor- plus there may be an option to turn this off that I haven't stumbled across yet.*UPDATE: Figured out why the player kept turning itself on and force swapping to its HDMI channel, even without a disc in.  Under the player's [SETTINGS]>[SYSTEM], there is an option for Anynet+(HDMI-CEC) that apparently automates certain functions, like what I described, when on.  Apparently, this includes the Panasonic HD TV I bought.  Turn the option off, no more irritation.  Heh.*● The online streaming feature requires a physical network cable to be plugged in.  Will not connect to Wireless Internet.  Not that it impacts me in the least, and I think it mentions this physical necessity in the product description, but a note that probably should be reiterated.● The remote has a subset control section for Samsung TVs.  I ended up buying a Panasonic HD TV... and admittedly wish a little I had gotten a Samsung now as this subset control would have basically allowed me to put away the separate TV remote.  Ah well.All in all, this Samsung F5100 came in a clean, ding-free, fully operational condition.  Mine even had batteries for the remote.  :)

This really is a great buy, I like the design of the sheath, and I like the look and feel of the knife. The blade does come pretty dull, but this is like to call an "edge ready" knife. After about 10 minutes of sharpening it can easily pass the paper test and holds its edge pretty well. Some of the discount knives that you see on here cannot be sharpened, this one can.The blade has a nice saw on the back that really works well. The sheath has a nice locking mechanism that is used both on the knife and on the belt loop. You can put it on without taking off your belt.The contents of the kit is smart. It comes with:Extra para cordA Slingshot (really cool)A small blade that can be attached to a long stick and used to spear a fish, it also has a bottle opener and can opener on it.A smaller blade that can be used as a scalpel or tied onto a stick as a arrowhead or spear tip.A fishing kit with string and floaters.Two band aidesA signaling mirrorTweezersMatches and striker stripSewing kit with needle threat and safety pin.On the case there is also a diagram that shows international hand signals.There is also a compass and a pencilIt is not a complete survival kit, but if I had to spend an unexpected night out in the woods I would definitely want to have this along. Whetstone usually makes crap, but this one is a winner. I am going to buy another one and stick it in a drawer for when my kid is old enough to own one.

I'd like to echo the comments made by others.  This is a FIVE STAR product.One thing I will comment on is the battery casing in this little gem.  It is unlike anything I've seen before.  There is a trick to it, but it's not all that hard to operate if you are somewhat mechanically inclined -- AND the light is good.  I can find my way around with a screwdriver and wrench fairly well, and even I could not load batteries into this thing in total darkness (I tested it).  In good light, closing the case can be done easily by lining up the arrows.  If you are not mechanically inclined, and don't know how to operate a paper clip, then this is not the lantern for you.If (when) you need to add batteries to this lantern in bad light during a power outage, then you will fail to do so if you don't plan for this in advance.  You must have some emergency lighting that comes on when the power shuts off.  There are all kinds of lights available that meet this requirement (I like the Energizer, and bought it from Amazon).  Use that light to find your way to your Go Dark Bag (everyone should have one), and load your batteries into this lantern using that light.  You should NEVER keep batteries in a product that is going to be stored for long-term storage since the batteries will almost certainly leak and corrode everything inside the unit.

I bought this bike for my daughter for Chistmas and put it together myself.  I will admit that it helps to have some prior knowledge of the mechanics of a bike before diving in.  I'm 37 and my younger brother and I worked on our bikes constantly.  If bikes were not your thing when you were a kid it just may be a little harder. Definitely not impossible though.  The bike is assembled in the factory totally and then taken apart in large pieces to fit in the box.  You will not build it from the ground up, this is told to you in the instructions.One problem is that the instruction manual is an all encompassing direction on how to build any of there mongoose bikes sold.  Example, it lists four different neck to fork assemblies with directions and has only one illustration, which doesn't happen to be for this bike.  You need to figure out which applies to this bike from the description, which may be frustrating for some people.  Then loosen some bolts and turn down the neck and stem 180 degrees (Stem will be inserted into forks and tightened with a hex key). This turning 180 degrees is not listed in the directions and may confuse some people.  Another thing, the front wheel when put on must be tightened with two wrenches at the same time in opposing direction so that the axel does not move, not mentioned in detail.Little things like this are small issues for the bike you are getting.  I can tell when putting it together that it's of a lot better quality than any of those Disney and Barbie bikes that I've seen and it comes in an 18", which is exactly what we needed.  Also, everything is adjustable, so the bike can grow with them in a sense.  My 5 year old rode it for 2 hours in the cold yesterday.  I couldn't get her off of it.  Some reviewers complained about the box.  Mine came in a sealed box with no issues, no parts missing.  I highly, highly recommend this bike.  You're daughter / granddaughter will love it!

The TomTom VIA 1535TM is our first dedicated nav unit.  Prior to this, we'd used the GPS functions and Google Maps on our cell phones.  There's a world of difference.First off, the TomTom is easy to hook up to a computer with the included right-angle USB cable.  Using Windows 7, and downloading the latest software from the TomTom website, the operating system identified the device and installed the appropriate driver.  No problems there.  However, at first the device was having some issues updating its own firmware.  I got an error message on the device that essentially said it wasn't talking with the TomTom web site.  I sent an e-mail to TomTom tech support, but never got an answer.  After poking around on TomTom support forums myself, I found that several people were having a similar problem.  I followed the suggested fix (resetting the device, monkeying with a few menu options in the TomTom computer software), and the device then downloaded a few large files, which included the latest map and firmware.  The device takes a long time to update, especially the map, which is a huge file.  (This is over a FiOS connection -- downloading the map alone took about 20 minutes.)We used the device to drive to a state park about 2.5 hours away from our home.  Planning the route was easy.  The touch screen interface on the device is intuitive, and the screen is large enough that it's easy to hit the right "keys."The unit incorporates a quick mount suction cup arm that fastens to your car's windshield.  (The arm is integrated into the device.  This isn't meant to be a handheld GPS unit.)  It also includes a flat, adhesive-backed plastic disc that fastens to your dash -- you can use it as a suction cup mounting surface if you don't want it on your windshield.  It will mount either way -- the unit will determine which side is up and adjust the screen accordingly.The trip to the state park went flawlessly.  The maps and directions were perfectly accurate, including on the small service roads in the state park.  In this respect, the directions were much better than those on Google Maps, which, in my experience, are often inaccurate.  The device recalculates routes quickly if you miss a turn.  The map also has a lot of up-to-date detail concerning restaurants, gas stations, etc.The voice of the device can be adjusted, and you can pay extra to download celebrity or cartoon voices from TomTom.Voice recognition works pretty well.  The device can quickly respond to requests for directions home or to the nearest restaurant/gas station, etc.In all, aside from the initial headache of getting the device to "talk" to the TomTom website, and aside from the completely unresponsive tech support, the device is nicely made and easy to use.  I would expect that TomTom will include a software fix that will eliminate the initial connection problem we had.  Highly recommended.

I recently purchased an Ozeri Razor pedometer, which is better than the Smart Health Walking Fit pedometer function in every way.  It is very thin and has a nice screen, with options to clip it to clothing, wear it around the neck, or carry it in the pocket.  It also detects steps more accurately.  However, the Walking Fit also functions as a very convenient heart rate monitor.  It is fast and having it on the the wrist allows a good reading without attaching anything to the chest.  Both options give basic statistics, though the main statistic of interest is the number of steps taken per day.While both types of devices can measure general activity level during a day, considering motion as "steps," having the device on the wrist measures a lot of false activity.  It is unclear if you've just had a rigorous workout or a fierce debate.A pedometer is a very useful device, and combining it with a wristwatch and heart rate monitor makes it a very convenient one as well.  If you don't mind wearing bulky watches, I recommend this watch to both fitness enthusiasts and people who want some motivation to start moving more.  It even tells time.

Received my new TOMTOM XL 340-TM today and was ready to try it out. Because this unit comes with lifetime updates I checked the TOMTOM website for any updates since my unit was produced.  There were updates so I "downloaded" them.  Then I tried my TOMTOM only to get the message "THERE IS NO MAP ON YOUR UNIT".  The process of updating the map had totally wiped out any data on my unit.  I was told by TOMTOM support that this TOMTOM unit is not compatible with my MAC OS X 10.6.6. operating system and that "MAC is working on a fix but we don't know when it will be available".  I had at least two choices, not counting throwing this unit in the trash.  I opted to drag out my PC and attempt to fix the problem.  It took over 2 hours for my PC (which is only 2 years old) to download the original map from the TOMTOM website.  Then, I had to download any updates to that map.I'm going to keep the TOMTOM now that it has a map and I was able to input FAVORITES, but I had to look up simple things like how to delete a duplicate FAVORITE that I added by accident.  I know my way around electronics, and this unit is not user friendly.  To delete that duplicate FAVORITE I had to go into the SETTINGS.  Hmmm... maybe Microsoft has something to do with the TOMTOM.  :-)Update 5/17/12:  I received an email from TomTom 3 months ago telling me updated maps were available to download.  Because of the initial problem (see above) I contacted TomTom to make sure they had fixed the bugs for MAC users.  I've since contacted them monthly and TomTom HOME is still not compatible with MAC Lion and upgrades are not available for my TomTom unit.  I have lifetime updates, but no way to get new maps because HOME doesn't work with MAC Lion.Update 7/17/12:  I turned on my TomTom unit today and it gave me a nasty note saying I have gone a LONG TIME without updating my maps, and they are seriously out of date.  SO..... I cranked up the MAC and read user notes that say that TOMTOM HOME is now compatible with m MAC.  Great!!!!  I downloaded the new maps and set down with a nice glass of wine to watch as the updates progressed, and my MAC immediately locked up.  I restarted and got a message from TomTom HOme that I needed to backup my map data before updating.  Then I got a message that TomTom Home could not backup my XL unit.  I give up.  TomTom has been a headache from day one.  If you are a MAC user don't get within a mile of a TomTom product.

Everyone else already nailed the great aspects of this unit, so I'll refrain.  I absolutely love my MBP, and the backlit LED keyboard and bright LED display are phenomenal.  I have one major gripe and a few smaller gripes, and this is why I gave it only four stars:BATTERY LIFE:Apple, like most other manufacturers, tends to be misleading in touting its technology.  That's the nature of marketing, so be it.  Getting 8 hours out of this battery just isn't realistic.  I have traveled many times with this computer, and if I watch mpeg4 movies (not DVD) for 2 hours on two flights, my battery is usually toast at the end of four hours, if not sooner.  This being said, I turn the display brightness way down, and the LED keyboard off (wireless/bluetooth as well).  The mpeg4 movies tax the processor(s), yes, but I was very let down by the battery life.  It barely outperforms my T series 9 cell Thinkpad when it was new.  Apple breaks technological barriers by print, nothing more.  If their 8-hour battery were real, it'd be a great leap in technology... problem is, its just not there, its simply a statement that people believe and no one calls them on it.  See my review on the 27" iMac... if you're open minded, you'll start to understand.I'm also a little frustrated that apple has not released a quad core unit.  The quad core will use quite a bit more power, which will blast their "8 hour" feature.. hence, even the flagship desktop replacement pictured here is only available in a core 2 duo.  Three words describe this - antiquated $2500 laptop.  I can't hold it against the product though, the core 2 duo performs great and most folks will never notice the difference provided by two more cores.  Obviously folks that buy the 17-inch MBP aren't as concerned with the mobility as much as those who would purchase the 13.3" MBP... so why not offer the quad core in the 17-inch?  Core power management would still allow nearly the same battery life.On the glossy/matte displays:If you are going to watch lots of movies, mostly at night or on planes, get the glossy display.  If you will be working in bright offices with lots of fluorescent lighting, or working outside at all, you need to get the anti-glare display.Docking stations?Why oh why has apple not manufactured a docking station for their flagship mobile computer?  Apple's greed bothers me.  They won't produce the dock station, because they don't want anyone replacing their imac or mac pro with a mobile solution that will do both.  It would also require another hole in the body for the docking port, which we all know is a major problem for Jobs... he hates holes.

I like to use a toner to be sure that there are no traces of make-up left after I cleanse my face in the evening.  I want to make sure that I'm starting with a clean slate (so to speak) before applying night cream or moisturizer.  I have combination skin (mostly "normal" with a bit of an oily T-Zone) and did not experience any stinging when I used this. I think this product would work well for most skin types.There are some skincare products that I think are worth spending a few extra dollars for, but toner is not one of them. I have been a long time fan of my expert mouse. Been using it for many years and would never try anything else. The only complaint i could have with it was that i wish the scroll ring moved smoother, and it was not on such a high angle. Even with the pad you could feel the tension in your wrist after a long day of hard work.So the other day it went out. I was in a panic fearing this day would come because using a mouse is out of the question. I wanted to just buy a new one but i also figured there would must be something better out by now after all these years. I noticed the new SlimBlade. Question was would it fit my hand well and is it smooth. Reading many of the reviews i was very concerned because this is no cheap product. I just received it and fell in love with it within the first 5 seconds of using it. I design circuit boards for a living so there are some features that i really need, and use allot. i need a ball to operate very smoothly with small movements from a stop. I also need to be able to zoom in and out quickly which the scroll feature does a great job at this.Some of the other reviewers have mentioned that it does not work with Win7 64-bit. Well lets lay that to rest. It does. On top of that i never even bothered to install its software. I plugged it in and started using it right away.From reading other reviews that actually had me concerned but now that i have it i know they are just very anal people who probably complain about the sun color.For the anal, it does make small feedback clicks when you twist the ball, however it is not that loud at all. your mouse click sound is louder that it.The typical mouse click sound is a bit off in sound from what we are used to. I did not notice the button sensitivity issue of the button someone else made mention of.All in all this is the best trackball i have used. Is it worth the price, NO its way to expensive for a HID. But i would recommend it.

Rigid plastic ammo can with watertight seal.  Has one clasp and two locations for locks (not included).  Since the only photo is so deceiving, let me tell you they've probably stacked 4x4 of the .40/.45 ACP boxes in the picture.  There's enough room on the non-handle side that you should be able to stack another box for a total of (18).  With 9mm, you have room for 5x4 + 5 (25), because there's space left over on one end to fit an opposing-facing stack.  For .38/.357 boxes, figure 4x4 +4 (20).  Note: I haven't tried to load 25 9mm boxes full of ammo to see how heavy the can gets, so I don't know if that would exceed the allotted weight recommendation.I currently store my ammo (all store-bought) in a safe, but I'm getting ready to start (re)loading my own rounds.  I'm guessing that before long, I'll have one of these filled (loose) to fit each caliber of my pistols.

I was looking for a decent ACOG clone to put on my M&P; 15-22, something that would be of enough quality to retain zero as well as give the general look of an ACOG. I know there are a lot of folks out there who will say right off the bat "don't buy a clone optic, buy a real one"... well, for me personally I can't find it in my wallet to spend a grand on a "real" optic for what is more or less itself a "clone" rifle in .22LR. Those folks will also say "don't buy a clone, just buy a -fill in name of cheap red dot or scope-"... well, again I want something that looks appropriate on my 15-22. While I have no doubt there is a wild gulf of quality low cost optics on the market, I'm embarrassed to say I'm more concerned with this optic looking like it belongs on the rifle. Yes that is a "mall ninja" opinion to have but hey... clone optic for a clone rifle.As for the optic itself it is metal bodied and painted with a semigloss black finish. For those who might be concerned this optic DOES NOT have any trademarks or markings of any kind on it. The photo in the Amazon listing shows a fake green fiber optic tube, mine came with a red one... but this is immaterial as it is just for show. Also for those who care the fake fiber optic tube on this optic is clear unlike some of the other clones who's are fuzzy or frosted. It has a traditional mildot reticle and not an ACOG reticle, but the glass is clear has a decent field of view. Much like a real ACOG optic the eye relief on this clone is very shallow, meaning you have to really get your eye close to the lens to use it. I wear glasses and there are times that I feel like my frames will bump the optic my eye is so close. If this optic was mounted on a caliber larger than a .22LR I'd be worried about it bumping my frames on recoil. The rear eyepiece also unscrews. Real ACOGs don't do that.The optic comes in a plain white box with no markings. It is seated in foam in a plastic bag and comes with a black lens cloth. That's it... no user manual, no tools or other mounts. Just the optic and a lens cloth. I didn't expect anything more but some folks who might expect more could be let down by the spartan packaging.The mount the optic comes pre-installed with is a traditional two thumbscrew 20mm weaver rail mount that the sight attaches to with two screws through the bottom. I don't know if this optic can be mounted on an AR15 carry handle or not... it appears that it could be as the shape of the foot that goes into the mount looks correct but the optic doesn't come with any additional screws and I don't want to detach the optic from the mount for fear it will mess it up. The thumbscrews on the mount have locking washers to help keep them tight and they are slightly smaller than the normal thumbscrews found on AR15 detachable carry handles.I installed the optic on my M&P; 15-22 and went to the range the same day I got it. It zeroed easily enough and held zero for the whole shooting session. Long term durability is unknown right now but I don't see anything about the optic that would lead me to believe it will fail on a .22LR. Accuracy of the 15-22 with the optic is what I would expect... it's not "everything through the same hole" but it's also not all over the paper.I'd say this optic is good for .22LR clone rifles like my M&P; 15-22 or a Sig 522... something with a top rail that an ACOG would look good on. And one would expect that if this optic can survive a .22LR that it would live just as well on an airsoft. That said, I would not expect this optic to survive use on a higher caliber rifle or for it to live long under "duress". This optic is a clone that fills a need for people like me... all it has to do is look good on the .22LR rifle it's on and function at a rifle range. It does both.In summary: This is a good clone optic for people looking for a clone optic. I only gave it 4 out of 5 stars due to it's spartan packaging and the very shallow eye relief.

Since this is my first reloading scale I will give you my overall impression. First I opted for the beam scale over digital scales in general after hearing about too many disappointed people with their digital scales.So after unpacking carefully I was surprised at how light the scale base is. For some reason I thought it would be much heavier, but it is about the weight of a  medium sized stapler.After reading the instructions I proceeded to zero the scale in. I found that I had to adjust the leveling foot quite a bit in order to do this. Still the scale zeroed in quite nicely. The magnetic dampening is a big plus. As far as the poises go - there are three of them - 1 large, 1 medium and 1 small. The largest has some good weight to it and is easy to set in the notches. The medium poise is much lighter and you have to be careful to get it set in the notch. The smallest is very light and you have to be super careful to place it in the notch correctly.I decided to weigh a penny and this turned out to be quite easy, then I practiced with a reloading charge using some sugar. I set the beam scale to the desired grain weight and then started to sprinkle some sugar in the tray. The scale is very sensitive - as the weight gets close to the setting it only takes a few grains over to tip the scale - I can see how a powder trickler would come in handy.OHAUS makes the scales for RCBS ( says so on the packaging ) I was a bit surprised actually but it seems to be a good purchase. I think an investment in a scale cover would be a good idea as well. Overall this 5-0-5 scale appears to do what it is supposed to do. You may have to figit with it at first but I think it should stay zeroed in quite nicely - if you move it to another table/bench you will have to zero it in all over again but that should be rather simple.

I will admit that I am very careful about taking care of my skin.  I even limit myself on what types of products I use because I like my skin to look flawless.  Until now, I have not heard of the AINHOA brand.  This product/brand is apparently offered to guests staying at the high end hotels.  Product is originally made in Barcelona, Spain.On the first look, this serum lists the following active ingredients: caviar and gold. I bought the Kershaw Military Boot Knife around the same time as this Cold Steel Kobun.  The Kershaw is the closest you can get to a "dagger" you can legally carry, has great corrosion resistance and was very well made.  However, this Cold Steel Kobun is longer, thicker, stronger, more attractive and substantially lighter!  The Kobun is REDICULOUSLY lightweight and slim. The knife+sheath was still thinner than my Ruger LCP that hangs a few inches away.  This knife would be the closest you can get to a legal and concealable samurai sword.  I would never dare stab anything with the Kershaw blade unless it was my only option.  The blade on the kershaw is less than 1/16" thick for the last 2 inches, but this Cold steel Kobun has a more than 1/8" wide blade all the way up til the last 3/4" of the blade.  The hand guard is not meant to be very functional on the either the Kobun or the Kershaw, and the handles are very thin.  The cold steel kobun came out ahead again by having a very well textured Kraton handle (rubber-like plastic) that offers very good hold on this knife.  The Cold Steel Kobun came razor sharp out of the box (even the 3/4" of tanto upsweep could shave).  The Kershaw Military Boot knife was dull enough make a sawing motion on my finger without breaking the skin, but if applied in great force, the thin blade may help that edge.  This Cold Steel Kobun is the best blade for the money by far.

There's a vast amount of heat generated inside of a PC, especially if it's used for a lot of gaming and/or graphics activity. Add a few components such as an upgraded graphics card or sound card, and the temperature begins to climb even more. Adding extra RAM results in additional heat buildup. The original standard case fans help cool the system, but by and large they usually only provide basic airflow that's usually sufficient for the PC for which they were designed.Heat erodes the life span of electronic components and devices. High case and component temperatures effectively making a PC more unstable and decrease the life of the components. If you increase air circulation, you will lower the case temperature. For cost-conscious shoppers and PC enthusiasts who want maximum bang-for-the-buck, use a cost-effective PCI slot fan. They work very well due to the fact that they exhaust the hot air directly out of the back of the case, instead of reticulating it.Some of the best of these are the highly ratedCyclo PCI Slot Blue Led Fanfrom Thermaltake and theAntec Cyclone Blower Case Fan. Placing either of these in an open slot as close to a hot PCI video card or other high activity adapter board will usually give excellent results towards lowering the temperature inside of the computer case, and either is good for removing hotspots over PCI graphics cards.Though it doesn't exhaust the heat from the PC case, theVantec Spectrum PCI Fan Card with Blue UV LEDmay prove to be another good solution, especially if it's placed in between hot graphics cards and sound cards. This Spectrum Fan Card serves a dual purpose of directly cooling any PCI/AGP cards surrounding it as well as providing better air circulation with two adjustable fans. This card works quite well with any of the above PCI cards as it's providing extra airflow while one of the other cards exhausts heat from the case.Some of these cards feature built-in LED lights to light up the case while cooling it down, if that's your thing. Read the specs on each for more info.Before you begin installing any of these cards, make sure your existing fans (and the rest of your components) aren't full of dust, because that will increase the temperature. In short, get rid of the dust bunnies before you start. To install any of these cards, just find an empty PCI slot next to your video card, slide it in, screw it in, and plug the power cable into a free Molex connector (the one that plugs into the hard drive).I bought thisCyclo PCI Slot Blue Led Fanlocally, and it's been doing a great job since it was installed. One or more of these cards will keep your system temperatures down... and help you keep your cool as well.7/25/2008
