I picked up the PanOxyl 10% Bar when I couldn't find the Foaming Wash at my local Wal-Mart which usually has the Foaming Wash of the same type, which I am still an enormous fan of, at a reasonable price. The bar was $4 cheaper as well so I figured hey, same thing but in a bar, why not right?Well, for reasons that I cannot explain, my acne has never flared up so badly with a Benzoyl Peroxide medication. I can't remember the last time I had so many zits on my forehead and cheeks. The kind of zits that are stubborn too, they returned over and over again. I am under the impression that the medication's tendency to dry the skin accelerated oil production. Even using a good lotion after application made no difference. Maybe there was something wrong in my routine I don't know.Taking a look at the ingredients the two are different. Not my much, but they are different. Perhaps my skin just disliked something in the bar. Either way, I cannot rate this product very highly for what it did to my face. I have since switched back to the PanOxyl Foaming Face Wash of the same grade and my skin has started clearing up again within a matter of a few days which makes me think something about the bar, whatever it was, just didn't get along with my skin. I am sure this bar will work miracles for most, but those of you who have some more sensitive skin like mine, try the Face Wash instead, it works wonderfully.

As the white mother of two biracial (black-white) girls, I will freely admit that the hair issue has been one of the most daunting aspects of parenthood for me.  I am always in the market for something that controls my daughters' curls (the worst possible combination of my husband's tight curls and my wavy, crazy, frizzy hair).  When I saw this I thought it would be worth trying out.The product instructions say to use an "adequate" amount of the product on damp hair, but they neglect to say what constitutes "adequate".  Through trial and error, I've found it takes slightly more than a quarter-sized blob to cover my daughter's mass of curls.As others have noted, it's pretty thin, which means it pours easily out of the bottle (a bit too easily perhaps) and spreads easily throughout the hair.  I don't particularly notice a fragrance one way or another, but my daughter says she likes the smell.  It feels a bit greasy on my hands, but no moreso than other similar products I've tried.  It does wash fairly easily off my hands, unlike the curl perfecter which we also tried.But the long and short of it is that it doesn't do much.  In the short run, it leaves my daughter's hair looking slightly wet and conditioned.  As the wetness dries, it controls frizz for a short time, but by the time my daughter comes home from school, or even a short play date at the park, her hair is every bit as frizzy and out of control as it ever is.We're not going to throw this bottle away, but we won't be buying more.

The ingredients are as follows:Water, cetyl alcohol, propylene glycol, sodium lauryl sulfate, stearyl alcohol, methylparaben, propylparaben, butylparaben.Far from "gentle" are the above listed, however, they are common in many products. Sodium Lauryl Sulphate is a harsh surfacant, Propylene gylcol is a KNOWN skin irritant. Obviously there is a lot of alcohol too.The parabens are all preservatives and the most disturbing. Methyparaben is known to cause breast cancer AND"The October 3, 2006, issue of Toxicology published a study that shows when skin treated with creams that contain methylparaben was exposed to the sun's UVB rays, the skin cells died and there was considerable oxidative stress on the skin's tissue. Oxidative stress, according to the National Institute of Standards and Technology, is responsible for a number of disease processes, including cancer."If you're male and you THINK you're safe think again"Parabens, including methylparaben, have estrogenic affects on the body, which means they can affect the reproductive glands. The January 2009 issue of Reproductive Toxicology reports that there is a probable interaction between parabens and the health and activity of cells in the testes, meaning a possible decrease in sperm production in males."I purchased this due to my sensitive skin. I have never displayed an allergy to ANYTHING skin related before I just have a bit of dryness from some products. I dont have many allergies, and certainly not skin allergies.I have used Cetaphil bars in the past with little concern. I figured liquid wash might be better. But I turned into a puffy looking lobster color anywhere it touched within 2 min of rinsing. I had to spend 24 hours inside until my skin calmed and take benadryl.This reaction confused my dermatologist tremendously as he recommends cetaphil to his patients with allergies. He said there must be some questionable ingredients within Cetaphil cleanser. This is the point I looked the above information up, and although I quoted Livestrong for information I found many instances and actual case studies.I'm left flabbergasted. Just beware that even though its marked "mild" thats due to it not having fragrance, and there are some harsh, potentially dangerous ingredients inside. I personally ended up moving onto home made, unscented cold process soap for my face.

...compared to my Infrashine, which in my opinion, had been comparable to the 1 inch CHI for years. I've loved the Infrashine, and have always felt that it is less damaging to my hair than the Conair or others available in the supermarkets and department stores. I can't decide between 3 star or 4 star, but based on my experiences on reviews, 3 stars meaning I probably won't use it much at all and 4 stars meaning I would use somewhat often, I gave it a 4.I have naturally wavy (at the end) black Asian hair. Somewhat thick but the salon stylists I've talked to said I have "normal" hair. I normally use my irons at around 350-400 degrees.I was very skeptical about the Wahl Pro. It arrived with obvious signs of use and some scratch-looking marks on the plates that I could not clean off, so I was a bit scared to use it, but decided I'd try it on a little bit of my hair to see how it goes (after cleaning the whole iron). It also doesn't come with any manuals, but I am not sure if that's just the one I got or all of them.I don't like putting any styling products on my hair so I didn't even bother trying the conditioning strip, especially after reading some of the other reviews about how it doesn't seem to make a difference. I did, however, use the comb attachment, which I LOVED, to my surprise.The comb attachment makes straightening the back side of my head EASY. In the past, I've always had a terrible time with the back of my head even with the expensive irons, because my body just wouldn't contort that way and my straightening job would always be pretty bad in the back because I couldn't run the brushes straight so it would often be uneven and crooked. With the comb attachment, my hair is straightened more evenly and it SEEMS to do a good job. Just make sure you brush your hair BEFORE using that attachment. I don't think it's meant to brush it for you from the start, just to keep hair in place.I am not sure how good it is for my hair though, because the quality doesn't seem nearly as good as my Infrashine. The ceramic plates don't close all the way flatly against each other (the tip end of the iron closes but the handle end doesn't, leaving a very obvious gap, which I think would cause the heating to be uneven). I am not sure what Tourmaline technology is though... my Infrashine never had it and the nice CHI irons I've seen never had it either.I would still use my Infrashine over this one but if I really want to straighten the back of my head, I would use this iron instead. At this price point, I think it's a worthy purchase. Not so sure without the attachment though. Although one down side is it DOES seem to DRY the tips of my hair more than my Infrashine. I do not like that part at all.

I've had this monitor now for almost a month and I've hooked it up to all of my devices. I bought it when it was a featured lightning deal for $129 and it was a great deal considering I paid $129 for not just a 23" LED but an "IPS" LED! For its size, 23" imho is just right and definitely an upgrade from my old 19". It's also an LED screen which makes it brighter and has better contrast levels and viewing angles than that LCD. That one was a 19" Samsung LCD PC monitor I've had since 2007 and it has great colors even today but this one is very clear, very sharp, and the frame is sturdy (the Samsung's glossy plastic fame got a crack early on and is full of scratches now). I also really like the base it stands on. My one complaint is that the controls for the OSD are hard to reach and are touch sensitive so it can be difficult to navigate the options since they are inset under the screen on the base. I guess it's a case of style versus function. One BIG surprise to me was that it even has built-in SRS Premium speakers! I guess I didn't really read through the description, but that makes the value on this thing just incredible! I've used it as an auxiliary monitor for a laptop, a main monitor for a desktop, an output display for a Motorola Atrix HD Android smartphone, and even a full on 1080p HDTV display for an Xbox 360. In each application the monitor is flawless. Initially you might have to adjust settings to your personal preference (the speakers were at maximum volume for instance).Here's my breakdown+ IPS LED technology has superb viewing angles+ black levels/contrast ratio is superb+ the screen is bright and colors are reproduced vibrantly+ 23" is a perfect size, not too big not too small+ sturdy and stylish frame construction and base+ connectivity options include HDMI, DVI with HDCP, VGA, audio in and out+ built-in SRS Premium Sound speakers deliver clear and crisp experience+ eco friendly and energy efficient design saves power and doesn't compromise performance- the OSD controls are hard to reach and touch sensitive, weird combo- no DisplayPortI would recommend this monitor to anyone regardless of application.

Disclaimer - I am a fixed blade lover/believer/carrier - if I had to have one knife, it would always be a fixed blade.  I even carry small fixed blades on my neck at times, belt at times, and a RAT (ESEE) Izula in my EDC bag.BUT - there are times when you want a great folder - or maybe a great slicer.  Or a featherweight knife (yes, it IS very light).  A friend knew of my love of fixed blades - and a childhood horror story of a folder about taking my fingers off) and said he would prove to me a folder had a place.I was presented with a Left handed version with the new steel and the G10 handles as proof.The history of this knife is well known - check it out.  The story behind it is solid.  Sal at Spiderco is a dedicated owner to his passionate customers...it's a good match.  They stand behind their product well.  There is a reason the knife has been in almost constant production for over a decade...and many say it's the single folder that all large folders are judged against.I'm not sure about that last line - as I doubt I'll ever own another folder.  First, I won't buy one, and second the Military will last the next 40-5- years of my life and then some. It's that tough.I won't be using it to build a shelter, spit wood, or dig a hole like I could with a fixed blade...but cleaning a fish, cutting rope, and even mundane tasks like opening boxes seem tailor made for the Spiderco Military.  I've carried this knife either on my person or in my bag for about 6 months now.  It's still as razorsharp as when I got it, the full flat grind makes it easy to sharpen.  If you are so inclined, Spiderco will put a scary edge on it for only $5 anytime you want them too...that's gotta be great for those who struggle with a stone.  buy two, use one and send it back, rotate the other one to useage.  pretty cool.It carries really well in my dress slacks or shorts or even sweatpants...it's so light that it can even be carried in the pocket of mesh basketball shorts and it won't drag down that side.The lock-up seems very tight and give a nice loud click.  It has no movement in the blade, and seems very strong.  Again, I'm not going to test it much...but I don't fear it closing accidently on me while I'm using for what I do use it for.Presentation is simple, fast, and quite impressive.  Should the need ever arise and I'm not in an area that I can carry my firearm legally...the Military does have quite a deterent effect. My state has no limit on the length of blade, make sure to check yours of course.So, if you love folders...this is a great one. And even if you don't trust them completely like I don't...this one will give you a lot of confidence in its use.

I've gone through a lot of compasses bushwhacking and the Suunto (Finland) is the best.  The edges are raised and beveled so with gloves on, you can easily rotate the compass for correct bearings.  It's great for use on a map (I laminate all my outdoor maps) as it contains two rubber pads that prevent slippage.  It also measures in centimeters, inches and 1"=1 mile on its three sides.  The markings show up easily in the dark or low light and the magnifying glass is convenient for very small reading.  There are even holes for marking up your maps for orienteering, but I have never used these.  I could see how for some hard-core hikers in the back country, it would be helpful.  The red lanyard makes it even easier to just hang around your neck.

The trackball on top is not very responsive, and there is no way to adjust the sensitivity of this mouse like you can with a TrackMan3.  The actual casing of the mouse is a very cheap plastic.  I feel like if I dropped this thing on my desk, it would shatter.4.  The actual design is awkward and uncomfortable.  First, the finger hole is really small, so if you have anything near a normal sized hand, you are already at a disadvantage.  Next, the buttons for the mouse are WAAAY to far up on the actual casing, they need to be farther back on the mouse, closer to the thumb to be convinient or effective.Overall, I am really dissapointed with this product, and even though it seems like it would make a great product, I would wait for a legitimate company (like Logitech) to make a similar product.  I would not recommend this product.
The only thing that would make this better is if the plugs folded for travel.  Otherwise, it's a great travel charger for car and wall outlets.

I have long hair of a medium thickness. Following the instructions I applied this to my hair when it was wet. The cream is thick enough to scoop out and not run everywhere. A really good buy considering the price vs video quality. I use it on a sega saturn and it is nearly (if not) pixel perfect and of course it is also upscaled to fit my LCD TV screen. It appears to automatically pick up the input signal type wether its NTSC or PAL and convert it to be compatible with your HD TV.I have heard it will also strip HDCP encoding from HDMI signals so if you are trying to record your PS3 that may be good news for you. Personally I have not tried this yet but I am fairly confident it will,. I will update if otherwise.I have not noticed any artifacts, latency/lag, or anything of that nature. I will update if anything occurs down the line.The only downside of using this is that you have to get different SCART cables for the various systems, and they have to be in the correct format; NTSC if you are in the US and PAL if you are in the EU. The cables can be expensive and hard to find for NTSC systems. A word of warning however do not cheap out and get flimsy ones or your video quality may suffer. Alternatively you may build your own SCART cables., the pinouts for most systems are online.[According to AdamKoralik(youtube)] SCART cables are availabe for/compatible with: Sega Saturn, Atari Jaguar, Atari Jaguar CD, Nintendo Super Nintendo [original model only], Sega Master System, Sega Genesis (Mega Drive), Sega CD (Mega CD), Sega 32X, Sony Playstation (1), SNK Neo Geo AES, SNK, Neo Geo CD.

I had used these McNett wraps before, having used the ACU camo ones for some of my military gear.  Multicam is the new flavor so I figured I'd pick up a couple to have on hand. My hopes are high that the camo pattern will hold longer on these, but if not, they're not too expensive.

Either some of the 5-star reviewers here have low expectations from a bulky, expensive portable mattress used "in the wild" or Thermarest has done an excellent job of promoting that "halo" on their brand. I can't give this 5 stars but admittedly, I can't quite figure out how to rationalize the 3 solid stars I think it's worth.The build quality of this mattress is excellent. It does self inflate and only requires 2-3 good puffs to get it really full if you like things firm. (I have the large which is 30" wide). But even with no puffs I find it just too firm and can't quite find that sweet spot where it's soft enough to be comfortable yet firm enough to handle the pressure points of a side sleeper.Before using mine I inflated it for a few days. It's a pain in the arse to deflate for packing. (Yeah, I do the "fold" thing as prescribed). Eventually, I get mine into the 30" x 7" stuff sack. This is no surprise and I'm not downgrading it for that. It just makes you wonder what niche market Thermarest is catering to. All of these Luxury Map mattresses regardless of size are way too big for backpacking and If you car camp where size/weight isn't a big issue... why not take something with you that gives you even more comfort? (And I wouldn't want to roll this mattress out at home for a guest. I think I could do better than that).So, after sleeping on this thing a half dozen times now, I question why the heck I just didn't buy a half a dozen cheap air mattresses that would give me 8" of loft and a felt flocked topper. Much more comfort flexibility, they're wider and mimic your home mattress more so than a 3" pad. (until they leak!). Plus, a twin sized air mattress will pack up much smaller than this even with a pump. (but weigh more of course). Again, the Thermarest mattress is not really that "portable".I dunno. I look at all the glowing praises here, scratch my head and ask myself if I'm sleeping on it wrong. I don't think so. I know everyone's sleeping habits are different and we all get different results depending on what we sleep on at home. (I come from a normal no frills bed). But everyone else here seems to be subjectively rating it on personal comfort so I'll do the same; it's just too firm for *me*. And before anyone says, "What did you expect, it's only a 3" mattress?" well... I read the reviews. :)Don't get me wrong... again, the build quality is great, the valves are solid and it *will* keep your arse from scraping the tent floor... you just won't know if this works for your body until you get one.

I recently bought a new Springfield XDS which is a compact (5+1) 45ACP pistol, single stack magazine.  It can definitely be a chore trying to get the Handall Jr onto the gun, but that's a good thing, you put in some effort up front and then the thing does not move, which is exactly what you want.I neglected to read the directions first, which advise you to heat the grip up to make the rubber easier to work with; oops.  Anyway, I was able to work it on there but ultimately found I actually prefer it to be installed upside down because of where the finger ridge meets my hand, so you may want to consider which way you put it on before installing it since it isn't exactly fun to remove and do again.The Springfield does have a grip safety.  My advice is to cut the rubber away a bit AFTER installing the Handall on the gun; you have a much greater chance of splitting it down the middle when you're pushing and tugging on it to get it onto the gun than you do cutting some material away after install.  I was able to cut out a notch for the grip safety using regular scissors.

I tried a number of routers before settling on the N66u.  My primary need was to support multiple devices; 3-4 streaming devices (TVs, DVD, Wii etc.), 5 active PCs and 6 or so Android devices.  The devices are spread across the house and the cable/modem and router are located in the basement on the back side of the firewall that encloses the furnace ( don't ask).  I had okay luck with an old LinkSys WRT but it was on its last legs.  Two other routers had trouble both with the number of devices and reach to the far end of the house where several of the PCs and streaming devices resideTh RT-N66U has improved overall streaming peformance (Netflix, Amazon Prime, and YouTube) and has no difficulty dealing with variety and number of devices.  The setup was very easy.  The router has every feature I could want - guest network (which I leverage), easy DHCP management, filtering, parental controls etc.  I will admit that I haven't stressed tested all features but I am thoroughly satisfied with the ease of use and performance.

Read this review for the extra stuff others may not have mentioned - I won't repeat the specs or what's included, I'll explain what I love about my new Zune HD and compare it to the original Zune 30gb, Zune 80gb and Zune 8gb.TV-OUTPUT Finally Fixed!=========================That's right, you no longer have to manually set TV-OUT like you did with the Zune 30/80/120gb ones.  You set this once and everytime you put your Zune in the docking station or turn on the TV/receiver the Zune HD A/V dock (NOT included) will auto-detect and automatically send the output to the TV.  This is especially awesome for me because I have a flip-out LCD in my car (Alpine).  I use the standard composite RCA cable (included only with the separate Zune HD A/V dock) which connects to one of my Aux inputs.  If I leave the Zune HD in the car or put it in the dock, it will auto-switch to the LCD screen.[Note: there is a small catch, if you used tv-output with HDMI and then switch to composite, you need to turn off the Zune in order for it to automatically go into tv-out mode, if you don't power off, you'll see it is finicky to make tv-output work at all; you'll knowattamean when you run into this.]Now I have wonderful HD radio as well as all of my music.  The screensaver is pretty slick as well.  It was the tv-output alone that made the Zune 80gb frustrating for me in my car - I had to manually turn this on every single time I got into the car.What is also nice is that it will auto-pause your music or radio whenever you turn the car off - it just knows.  The only downside is that you have to press the Play button on the remote when entering the car - but this is soooo minor.Internet/WiFi:===============1. This is absolutely great when you don't want to lug your laptop around or if you're watching sports and want to settle some silly stats argument.  Also, when you get a question like, "Hey, wasn't Robert De Niro in a movie with Jodie Foster" then you can enjoy your rented movie and log into IMDB with your Zune HD and find out. :)2. My friend with an I-Phone says the 'tilt' functionality on the Zune HD is far faster than the 3GS Iphone that he owns and he thinks the UI is just as slick, minus the sensitivity of the keyboard.  Lack of ability to watch You-Tube or [...] on the Zune HD is a bummer though.Music:========1. The smart DJ playlists and Channels you can subscribe to that auto-pick your songs is a dream.  My Ipod friend has no concept of subscription services like the Zune Pass in his ITunes store so when I demo'd the Marketplace and the Zune Pass he was a bit jealous.  Now of course, he showed me all of the TV shows and movies he had access to, so I hope the Zune Marketplace grows their library.What I don't like:=====================1. The clock, when your Zune HD is being charged, well, there's no real way to get to it...at all.  It is only visible when it goes to power-saving/screen-lock mode and you press the big I______I button on the front.  Sometimes you want to charge/sync your Zune (with the dock) and see the clock or skip back to the home screen and view the clock - this is easy to do on the Zune 30gb, 8gb, and 80gb that I have owned.  This is nothing a firmware update can't fix...2. I do wish the Zune Marketplace had MORE apps and MORE tv-shows like "Community" etc and MORE newer movies to download, but thta's only a matter of time.Everything else about the Zune, I truly love - I love having HD Radio, I love the new music Equalizer, I love the styling, the size, the UI, I truly love everything about it.  I wish I could have this in a phone, but for now, I love carrying my Zune HD everywhere I go.

I have used the HDP as a desktop hub for all my computer-based listening for about six months. Its connections and footprint made it the most suitable contender for a high-quality interface to sit on a desktop without taking up too much space. I have it connected to a MacBook Pro and a set of AudioEngine 2 speakers. I use a pair of Sennheiser 535's and Beyerdynamic DT 880' 600 ohm as my primary headphone inputs. All in all, the fidelity of the HDP is excellent given its price point and its size. I own a UDAC2, which is a wonderful and tiny addition to any computer-based music set-up, and the HDP is much better in its sound reproduction. However, my primary reason for giving this device three stars is its build quality, and second, its relatively low power.At first, the unit appears very well made, with a brushed aluminum cover and solid knobs. However, I often use the device in a darkened environment, and I noticed that light from the internals was leaking from the right side of the faceplate. Upon closer inspection, I could see that the front plate did not align properly with the aluminum case. Moreover, as others have pointed out, the unit stays on, even when the power is turned off. When I powered the unit down, an internal red light came on, and it was clearly visible through the gap in the faceplate. I contacted NuForce service, and they agreed to send out a new unit, but only after they had received my faulty unit. I complied, and had to go without desktop audio for ten days, which is an annoyance, since I use my audio setup for music creation as well as listening. When the second unit arrived, I was dismayed to find that the light bleed was even worse. Back on the phone to customer support, who this time agreed to send me yet a third unit. Guess what? Same problem with the third unit. According to the factory, this known defect is "within the factory tolerances" of this Taiwan-made unit. The Icon HDP that I own does not have this problem.The other area of concern is the lack of power when it comes to driving high-impedance phones like the DT880's. The unit is a preamp for external speakers, so it either requires active monitors or an amp in the chain. But for headphone use, this device is billed as a Class A amplifier. I don't listen to music that loudly, and I often listen to demanding classical music. There is no doubt that the HDP reproduces sound very well, but it takes turning the volume to well past 3 o'clock on the rotary dial to get the music to that level where it "fills" the cans. For low impedance phones, this is not an issue, but for high impedance cans such as the DT 880's and others of its caliber, it is a major issue. Even at high volume, I never have the sense that the unit is really bringing out the best that the cans can offer.  While at that level the amplification is sufficient, there is a sense that the unit is really straining. I had a Fio E9 headphone amp for a short period of time, and though a different beast altogether, it handled the impedance of the DT880's with nary a hiccup. Given that the HDP bills itself as an audiophile-grade headphone amp (and one of the reasons I invested in it), and that the company states that it can handle the most demanding headphones, its lack of power is surprising and off putting.  I often get the feeling as I listen to one of my mixes or a demanding piece of music that it is struggling to keep up.I gave serious thought to returning the third unit, primarily because I believe that a device that bills itself as audiophile quality and costs $450 ought to be constructed sufficiently well so that odd lights and gaps don't reveal themselves. In the end, I kept the unit, primarily because the sound quality is excellent, and primarily because I could not find another device at this price that does what the HDP does in terms of its simple interface and useful connectivity. The combination of its headphone amp quality (though underpowered), its DAC for computer-based applications, and its high quality preamp ultimately compelled me to keep it. Just barely.

I wish someone had told me that the 3.0 device plugged into a USB 2.0 port would cause the enclosed hard drive to grind to a halt.  When I tried to copy data to it, after a while, it would say "the file you're trying to copy could not be found *on the destination*."  When I looked in the Event Log, I saw tons of RaidPort resets for the drive and "retry logical IO."Swapping drives (I thought maybe it was the 4TB drive) didn't help.  Nothing else did either.  Using it with a computer that had a USB 3.0 interface did.  Retrofitting the original machine with a 3.0 card made it work great.Btw, I recommend the "Anker Uspeed Superspeed USB 3.0 PCI-E Express Card with 4 USB 3.0 Ports" as a cheap, easy, upgrade for a non-3.0 machine that just works.  Windows installed drivers automatically, and now I sustain 100MB/s+ transfer rates for hours at a time.

This scope is a great value for the money. However, don't expect to much when trying to focus using 24x. Image resolution is a function of objective lens diameter. The 40mm objective lens will be marginal, if you are expecting to see a bullet hole at 200-yds. Seeing holes at 100-yds is no problem. An 18x will allow that. Thus you do not need a spotting scope.Magnification can be misleading in that high magnification just produces a larger blur. The objective [front lens] produces the image that the eyepiece magnifies.If you are shooting varmits at extreme ranges, they don't have to appear in absolute focus. All you need to do is to get them in the cross-hairs. I also use a 50mm sniper scope that is clearer, but it is physically larger and must be mounted on higher rings.My recommendation is to define your primary scope purpose and then try one out. You must balance physical size and convenience to practical performance. If you do this, you will not be disappointed by the scope not meeting your expectations.

I wish the joule rating was a little higher but this is not bad all things considered.  The clamping voltage is low which is a good thing and something that many competing products may not have.  You see lots of product out there with high joule ratings and really high clamping voltages which is bad because with a high clamping voltage, the chances of it tripping to protect your electronics is lower. I'm a hunter, but I also do a lot of other things outdoors.  I recently have been switching my base layers from your average plastic synthetic (capeline, polypropylene, etc) to all MERINO wool, which is a game changer.  I had a buff type garment from a different company, that was made of polyester, and always wanted to replace it with a merino garment (like I've been doing with my other base layers, all the way to glove liners and socks).  This item topped it off perfectly!  The buff is awesome to wear as a loose neck gaiter, or tucked up into your beanie as a neck gaiter/mouth mask, and so many more ways of wearing it--and topping it all off, you get a buff that's made of merino, which doesn't stretch out like the polyester ones, and takes quite a lot of work and wear, and stank, to get even an inkling of funky smell.  Not to mention, like any other merino product, it dries in a flash, which is awesome for those days when you occasionally have to keep your buff over your entire face and it starts to get moist from your breath.  Even when it's a bit damp, it's merino material still keeps you warm.  I'd definitely recommend this to any outdoors peeps, particularly hunters.

This deep penetrating treatment conditioner is very thick. According to the instructions, for maximum benefit apply to entire hair after shampoo and then cover the hair with a plastic cap and go under a warm dryer for 5-10 minutes.  Since I have very fine hair, I did not want to overcondition and risk my hair going flat. Therefore, I applied a generous amount from root to end and since I was taking a hot shower, I rinsed off my hair about 6 minutes later and let the hot shower and it's steam provide the "heat" treatment.My hair was extremely easy to comb in the shower with this treatment on and the fragrance of the conditioner was divine - like a fresh field of flowers. I let my hair air dry and was able to brush it easier than normal, it felt silky, and was bouncy and not flat.I would definitely buy this or theirMotions Naturally You, Deep Conditioning Masque, 8 Ounceas both are excellent on my fine hair. You could even say this one is slightly better than their other product because it does not sting if it gets into the eyes.I love it!

Great! A small, sharp knife, combination file and small screwdriver, tweezer, toothpick, and philips screwdriver/bottle opener combo tool. All in one package that can go on your keyring.The question is, should you get the Rally, or the Classic, which is the identical pocket knife, but with a small scissor instead of the combination small philips head screwdriver / bottle opener?It's a matter of which you get the most use out of; I have both but prefer the Rally for the bottle opener. Most people have the Classic, perhaps because the Rally is newer and isn't as well-known.Have a look at the classic before you decide; it is on the Amazon website for the same price.

This is a very good zinc oxide type sunscreen. It goes on smoothly and rubs onto the skin quickly. I have looked over the active and inactive ingredients on the box and was unable to see any of the oxybenzone or retinyl palmitate that has raised concerns with some consumer groups. I know it is listed on the Amazon description of ingredients, but I could not find it listed on the box I received. If you know you do not like zinc oxide type sunscreens this may not be the product for you. I choose this type to limit my childrens exposure to the chemicals in other sunscreens. I would recomend this to others.

I owned the old Disc doctor (green wheel) that came out i don't even know how many years ago and used that thing up until now. it basically would not work anymore since i'm pretty sure between me and my mom over the years we used it on waaay more than the 50 recommended discs-probably 200+ discs. anyway, this will be a comparison review between the old one and new updated one.what prompted me to purchase a new device was that i was playing guitar hero 5 at my cousins and i accidentaly knocked the xbox over while playing. if any of you own an xbox you know that this is the last thing you want to do! not only does your xbox slam into the floor, but you get a HUGE circular gash all the way around the disc from the spinning motion scraping on something inside the xbox. :( this made the game unplayable. so i looked online for a new replacement wheel. the replacement wheel kit is something like 12 bucks and it made sense to just buy a brand new one for 16 bucks. what i ended up getting in the mail is essentially the same as my old one but with a few suprising updates. for starters, you don't need to flip the switch to rotate the disc in reverse like you did with the old one and gone is the snappy part that the disc sits into which was annoying to attach every time. now it rotates in only one direction and you simply slide the arm out, put the disc in the device and start turning. when your done you just fold the arm back into the device and your done. very ingenious. also no more plastic baggies to hold the towels, buffer pad and fluid since the device can hold all of those things in the handle.now actually using the device on my guitar hero game: needless to say, i needed 5-6 full cycles to repair the game not the 2 they recommend for normal scratch repairing. it didn't look like it had done anything but sure enough the game works brand new again! it paid for itself in one repair.In conclusion, there is no way you can go wrong dropping 16 for a device like this when nothing else besides buying a new disc will do. don't throw that game out, repair it. i actually got a free copy of halo 3 this way when my buddy's copy stopped working. i just gave it a spin in the disc dr and good as new!

This product is an external speaker -- actually, three good-sized speakers, positioned on three sides of a 9-inch cube -- that incorporates wireless Bluetooth technology so that you can, per the User Guide, "Operate your Bluetooth device and its sound will be heard through this unit's speakers." (It also has an AUX jack (cable not included) to play audio from non-Bluetooth devices.) It operates on AC only (power cord included) and has a USB port (cable not included) to charge a device.Functions are accessible via retro-styled knobs on the front and/or a tiny wireless remote that has very good range but not-so-good sensitivity (I have to press the buttons quite deliberately, and often more than once, and there's a delay in response). The cube has great rubbery pads on the bottom corners to prevent it from sliding, and a handle on the top implies portability -- but keep in mind that this unit is not weatherproof and has no battery power.I've listened to music, podcasts and audiobooks and found the volume range to be very good (from quiet to window-vibrating loud when the unit's max volume is combined with a device's max volume). The sound quality is okay -- the music sometimes pop and skips, which never happens with my device alone or through earphones, and I'm disappointed that each adjustment of bass/treble involves a complicated, multi-step procedure. Most discouraging is the troublesome Bluetooth connection -- pairing is easy, but over dozens of listening sessions with my iPhone and the speaker unit in a range of proximities (mostly close and always in a direct line), the Bluetooth connection was lost in *every* session (after as little as three minutes to as long as 45 minutes) ... the speaker audio just stopped while the iPhone played on silently. My phone and the unit were obviously paired, but I finally re-paired them anyway and the connection problem hasn't recurred but the popping/skipping has.I'm enthusiastic about this product in concept but much less enthusiastic about it in operation.

I've had the Zune 120gb since February of this year. I was debating on this and the iPod Classic 120gb back and forth and finally came to the conclusion of the Zune 120gb because I wanted to try something new and the features on the Zune beat the iPod Classic...Upon first opening the box you have the:1. Zune 120gb device.2. Earphones.3. Sync/Charge USB Cable4. 14 day free trial of Zune Pass.5. Manual and other documents.This is from memory since I lost the box, so If I left out anything I'm sorry...The software disc is "NOT" included with the Zune this is the reason why the box is so small. You need to download the Zune software off of the internet on [...]. When the software is installing it'll ask you to plug in your Zune to update it and handle the rest. Just leave the computer alone and let it do it's thing. The software once fully loaded is very stylish and smooth. Sending media to the Zune is a breeze. It takes some time to get used to it but it's pretty simple.Zune Pass is a service in which you par $15 a month for unlimited music downloads. You can listen to the music over and over until that month is over or unless you continue to renew the subscription until you stop renewing it. After every month you can keep 10 of the songs you downloaded. I personally don't use this service since I'm an audiophile and I buy the actual CD's and rip them into WMA Lossless format to be sent to my Zune 120gb. When you purchase a song from the marketplace they are 320kbps in bitrate quality. Songs acquired from the Zune Pass subscription service are in 192kbps. WMA Lossless songs usually average 800kbps bitrate quality so yea you are losing quality when downloading from the marketplace. But most people don't buy expensive earphones like me and use the bundled earphones that comes with the device so they won't notice the difference.Ok I will go into different categories and rate the Zune in it....Navigation [ 5 out of 5 ]:The touchpad squircle is a very nice addition to the newer Zune's. It gives you that touch screen feeling without the fingerprints all over your screen. Navigating through the menu on the Zune is very straight forward and easy. The Menu consists of:MusicVideosPicturesSocialRadioMarketplaceGamesPodcastsSettingsClicking on Music brings you to your music collection in which you can search for songs by songs, artists, genres, albums and playlists. Clicking on Videos will bring you to your video collection in which you can't specifically search for your videos. The videos are just listed there. Clicking on Pictures will bring you to your picture collection in which you can search by folder or by date in which the pictures were uploaded to the device. You can save a picture as the background wallpaper in the menu by clicking the picture and while viewing the picture click the middle button on the directional pad and select "Set As Background". Clicking on Social will bring you to an area where you can search for nearby Zune users in your vicinity who has their wireless activated. You can also look at your friends list or check you inbox for incoming invites to share a song or picture. To take full advanatage of Social you need to enable the WiFi chip on your Zune by going into Settings. This reduces battery life so use only when needed! Clicking on Radio brings you to the radio tuner in which you can select different stations. Clicking on Marketplace brings you to the Marketplace to download more songs but you need the WiFi chip to be enabled along with a wireless router with open access in your vicinity. Clicking on Games brings up a list of games you have installed on the device in which you can either launch or delete. Clicking on podcasts brings up a list of podcasts you have on the device. You can switch between Video or Audio podcasts. Clicking of Settings brings up a list of options you can change to suit your needs better.Sound Quality [ 4 out of 5 ]:The sound is very clean and crisp. It sounds better than a iPod Classic. The sound coming from the Zune 120gb is also suprisingly nuetral with no noticeable emphasis in any part of the frequency spectrum. It's a nice balanced sound. The soundstage is very open and clear. The radio has a clean reception of sound with no extreme hissing or static. The Zune's amp hisses when you are using sensitive earphones. I personally use the Shure SE530's which cost about $300 on Amazon. Wonderful earphones. I even did a review on it go check it out when you're done reading this! What I mean by the hissing is when there is no music playing or quiet passages of the music are going by you will hear a faint ssssssssssss noise that sounds like a calming ocean sound. You won't hear hissing with the bundled earbuds because they are not sensitive. There is no type of sound enhancement or sound editing option the Zune 120gb. Those options eat up battery life and the Zune 120gb battery life isn't the best to begin with. The older Zune 30gb has simple EQ presets. The Zune 120gb as well as the Zune 80, 16, 8, and 4gb share the same DAC or digital to analog converter which was built from the ground up with Microsoft telling Wolfson what to add and what to leave out.Screen Quality [ 4 out of 5 ]:The screen is made out of glass so it is scratch proof since glass is pretty much harder than nearly everything in your house except if you own diamonds. The screen is a nice 3.2" screen which is .7" bigger than the iPod Classic's 2.5" screen. Also this Zune has a 18 bit 262,144 color screen at a screen resolution of 320x240 pixels. The iPod Classic has less colors per pixel but shares the same resolution so images will look sharper on the iPod's smaller screen but more washed out. When viewing videos on the Zune 120gb you will need to flip the device horizontally to watch it in landscape mode. The iPod Classic doesn't require this but I personally think it's a eye sore watching videos on a 2.5" screen. Since February I have yet to see a scratch on the screen of my Zune 120gb. They're scratches on the faceplate of the device though since that is made of plastic.Aesthetics / Looks [ 5 out of 5 ]:This is a very sleek and sexy device. This is a major improvement from the original Zune 30gb that was literally a brick with buttons and a screen. The Zune 120gb comes in a glossy shiny black or a glossy red if you order it from [...]. You can buy the zune originals and have custom artwork get engraved on the back of the Zune with any text message you would like. The buttons are placed very well on the device. The back button is on the left of the device. The squircle touchpad is in the middle of the device and it also is a 5 button direction pad. The play/pause button is on the right of the device. The hold switch is on the top left of the device. The headphone out is at the top right of the device and the USB input is at the bottom middle of the device.Battery Life [ 3 out of 5 ]:It's big improvement over the Zune 30gb. The battery for me lasts about 13 hours on audio alone. I personally use lossless files which eat the battery up much more quickly them lower quality audio files. If you are using regular audio files and don't fiddle with the device much when you're outside you should expect around a 18 hour batter life or more. The iPod Classic has a whopping 33 hour battery life but this is not the case in a real life scenario. You may just get about 24 hours or less out of the iPod Classic from a real life user with real life settings.Compatibility / File Support [ 3 out of 5 ]:The Zune 120gb cannot be used with no other software but the Zune software. So you cannot drag and drop files onto the device straight from "My Computer" or windows explorer. The USB connector on the device is proprietary so if you lose or damage the cable you will need to pay $20 to buy another cable since you can't use any USB cable you may have laying about in your house. With 120gb's of storage on the Zune it can't be used as a external hard drive to store documents or back ups. iPod Classic supports this feature. There is a way around this but I dont know too much about it, sorry.The Audio formats the Zune 120gb supports:Windows Media Audio Standard[3]  (WMA) (.wma): Up to 320 Kbps; constant bit rate (CBR) and variable bit rate (VBR) up to 48 kHz sample rate. WMA Pro 2-channel up to 384 Kbps; CBR and VBR up to 48 kHz.WMA LosslessAdvanced Audio Coding (AAC) (.mp4, .m4a, .m4b, .mov): .m4a and .m4b files without FairPlay DRM up to 320 Kbps; CBR and VBR up to 48 kHz.MP3 (.mp3): up to 320 Kbps; CBR and VBR up to 48 kHz.The Video formats the Zune 120gb supports:Windows Media Video (WMV) (.wmv): Main and Simple Profile, CBR or VBR, up to 3.0 Mbps peak video bit rate; 720 pixels x 480 pixels up to 30 frames per second (or 720 pixels x 576 pixels up to 25 frames per second). Zune software will transcode HD WMV files at device sync.MPEG-4 (MP4/M4V) (.mp4) Part 2 video[4]: Simple Profile up to 2.5 Mbps peak video bit rate; 720 pixels x 480 pixels up to 30 frames per second (or 720 pixels x 576 pixels up to 25 frames per second).  Zune software will transcode HD MPEG-4 files at device sync.H.264 video[4]: Baseline profile up to 2.5 Mbps peak video bit rate; 720 pixels x 480 pixels up to 30 frames per second (or 720 pixels x 576 pixels up to 25 frames per second). Zune software will transcode HD H.264 files at device sync.DVR-MS[5]: Zune software will transcode at device sync.Miscellaneous information [ No rating ]:Capacity:120 gigabytes (GB)Up to 30,000 songs, or 25,000 pictures, or 375 hours of videoDevice Software Features:Buy from FMZune MarketplaceChannels refresh from the InternetPreloaded gamesCompatibile with Audible and Overdrive audiobooksClockScreen lockSize and Weight:Size: 61.1 mm x 108.2 mm x 12.9 mm (w x h x d)Weight: 4.5 ounces (128 grams)Battery:Music, up to 30 hours (wireless off); video, up to 4 hoursCharge time: 3 hours; 2 hours to 90 percentScreen:Size: 3.2-inch color displayType: Scratch-resistant glassOrientation: Vertical (music) and horizontal (pictures and videos)Resolution: 320 pixels x 240 pixels18-bit color (262,144 colors)Wireless:Connectivity: 802.11b/g compatibleRange: Up to 30 feetIn range, see other Zune users, see their "now playing" status (when enabled on a given Zune), and send and receive songs and pictures.Wireless Sync:YesConnectivity: 802.11b/g compatibleAuthentication modes: Open, WEP, WPA, and WPA2Encryption modes: WEP 64- and 128-bit, TKIP, and AESVideo Output:Yes (composite-video through headphone jack; composite- or component-video through the Zune Dock, sold separately)FM Radio:Built in with RDS/RBDSPorts:USB Connector portHeadphone jack / AV outputLanguages:English, French, SpanishValue [ 5 out of 5 ]:For $250 you get a great sounding all around player with a hefty storage of 120gb's. It's very durable and sleek looking. It's money well spent! I personally recommend this device over the iPod Classic.Hope you enjoyed this review, also check out my Shure SE530 review.

I just got this last night. It is a Christmas present for my 18-month-old daughter. I set up Skype so we can talk to grandma, downloaded a bunch of videos, and installed a bunch of toddler games and kiddie music apps. If you have used a newer Android phone, you will be very familiar with this interface because it is almost identical and it was very easy to set up. All of my setup work went off without a hitch and it never crashed once (surprising for an Android)! As an added bonus, they included a screen protector, so they must have been paying attention to other reviewers who said that it needed one!For all intents, this will be a perfectly functional tablet for my toddler and because of the low price I won't have to worry if it gets damaged. I would have given it four stars but there are a few problems that I find worth mentioning, and they were enough to knock off a star:1. Internal memory is 1GB, NOT 4GB as stated in the product details. Thankfully, Apps2SD works on this tablet without rooting, and I already had a 32GB micro SD card from an old phone that mounted with no problem in this tablet, so I shouldn't run into much trouble with the limited onboard memory.2. Charger stopped working the first night. There are two ways to charge this tablet - through the DC charger port and through the micro USB port. I was originally charging through the DC port and for some reason it just stopped charging. I unplugged and re-plugged the charger but it still wouldn't charge. I can charge through the USB with no problem but it is something worth mentioning.3. Strange angle on the front facing camera. While testing Skype I noticed that the front-facing camera is angled up. That will be a slight inconvenience because one would typically hold this tablet on his/her lap and tilt it up to use the front camera. If you do that on this tablet, the camera will be aimed at your forehead, so it must be slightly angled downward to get a good angle with that camera. This is not an issue of my height because I am 6' tall.4. Poor viewing at an angle. The picture quality drops off rapidly as you tilt or turn the screen, so one must keep it close to perpendicular with ones eyes to get a good picture.I will come back and update if I find anything else worth mentioning, but so far it is a "just ok" tablet that will work for its intended purpose.

Review for:  Plantronics SupraPlus Telephone Headset (HW261N binaural and HW251N monaural)OVERVIEW:-----------Here below we compare and discuss the 2 highest-end (sound quality especially) Plantronics wired headsets with noise reduction, SupraPlus vs. EncorePro.  Let's begin by noting which models are monaural vs. binaural:SupraPlus HW251N - Monaural (1 earmuff), microphone noise reducingSupraPlus HW261N - Binaural (2 earmuff), microphone noise reducingEncorePro HW291N - Monaural (1 earmuff), microphone noise reducingEncorePro HW301N - Binaural (2 earmuff), microphone noise reducingThe W in the model names stands for WIDEBAND AUDIO for which Plantronics claims improvement in sound clarity, etc., however support says you likely wouldn't detect any difference with or without it with wired headsets.  That said, these two headphones are considered to have the BEST SOUND QUALITY OF THE PLANTRONICS LINES WITH NOISE-CANCELING.The N in the model names stands for NOISE-CANCELING technology which I will refer to here mostly as NOISE REDUCTION, that is the noise that comes to the microphone.SIMILARITIES:--------------(1) NOISE REDUCTIONThis is reducing the background noise that comes into the microphone.Plantronics measures microphone noise reduction between 30%-75% for both headsets, so there is no difference there.  After testing, however, I think it is much closer to 30%-40% and Plantronics support agreed.As I had heard the noise reduction may be higher for one of the two headsets I did a test and if there is a difference I couldn't tell.  I was unable to get frequency distribution vs. noise reduction information, thus I can't say if there is a difference in handling low or high pitched noises.  Support says they use the same noise reduction system.Please note that this noise reduction will NOT SILENCE NOR EVEN SEVERELY DROP THE VOLUME of human voices around you.  Talking near the headset can certainly still be heard, just at a reduced level.  Claims made that conference participants magically can no longer hear loud people around a reviewer and his/her headset should be taken with suspicion.  I've tested noises out to around 8' away from the headset.  Home office users will want physical separation from household noise and not want to entirely depend on microphone noise reduction.See below in the section "How To Get More Noise Reduction" for how to do just that from these headsets.Calling the headsets "noise-canceling" is perhaps a bit misleading, but to be fair that is the generally accepted name of the technology that does the noise reduction.DIFFERENCES:--------------So other than number of earmuffs, what are some of the other less obvious differences?(1) ADJUSTMENT OF MICROPHONE POSITIONThe EncorePro (HW301N/HW291N) is easier for microphone positioning, however the SupraPlus boom readily bends to accomplish much of the same thing.The EncorePro microphone boom nicely pivots independently of earmuff angle.  For perfectionists or particularly large faces, the microphone tip also BOOMS IN AND OUT only with the EncorePro, though I have never used that feature.So, although the SupraPlus boom can be bent to accomplish a similar positioning, the EncorePro positioning method is superior in ease and exact positioning.(2) COMFORT AND FIT/FINISHSo other than microphone positioning, what else can we say about comfort and fit/finish differences?The EncorePro (HW301N/HW291N) has high quality leatherette earmuffs, while the SupraPlus has perfectly fine simple foam earmuffs.  Most users trying both told me the EncorePro earmuffs were marginally more comfortable at the start.The EncorePro is a 10% LIGHTER headset (2.5 vs. 2.8 ounces).  It has more aluminum and superlight nylon in the build compared to the SupraPlus.Though initially everyone found the EncorePro SLIGHTLY MORE COMFORTABLE than the SupraPlus, as time passed some folks said the EncorePro felt too WOBBLY/INSECURE on the head and switched to SupraPlus.  Thus while some might prefer the EncorePro fit, I'm giving the SupraPlus the edge for comfort.I should note that the combination of the leatherette earmuffs and overall fit/finish of the EncorePro LOOKS HIGHER-END.  That said, the SupraPlus doesn't look bad at all, just plain Jane compared to the EncorePro.Notes:- You can get LEATHERETTE EARMUFFS SEPARATELY FOR THE SupraPlus (HW251N/HW261N).  These are NOT as high quality as those that come with the EncorePro, but folks who tried them out for me marginally prefer them over the foam.  It takes a few minutes and some dexterity to replace the foam ones (tested) with one of these fairly inexpensive ear cushions.Accessories Ear Cushions, Tips & Loops 67712-01 - Leatherette Cushion(3) DURABILITYThe SupraPlus is supposed to be sturdier/tougher than the EncorePro, more durable, designed to handle the rotation of call center personnel and the occasional drop more easily than the EncorePro.CONS FOR BOTH HEADSETS:---------------------------(1) NO VOICE TUBE OPTIONWe can't use voice tubes with these two headsets.  If you are in a quiet environment you may sound clearer with a headset with a voice tube.  However, in all likelihood this is not an issue for someone paying extra to get a headset with noise reduction.(2) NO OPTION TO TAKE CALLWITHOUT PICKING UP HANDSETPlantronics support confirmed this.  If you found a solution for this, like perhaps found a phone that allows this, please describe in comments.HOW TO GET MORE NOISE REDUCTION!:--------------------------------------You can get MORE NOISE REDUCTION by pointing the hole on the outer side of the microphone, that is the side of the microphone not pointing directly away from your mouth, more in the direction of the likeliest source of noise, like towards an office door or where kids might come in.  For example, when choosing the side of your mouth the microphone is on you can take this into account.SUPRAPLUS IS A WORKHORSE:-----------------------------Millions of the SupraPlus headsets, both in its present incarnation and previous versions have been in commercial use such as in call centers worldwide for something like 10-15 years.  It is a proven workhorse.  The EncorePlus tries to one up it a notch, however the SupraPlus will likely meet most of your wired headset needs, too.WHY WIRED?:-------------The four classic reasons to go wired are:- Eliminate possibility of interference.  For example wireless routers, wireless printers, etc. can cause static or choppiness with Dect 6.0 wireless headsets (source: support).- Wired is always better sound quality than wireless all else being the same, however as time marches forward folks have a tougher time telling the qualitative difference between top quality wired and wireless headsets (source: support).- Cost savings at the higher quality end.- Some folks don't want to essentially strap a tiny cordless phone an inch from their head all day.Having said that, lots of folks use high-end wireless headsets and you might consider that before committing to these wired ones.  However, I would suggest not going cheap if you commit to wireless, the difference in quality can be considerable.  If you have the funds you can also, of course, consider wired for most use and wireless for the occasional need.VISTA M22 AMPLIFIER ANDITS POWER ADAPTER:--------------------------Officially the Plantronics Vista M22 amplifier is REQUIRED to use these high-end headsets.Some high-end telephones have built-in headset amps (Cisco IP Phone 7942, for example) so you might test if you really need an amp.  Keep in mind, though, that such phones likely don't have microphone adjustment like the M22 does.The M22 amplifier in addition to being a headset amplifier also offers precision setting in microphone and earpiece sound volume.  The microphone sound adjustment after being made is hidden away behind a case wall so NO ONE CAN ACCIDENTALLY ALTER IT.  This is nice as you are unlikely to know for sometime that it has been altered.The earpiece volume knob on the M22 is on the outside, large, numbered with a lot of setting granularity, and very easy to read.  This makes adjustment on-the-fly as needed easier than fumbling for an inline volume control knob.The M22 also has a LOUD NOISE PROTECTOR that lowers volume in case of very loud noise.Be aware that the M22 DOESN'T INCLUDE AN AC POWER ADAPTER, it comes with batteries, however you will probably want the power adapter.-Plantronics Vista M22 Amplifier-Plantronics Ac Adapter For M10, M12, M22, S10, T20 (pl-45671-01) -SUGGESTIONS:--------------- Position the microphone approximately a finger's width from the corner of the mouth.NOTES:-------- There are no wired nor wireless Plantronics headsets with better noise reduction (source: support).CONCLUSION:-------------Plantronics positions these headsets as having the best sound quality of the their models with noise reduction.The EncorePro offers no advantage in sound quality nor noise reduction over the SupraPlus, however the few folks who tried them with me felt the EncorePro was incrementally MORE COMFORTABLE at the start, though most felt over time that it was too WOBBLY/INSECURE on the head and switched to SupraPlus.Plantronics positions the call center workhorse SupraPlus as more DURABLE.The EncorePro is considered MORE ATTRACTIVE, you be the judge.You are usually, but not always, REQUIRED to use a M22 AMPLIFIER with either headset, and for that you would likely want to buy the AC POWER ADAPTER for it.  See above for related product links.  The M22 amplifier also allows you to prevent the microphone volume from being altered by accident when you configure it by hiding its control behind a panel.

We got this for use during camping, but knew this compactable lantern would also be great for power outages.  I wish I could give this unit four stars, but let us say 3.5 is a good measure.First, in terms of overall performance, when the lantern's battery is fresh, it throws a healthy amount of light, smoothly and consistantly bright, in all directions.  It would be perfect for what we thought it would be perfect for... except that the battery that came with it lasted as long as a campfire song.  Then we'd turn it on, and it would turn off within a couple of seconds.  We are on our third set of AA batteries and we know that the battery life is not all that long because we have a four year-old son who loves this lantern.  He always wants to take it to bed with him, so a true guesstime-ation of battery life is difficult for me to convey.  I just know the batch that came with the lantern didn't have much gusto... but I suppose that is to be expected with starter batteries.I really like how the lantern collapses into itself.  It is just a little box when the light section is pushed into the case, and very portable, easy to store in a junk drawer.The handle is a little flimsy, but it works for such a lightweight lantern.  It also allows you to hang the lantern wherever you may.I do like this lantern, and wish I could have given it another 1/2 star.  For $20, it's not a bad price, but I also think it is worth the time to look around for alternative battery-powered lanterns.

I'll start with the conclusion : this is a well built product that does what it is supposed to. It has some limitations, and the battery is rather small. It's also large and expensive compared to other available batteries.This is a simple charger with 3 ports - a Lightning plug for Apple devices that uses it, a microSD plug for those devices that uses it, and a microSD port at the bottom for recharging. All connectors and ports are protected from dust and lint. There is also a button at the bottom that will tell you how much charge is in the device by the color of the LED - green, orange, and red in decreasing level. Blinking red means practically empty.Each port supplies up to 1A, for a total combined output of 1.5A. You can recharge the battery while charging your phone - it doesn't make it faster, but it makes it more convenient - plug everything in at night before you go to sleep, and wake up with a fully recharged phone and battery.The 2000 mAh battery is not quite enough to recharge my phone (1750 mAh battery). This is to be expected, as there is a fair amount of loss from voltage conversions. It will bring my phone from 0% to about 80% though, which is good enough in an emergency. Forget about recharging a tablet with this.Pros :Built-in Lightning plug for those with newer iOS devices. Note that you need to press the button at the bottom to activate the charging if you're using the Lightning plug.Cons :Lack of a generic port. You cannot use this with your own cable. Mini USB? Out of luck.Limit of 1A even when only using one port.If you're not using the Lightning plug, it doesn't make sense to get this, as you'll be paying extra for it. This is rather expensive for a 2000 mAh battery.Physically, it is very big for a 2000 mAh battery. It's not heavy, but the physical size makes it uncomfortable to just stuff into your pocket.Plastic silver finish is not exactly my favorite color scheme, but my main complain is that it scratches easily.Other notes :The carabiner clip actually comes in useful - I clip it to all sorts of places, like the belt loops on my jeans, when my hands are full. Of course I have to remember to remove them, or I'll look rather silly walking around with a battery hooked to my jeans.

My son volunteered to test a variety of new body & hair products designed for males.  As an older teen, he is pretty particular about the products he uses.  His review follows:Personally, I thought that this Axe product made my hair look better and healthier.As someone who had (and occasionally still has) problems with dandruff, I thought that this product did exactly what it advertised: lessened the amount of embarrassing dandruff in my hair.I have absolutely no idea what the "Pyrithione Zinc" has to do with this product, other than make it sound different than the rest. Other than that, this product made styling my hair much easier than normal hair gel.However, the only downside of this styling cream is that I had to completely make sure that it was washed off of my hands after applying it- especially since it did not go away as the day went on (like some other gels may do).Despite that, overall, I liked this styling cream and would recommend it to anyone who had or still has problems with dandruff.

A free sample struck again! The people at Sweetsation are very smart indeed...everytime I order a new product, I receive samples of others that I fall in love with. This is how I first tried LumiEssence.One evening a few weeks ago I was cleaning out a cosmetic bag and found this sample. I don't even remember when I received it, but I was happy to find it and thought I would try it as I love their other products. I was a little worried when I opened it that I may have let it sit around for too long, and it had been laying on its side, so all of the product was over to one side which never makes a cream look appealing- plus I am used to my other Sweetsation facial moisturizers being white but this was golden. When I first put my finger in, it felt a little stiff. I continued to put some on my finger, and right away it softened up and I noticed the lovely yet subtle fragrance. So I decided to give it a try.Okay, I love this stuff. And I love the fact that there are skin care products that FEEL SO GOOD, SMELL SO GOOD, HELP MY SKIN, and are TOTALLY SAFE for me, and while I am pregnant - in case that happens again. :-) But back to my review.LumiEssence was the perfect consistency for me to use alone as a night cream before bed. I have really sensitive, fairly dry skin and I usually use Aqua Tica, which I also love. Because it was a sample, the container didn't have any claims on it, so I didn't realize it was supposed to brighten and lighten spots the way it does. I planned to go to their website and read about it, but I didn't get around to that before I noticed a difference in my skin!I am almost 43. I have pretty nice skin for my age, but I recently got some sun spots on my cheeks from a road trip...I didn't put sunscreen on late in the day when the sun was setting, and that seems to have been what got my cheeks. This is the first time I have noticed the sun doing this to me...my upper cheeks have dark spots, like merged freckles. ACK! A bit of sun exposure that used to be a nice sun glow, now just makes my skin look old and damaged, I am unhappy to say.Well, after only using this for a few days, I already noticed a lightening of those spots! They didn't go away, for sure. But they are softer looking, less hard edged, and lighter. And my skin in general looks "brighter". Most days I use the SunSiBelle tinted sunscreen in lieu of makeup, as it evens out my skin (covers those sun spots!) and makes it look fresh and younger, while also providing sun protection. (I use a natural powder product by Coastal Classic Creations for an under eye concealer and powder with the Tinted SunSiBelle).  But one day I didn't bother to put the tinted sibelle or powder on, and I was pleased to notice that my skin looked better naked than it has in a LONG time!!!I have been using this nightly since, and my skin has continued to look better and better. I just ordered a full size and hope it arrives before my sample runs out!Oh, if you have made it this far in my lengthy review, I also want to mention something about their packaging. I have read other reviews that criticize their packaging for not looking professional enough or something like that. Well, I think their packaging is pretty, though not as upscale as the quality of the products warrants. The plastic containers don't seem as expensive as some other products. But the packages work well, and the pumps ensure that almost no product is wasted. I'd rather be able to AFFORD such amazing skin care products than have the packaging so high-end that the price makes them unattainable for my budget. Plus, they are made in the USA, and created by a Momma. Gotta love that!UPDATE AUGUST 20, 2013:I hadn't been using this for a while as I ran out and for some crazy reason, just didn't bother to order any more for a few months. In that time I have moved to New Mexico which as you probably know is VERY SUNNY EVERY DAY!!! Even though I use 30+ sunscreen religiously, I had started to get some dark spotting on my cheeks and forehead. Well, I finally ordered more LumiEssence and it arrived yesterday. I CAN'T EVEN BELIEVE IT, but after using it last night, this morning and this evening (a few hours ago) I ALREADY SEE A REDUCTION IN THE DARKNESS! There was more of a defined transition visible on my skin from the dark areas to the lighter, and that is actually reduced. I can't even believe it. I just had to make note here. At least for me, this stuff is AMAZING. Thank you, Sweetsation Therapy!

I'm a road biking enthusiast and while I know there's benefit to training with tires not quite up to max pressure, sometimes it is just nice to cut your rolling resistance even if it makes the ride a bit hard on the butt. Trying to attain 120 psi with a hand pump is not fun even if you tell yourself you're getting good chest and shoulder exercise! A good thing to remember is that a CO2 cartridge costs about a buck... I'll pump until I can't pump without straining.I purchased this to carry in addition to a hand pump after doing a bunch of research and while this isn't the top of the line inflator, I thought I'd try it out. It has the possibility of using both 12- and 16-gram, threaded or unthreaded cartridges and having a small pouch to keep it all together in my backpack (on long rides) appealed to me. The fact that it included a patch kit and tire tools was just a bonus. If I find I like it enough, I'll go with the more expensive version if the features merit the extra cost sometime in the future.I tried it out yesterday, topping off both tires before my ride. Frankly, overall, it was an easy, nearly effortless task that was instantly noticeable in pedaling ease. Looking down at my tires while mounted on the bike, I could see that inflation was much better from the much reduced broadening of the tires at the road contact points.My only complaints about this item are:(1) The instructions are difficult to read due to the size of print and the fact that they opted to print not on a white background (although the paper is white), but shaded the background grey! Don't do that; why not print on the white paper without the background?(2) Attaching the inflator to the valve: while the instructions provided in Spanish, Italian, French and German obviously give guidance for use with either Schrader or Presta valves, the English instructions don't mention either valve type, simply suggesting that one "screw the nozzle fully onto the valve stem". This was not difficult but it was hard to tell if the inflator was fully attached. For the Presta, you don't need the Presta adaptor to attach the inflator. Nice to know. I'm fortunate to be able to read all the "foreign language" instructions except the Italian, but most Americans are lucky to read only English (and many are challenged to do that!). Why not include the same information in English as you do in French, Italian, Spanish and German? The only place that the monolinguistic English-speaking user will find out about the valves is in the Quick Start instructions where step 2 shows that one simply presses the tool onto the Presta type valve but screws it onto the Schrader!(2a) I just discovered two more errors in the instructions: reading the French instructions, when one gets to the part about the Schrader vs. Presta valves, one needs to be able to read and understand German... someone forgot to change the instructions to French! In order to get the full instructions in French, one needs to go back to the valve directions in the Italian section and voila! there they are in French! Guess it's the Italians who are screwed! (Of course, most Italians probably speak another language so we "silly English types" are the ones who are truly screwed! Apologies to Monty Python fans!)(3) The instructions talk about a RED safety lever... there is not a single bit of red plastic or any other material on this tool! It's bright green, just like the inflation trigger. There are two choices here and I'd recommend the first: actually go to the trouble of making the unit with a red safety lever, or change the instructions.I have just one suggestion so far (beyond those listed above):It's fortunate for me that I don't have to use a pressure gauge to determine that I have 120 psi. I've pumped enough tires. But for those that need to have that confirmation, it would be really excellent to have a pressure gauge built into the nozzle so that one doesn't have to switch between inflator and gauge until the correct pressure is achieved. Could be done for about $10 price increase for a reasonably accurate sensor.I can't promise that I'll update this, especially if it works as advertised. If, however, I find something disappointing, I'll be sure to follow up appropriately and post another message.Happy riding!

(This is a combined review for both the wide-angle and fisheye attachments)If you have the 16mm NEX pancake, you should have the wide-angle and the fish-eye converter.  The .75x wide-angle really does make a difference. The fish-eye is even wider than the wide-angle... and you can also play with your creative side with the curved fish-eye effect. These attachments are very practical in actual use. If I have the 16mm on, I'd usually either have the wide-angle or the fisheye lens on as well. If I don't want to use it, I simply take it off and hold it in my left hand, take the photo, and snap it back on. It's very easy.  Image quality is good though you won't get that tack-sharp image. Not with the pancake and this attachment. But it's great for what it is and the real genius is that these are designed to be used with the 16mm, which cuts down on the bulk of having all three as separate lenses. And if these were all separate lenses, I'd probably never buy them all. However, for about $100 for each of the attachments, why not? It's a bargain. If buying only one, then I'd probably pick the wide-angle over fish-eye b/c it's more practical for more shots... however that said, the fisheye literally changes the shape of the photo and gives your photo a cool effect while the wide-angle makes the pancake more, well, wide-angle. Up to you. I also like that the fisheye's curve effect is more mild than typical fisheye lens, which makes it more practical to use in more shots.There are a few annoyances:- The attachments are a bit big and heavy *relatively*.  Attaching it essentially doubles the 16mm pancake size and weight and it's like carrying the 18-55 lens.- You can't attach a filter. Also when you attach it to the 16mm, you must take off the filter from that lens. I have to take off the UV filter and it's annoying.- The front and rear caps are not the standard caps but are more like soft plastic lids. The front cap is especially prone to falling off.- Comes with a nice case, which I suppose is a good thing, however it adds to the bulk and weight when you carry the lens around. Of course you can leave this at home.- Not image stabilized. This a limitation of the 16mm lens, not this attachment, however, since the attachment can be used only on the 16mm, it is impacted. Can't have it all I guess.
