This is a good battery charger. It's not perfect though, and is costs quite a bit compared to its competition, but it just might be worth it.Note that this review is for the 7200 version. There are smaller versions that are cheaper that might be more appropriate. This one, however, is pretty full-featured, and includes some VERY handy options cheaper versions don't (the 13.6V Supply mode, and the 16V Boost mode).The 13.6V Supply mode is especially handy if you have a battery that has been drained until the voltage is below what this charger (or others) can recognize and charge. You hook up the constant 13.6V supply and the battery will get enough of a charge that the charger can recognize it and apply its magic. It would be nice if it could do this automatically, but this "genius" charger ain't that smart. It's a manual mode. I had a bunch of batteries that gave "error" messages until given a bit of a charge with this manual mode. The Supply mode can also make this charger work as a constant 12V (actually 13.6V) power supply, for powering anything that draws 5A or less. Nice to have that feature.The 16V boost mode is for heavily sulphated batteries that won't hold a charge well anymore. It's also a manual mode, but has the ability to recondition batteries and bring back some of their capacity. Not many other chargers offer that option (theBatteryMINDer Model# 12248does, but it costs more and doesn't do as much).The 7.2A charging current is fast enough to charge pretty much any drained battery in a reasonable amount of time (overnight). While it's a decent current, it's not the 50A (or even 10A) that somefast chargerscan deliver, so there are faster chargers. It is also safe to leave on, as it goes into maintenance mode and just keeps them topped off as needed. Most other battery maintainers (like theBattery Tender, which is excellent), have a much slower charging rate, and if a battery is actually drained, could take days to charge with these (which isn't what they're designed for, but it's nice to have a charger that can handle that).This charger handles both 12V and 24V. Nice if you have any 24V batteries (most of us don't). If you don't, but have any smaller 6V batteries, it would likely be worth getting one of NOCO's 6V/12V battery chargers (like theNOCO Genius G3500) instead. The added plus there is that they can also handle smaller capacity batteries. The 7200 shouldn't be used with batteries under 14Ah. I have a lot of small (1.2Ah-12Ah) 12V SLA batteries that require a different charger than this one, for instance).I do wish the connectors used were Anderson PowerPoles, since I've standardized all of my other 12V gear with those, but I can swap in those connectors on my own when needed.Anyway, this is a great charger. It costs a bit, and you can buy something cheap (but decent) like theBattery Tender 021-0123 Battery Tender Junior 12V Battery Chargeror one of NOCO's cheaper chargers if you JUST need a maintainer. If you just want a simple battery charger (that can handle large discharged batteries), there are cheaper (but less "smart") options for that as well. This is just a nice all-around, do-it-all type of battery charger though. Hard to go wrong here.

For those of you who cannot find the software---after connecting the HP harddrive to your computer, OPEN MY COMPUTER (on your computer) and click on your CD drive (it may be Drive F). There you will find the software and that you can select the types of files you want backed up---you can also select those files (after backing them up) which you may wish to retrieve.  I had, among many other files, over 74 GB of TIF photos---and the HP backed up my entire harddrive in 1-1/2 hours.  I even stopped the process to go back and make sure it had backed up those files that were important to me----then started the entire process again---and it just continued to back up my files. My computer and I are located in an area which has very frequent electrical outages, so I do not keep my computer on all the time, or even in sleep mode. I have had the HP for over a week, and just backed up my computer harddrive today on it; frankly, I hesitated because of all the complaints and mixed reviews I previously had read on this site. I am hoping that the HP will continue to back up after I close and restart my computer---since I was able to interrupt the process before, I feel secure that it shall do so.FOLLOW-UP: I now have had this backup harddrive for some time and find it invaluable! The only con I can find is that it backs up files only, and not programs, so that if I wish to read an email I already have backed up on the harddrive, I have to download it to my PC so that it is contained within Outlook Express. The reason for this is that an email cannot be opened unless it is contained in the original program from which it was taken because it must be linked with the "root" for the program.

I picked up one of these drives to replace a 500 Gig Seagate drive that came with my computer. I have a computer couple years so I just wanted to do a little refresh; I also upgraded the graphics card. I figured I'd make the computer little nicer until I can afford a replacement in another year or two but I'm beginning to think it was a mistake.The drive worked flawlessly for about a month, sadly exactly the return policy time, but since then there've been a few oddities. For one thing, the drive makes some particularly worrying clicking sounds that may be nothing but have me very worried. I say they may be nothing, because Seatools (Seagate's drive test software) says the drive is in perfect health...Of course it also says the hard drive in my other computer is running at 275C so I don't know how much I can trust its results. For some reason the Windows version of Seatools can never see any of my drives so I have to use the DOS version.  I do have experience with another one terabyte drive.  The caviar Green drive that I got for my external hard drive enclosure is much quieter and cooler.On the plus side, performance on this drive is quite good. It seems to be a lot faster than my old 500 Gig drive. I haven't noticed any slowdown or access issues other than the aforementioned odd slow motion crash issue. I'm running Windows Vista and the performance analyzer is a 5.9 rating. According to the specs I found it can take up to 60C as an operating temperature which is quite good.  If it didn't have these other problems it'd be a sweet drive.  If I can stress anything from this experience it is to ONLY pay attention to people who say they've had the drive more than a month or so.  If it's an out of the box evaluation they may end up changing their tune.It clicks loudly all the time now.  It's enough louder from when I first got it that I have lost confidence and it sure sounds like the universal "I'm dying" sounds to me.  Really I'd suggest going with Western Digital. I was much happier with the 1 TB drive I got from them.  When this one goes out I'll probably just buy one of theirs.  I've loved Seagate for a lot of years, but I can't see buying another after all this stress.  Of course, a lot of people have had bad experiences with any brand so it could just be a bad one.Update-----3 months now and the drive crashes constantly. I had been coveting this cup for years but refused to spend an excessive amount of money on a cup. However it was the perfect way to spend part of a $200 gift certificate I received.The quality is what you would expect from a $50 cup and it should a lifetime or two. The double wall keeps your drink warm for a while, not quite like a thermos but better than most cups plus it keeps you from burning your lips  and fingers like I regularly did on my single wall titanium cup. The outside of the cup gets just warm enough to warm your hands through a pair of gloves on a freezing morning at camp. This cup also fits nicely into their 700ml cup/pot.I only have 2 slightly negative things to say and one of them can't be helped. If you fill the cup to the top (much higher than what you would fill it to to drink it measures out to 400ml rather than the stated 450ml so the drinking capacity is 380ml. I know this is picky but come on, it's a $50 cup. The second thing is the double wall cup looses it's flexibility as a small pot because you can't put it over a stove. Often I would heat up water for my tea in my single wall cup and toss the bag in rather than using to pots or I would heat up coffee that I set aside and forgot to finish. Once again I know that is picky but it's something to consider.Go ahead and buy one, you will get over the guilt of owning a $50 cup eventually.

Before deciding to buy the moshi ivisor AG, I made a lot of research and read a lot of comments, thoughts and reviews about screen protector films. I am glad I chose this product, it saved me from the stress of putting on the protector film without any lint and/or bubbles.Due to the nature of the anti-glare feature, there is a subtle clarity decrease on the screen but I think it is something that can be lived with. Powersupport HD is said to have more clarity but I wouldn't want to deal with a regular film application.As the film only sticks to the screen around the bezel, when I first applied the film there was a little bit of air pocket created in the middle of the screen but I managed to get it out almost completely by lifting one edge of the film up and pushing the air out from the lifted edge. There are enough detailed reviews, pro and con, that give an accurate picture of this player. I am not going to repeat what others have said, but will explain why I think the review sample that I received is a five star entertainment center for certain folks. And to be fair, it's less than that for others - it depends on what you want from it.For me I have high end audio gear, as well as a decent HD TV, and DVD and Blu-Ray players. This falls short in comparison. But where this shines is it's portable, is actually quite capable as a TV, audio and DVD player. It has a decent screen - especially for a portable unit. In fact, the large screen is one of the reasons why I rated it five stars.I see the audience as someone who travels or has kids, or someone on a budget who wants a functional entertainment center, but is barely scraping by. Any of those will probably agree that this is wonderful.Here are some reasons why I love it: first, it can stand alone, and is even portable. I am not thrilled that the battery only lasts for two hours between charges, nor am I excited about the fact that it is not user replaceable. On the other hand, it runs forever on wall power, and will work out of the box if you plug in the wall wart to charge it.Some features are more important to me than others. I am going to use this as a portable system, so the large screen is a major plus. In fact, the resolution is so good that I have no problem reading subtitles when I am watching foreign films. And the screen swivels, which is another plus for me because when I am out and about with this thing I sometimes set it up in the darnedest places. Of course, for in car use with children who are best behaved when occupied, the swivel screen is ideal.It has interesting features like a multimedia card slot, video in and out, a channel selector and even a game port. I am not much into games, but fir fire up the games that came on the mini-disc that was bundled with this player. I didn't care for any of them, but I am not much of a gamer to begin with. I am sure some youngster would appreciate the diversion on a long trip.Some reviewers called this flimsy. I call it light. True, it is not ruggedized, but if you treat it with the same respect as you would a laptop or tablet, it will last.One thing I like is it played all of the DVDs I tried, including some problematic ones what have spots that slow down or freeze the video on my main player. It also had no problems playing back CDs I made from MP3 files on my laptop. I did not test it on the road to see if it skipped, so cannot say one way or the other how car worthy it is.Bottom line: if you are on a budget, but want an inexpensive and capable entertainment center, this is a good choice. Same goes for if you travel or want something to occupy the young ones. However, if you want a rock solid portable player that has spectacular audio and video, you may want to keep looking.

I bought this repair stand locally from the original owner and am very impressed. This is an entry level repair stand with big ambitions and compares favorably to more expensive stands. I have been considering purchase of other brands but am glad I found this one, it is very sturdy and well-built..The clamp mechanism is very simple and quite solid. When I mount my bike in the stand there is no sag at all with the clamp in the horizontal position. With the clamp in the vertical position there is just the slightest amount of sag, barely noticeable at all. Pretty impressive for an entry level stand. There is some plastic but it is very heavy and should last many more years.It does fold up and is portable, although not as easily as some of the more expensive stands. If you are going to be setting it up and breaking it down a lot, it might not be the best stand for you, you'll have to decide that. For me, that is not an issue so I am more than happy with it.One issue with some of the non-Park tripod stands seems to be that the metal is not as sturdy and the stand flexes enough under the weight of the bike that the pedals come close to hitting the upright when the rear wheel is turned. Not even close with this stand. With my bike mounted there is a good 8 inches of clearance, no chance of the pedals hitting the stand upright.After using this stand I am definitely a Park fan and will consider Park first if I need to upgrade. For now it is all I need, Thanks Park!Follow Up:I want to share a couple of simple enhancements to the PCS-9 that you might find useful. A number of buyers have noted that they wished the stand came with a quick release clamp on the stand upright used to adjust the height of the bike clamp, instead of an allen head screw that requires a tool for adjustment. Well, I went out to the garage and found a quick release seat clamp from an old bike. It fit perfect and was free, problem solved.Another thing I noticed was that the ends of the handle that tightens the main clamp are a little rough. A very simple and cheap fix is to buy a couple of bolt thread protectors and push them onto the bare ends of the clamp handle. The green ones fit perfect. Protects the hands and gives a nice finished appearance.The last thing I did was buy a clamping knob (cost about $4) to replace the allen head screw that tightens the clamp base onto the rear of the stand upright near the floor. Now, no tools are needed to set up the stand and it makes for a professional appearance.I hope you find these tips useful.

It's a hot pink for sure, but I think it brightens up my face and gives me a genuine barbie doll look.  It's a bit intimidating, but didn't look clownish on me at all. UPDATED 6/28/14I had great hopes for this product. I originally planned to use two bottles so that I could give the best review I could. I thought I would order the 2nd bottle half way through the first, however when I received the first bottle I also got a note saying they would send me a free bottle, if I write a great review of the product. I thought: Great! I wanted 2 anyway. So, almost at the end of the first bottle I attempted to order the free 2nd bottle so that I could have it prior to running out of the first bottle. This is where my discontent started. I went through about 3 very frustrating weeks (or more) with emailing the company trying to get the bottle. I wanted to use them back to back to get the best results. Needless to say I was never able to get the email information to get the product or the promised promo code. They kept sending me an email address knowing that Amazon removed it. I was extremely frustrated! Instead of just emailing me the promo code I went through this craziness. I eventually got a call from the Owner and CEO Mr. Carr who called my home and left 2 messages. He touted his product and said how wonderful it was over and over again. Instead of leaving a promo code on either of the call, I had to call him back or the other option on the second call to me was to email him. I did. Finally, I got a promo code! Well, need less to say, this was now 1 month plus after I had completed my first bottle, so to me I was back at square one where I felt using only 1 bottle and looking for great results wasn't going to happen. I actually asked him in my email if he would consider allowing me 2 to start over and get the best results. He declined, touting how great his product was.  I disagree based on my experience with the product, his staff and the owner himself. I thought it would be nothing to him to acknowledge my frustrating experience with my request but he declined saying they were soon doing away with the promo offer anyway. However from what I can see by other comments, this has been their standard. Send the product out, offer a free second bottle in exchange for a great written review of his product. He's my summation: Save your money. I do not trust any of these reviews about how great this product is. Go to your local health food store, talk to a health consultant there, and buy local.

Okay, these are louder than your laptop speakers, however they don't sound very good.  They are adequate, but overpriced considering the quality of the audio and the competition out there.This might be specific to the set I got ---> You have to push the top down to turn them on, one of the speakers I got has a crooked lid that never really looks closed.  Also, one of the speakers makes a popping noise every time you close it, it actually sounds like a pop from the speaker not from the materials rubbing or clipping.There are blue lights that light up at the base of the speakers, but they are flashing almost all the time which I find super annoying, its like a slow strobe light going off while I'm trying to pay attention to the screen, I wish they didn't exist.These speakers have batteries built in which is completely unnecessary as I will never use these not attached to USB for power, so I'm not sure why that is even a feature of these speakers considering they are sold to be used with a laptop... It just adds to the cost, and there are much better rechargeable wireless speaker options out there in this price range that sound better.I have a big laptop and I find that the cables are just barely long enough to work, it would have been nice to have an extra 6 inches of cable at least.And you cannot tell which speaker is left or right without unplugging the speakers.  The L and R is only marked on the end of the cable where it plugs into the speakers, and the label is covered when they are plugged in.  Not a big deal, but if you carry them around by the cable, or just toss them in your bag with the cables attached you have to unplug them to know which side they go on.It would have been nice to have some cool looking braided or green rubber cables to go with these as the black cabling is kinda ugly.I still use them, but I have my radar on for a different set of small speakers that sound better.  Get them used or something, the MSRP price is terrible, try not to pay over 30 bucks or so for these.

As someone with oily skin who lives in a sunny clime and has a family history of skin cancer, it can be a challenging to find a good sunblock. Most non-chemical sunblocks-those that use titanium dioxide and zinc oxide as their active ingrediants-tend to be heavy, "pill" off, or clog my pores. This one, however, does none of that. I was really impressed by the texture of this product. More often than not, most sunscreens and sunblocks tend to be oily, but this one was not and had a light, matte finish when dry. The light tint did not leave me looking like I was wearing a mask, and this lasted all day-even through a yoga class.However, this product did have some drawbacks such as the cost: 1.5 oz of sunscreen is not going to last anyone long at all even if you only apply this product to the face. At nearly $20 a tube, it isn't cheap.Lastly, why aren't there more skin colors to chose from? It would be nice if this came in darker shades because many women of color tend to have oily skin and despite the myths, need sun protection just like their Caucasian counterparts.

First of all, just like the title suggests. This was an interesting find for me, when looking to upgrade an older laptop that I had laying around. I decided that I would use this little laptop as a "school" computer. So looking at the specifications of it, I figured I would need to max out it's potential. First added more ram bringing it from 256mb, to 2GB. Then looked at wifi adapters, got it a 300mbps wireless N card. Following doing that Windows XP sp 3 decided to crash on me...(non recoverable). Found an old 40GB HDD 4200 RPM...Loaded Ubuntu Linux on it...Worked great for a time....Then started slowing down, and the drive was getting full....Started searching for a larger drive...Stumbled across this little guy. Figured "Why Not"...+So far is working great+Fairly decent size drive for an older machine+Older interface, yet is an SSD (old tech gets new tech standards)+Seems to speed up even Ubuntu Linux on an older machine (Linux is pretty fast before this upgrade)-Yet to encounter anyOther thoughts:Will probably update this review after some time has pasted...Only got the drive about a day ago. Installation of OS took 28.3 minutes, versus Win XP's 3 hours. That's a huge difference if you ask me.

I love this little computer.  I had one of the older samsung Chromebooks based off the original CR-48 Chromebook.  It showed me that I could be productive, but the performance left a lot to be desired.  Video's stuttered, sometimes network performance was slow and on occasion it would lock up for a few minutes.  Fast forward to this little gem...The ACER's screen is about the same size as the Samsung's 12" screen, the color of the Samsung is warmer and better, and the keyboard keys of the Samsung feel firmer and make for a better typing experience but that's where its advantages end.  The Acer is faster, shows youtube 1440HD video without stuttering, I can open as many tabs as I need, and although I WANT the 4GB of memory, from the earlier Acer, I am really not missing/noticing the any performance lapse because of the reduced memory.  As a matter of fact the ACER outperforms the Samsung on every level except the afore mentioned keyboard/screen quality.The acer has Bluetooth, USB 3.0, SD card slot AND 32GB SSD, and a touch screen. I thought the touch screen was a bit gimmicky initially but after you find a few Google Apps that use it you see that the touch screen has real value.  Most notably apps like "Cut the Rope" where getting the right motion/timing is important.  Its so useful that when I use my Samsung again I find I miss not having the touch screen for scrolling through pictures, and opening apps, etc...Microsoft really, really needs to be nervous about Google, and its Chromebooks, especially now that some of the apps like Google Docs and games like Cut the Rope, can be used without a network.  Add the bonus of 2 years of 100GB of storage ($120), 10 gogo passes ($100+) and one extra month of google music ($10), and the hardware is pretty much free.  Also Google continues to update the OS.  My older Samsung is at the same software revision as the Acer C720P.  On the Samsung I would notice a slowness with the computer when the updates were downloading, on the Acer I notice no such slowness.  As a matter of fact when I see the update sign at the bottom of the screen, I stop what I am doing and reboot so that I can see the new, sometimes subtle goodies/features from Google.  This beats the pants off of a MS update, which while automatic will reboot my pc with a 15 minute warning, whether I am ready or not.  Simply put the chromebooks are very very easy to live with.Anyrate, if could only have two devices this would be the second one after my Android phone.  Yes I like this better than the tablets I have.

OK, so you are new to boating and want to stick with a familiar name when it come to boat care products that you seem to trust when maintaining your cars. Well this will not only satisfy your comfort-food needs, but will do the job nicely. It's pricey, but what cleaning product for boating isn't, or anything for boats for that matter? I bought this after much research and price comparison. I reviewed inputs on Amazon from users, I also checked out other e-tailers' pages and their reviews. I even read all the marketing pitches from a dozen or so vendors. And this I did not only for Meguiars, but its competitors products as well. I finally settled on this one as my choice.I am using this on a 1999 24' Sea Ray fixer upper that has seen better days. The craft has sat in saltwater its full life up until 2006. During those 7 years, the previous owner never waxed the boat. So as you can imagine, lava rocks had a better shine than this poor neglected craft. I applied it with a 7 inch wool cutting bonnet using a polisher at 2600 RPMs. The years came off right before my eyes. The bottle's applicator allowed me to meter the exact amount of product to apply so I can control the rate of oxidation removal and not go deeper than needed. The boat's before and after shot would make a marketer proud. It was inconceivable that I would get a mirror finish again, but this compound restored it to showroom. The compound removed saltwater eroded topcoat, oxidation tears from snaps and embedded grime in the clear coat.So far, for a heavily oxidized hull and upper deck, I have used only 1/2 of the bottle. I predicted to buy a second bottle to finish, but the product does cut well without having to glob it on.Pros:EffectivenessEasy to follow instructions for the noviceFamiliar nameGood bottle designCon's:PriceyNot easily found locally

I exfoliate every single day twice a day. I use ALL kinds of things in the drugstore and let me tell you, nothing comes close to this clay! Spending ten bucks on it then five on ACV is all the money you'll ever need to spend for your skincare. This is truly a hidden gem! I put a small amount of powdered clay into a cup, put enough ACV to make it clay-like (not too clumped nor soupy) and then put a thin layer on my face and sensitive areas of my body and thick layers on the parts with thicker skin like my legs/bum. Yes, I even put it on my bum! I put it everywhere! It tightens skin like crazy, smooths cellulite, and gives a golden glow. I do my whole body once a week and then take a shower. I do my face every two days for maintenance, it works away those tiny smile lines and eye wrinkles I'm starting to get as well as bring out my black heads. When I first got this, I had terrible black heads.I used it twice a day for two days (wash with warm water before putting on, wash out with warm-cold water) and the black heads just all came out. I also washed my face with tea tree oil soap after to tighten the pores even more but I only did the soap once a day. My face dried out a bit so I used oil free moisturizer from neutrogena (got at drugstore). To prevent drying or reddening of the skin, use a thinner layer or only do the mask once a day or once every other day instead of twice a day. I have sensitive skin so I could only do it twice a day for two days and I still got drying of the face and slight reddening. I only left it on for 15 minutes morning/night though. I put it on my mom and now shes hooked! Her wrinkles are obviously much worse than mine so I will let you know if she notices a disappearing of them. She did get the same golden glow I got though (I think this is just from it being such a good exfoliater) and the youthful glow alone got her to buy her own! We get compliments every day!

This router is clearly aimed at the consumer.  It comes complete with cables already plugged into the router and labeled as to where they go, and a simple configuration process that has the router figure out its own configuration after you plug it in.After extensive use, I have found several "fatal flaws" with the product.  One of those is the cornerstone of the product: the RF transmission power: the device claims 600mw.  With a Linksys WiFi router claiming 50mw power output next to it the signal strength is nearly identical.  My WiFi receiver should show the R10000G as having a much greater signal strength, but it doesn't.  The R10000G claims 12x the power of the other router.  I should see a significant difference is signal strength, but where I'm near the routers, whether far away or close up, the signal strength is nearly identical.  I suppose maybe I just have a really good Linksys, but considering electronic circuit design, manufacturing, and assembly, I think the odds are far greater that the Amped R10000G just isn't doing what it says it does.  I will likely RMA the product for a warranty exchange.Pros: 5 DBi detachable antennassuper simple to setuphas some advanced features, like support for VLANs, guest networks, scheduled access, mac-address control, ip-address control, and a slew of specialty wired and wireless settingsmade in TaiwanCons: every time you change a configuration item, the router needs to rebootdoesn't provide much diagnostic information on connected clientsdoesn't provide any statistics on things like port utilization or firewall activityno USB port for drive or printer sharing

The only thing is, the sound isn't that much better than from the iPad itself!  I use these in my bathroom and I can keep the iPad or iPhone out of harm's (water) way.  The sound is adequate to sing along in the shower and the rechargeable battery means I don't have to place it near inconveniently located outlets.I liked the way it synced and when you turn off the speaker the iPhone/Pad pauses play.  I have found my use for this so I think 4 stars is fair.  I'd even go five, but I like other speaker solutions better so I have to dock this a star.

Most of my computer work is done in an office environment. I enjoy some video games in the evening, but usually do not get much time to play. You could say I am a casual gamer these days, but I spend probably 10 hours every day at a computer. I play League of Legends, FPS, used to play MMOs, and some variety of single player games.My Logitech G5 was starting to feel dated, so I decided to pick this mouse up while I researched something in a higher price/quality range. I figured the worst that would happen is I would throw away a relatively small amount of money and the best outcome would be finding a good value purchase.While I like the shape and texture of the mouse itself and the smooth-scrolling of the wheel, those features do not differentiate it from others available.After having used it for two and a half months, it's going into my junk drawer and the G5 is coming back out (never got around to ordering another mouse as I was distracted giving this one its test drive).The big negatives include:(1) After a longer-than-normal gaming session, my wrist was really bothering me; I have not had an issue with other mice (YMMV) - this is the only red flag I need to ditch a mouse(2) The LMB's switch feels like it is already wearing out - it's making a slightly different noise now (than it used to) and feels much "looser" than the RMB(3) The LED is bright and never sleeps. My computer is in the guest room, so it is not an issue for me, but if I were sleeping in the same room, it would require a solution(4) The side buttons are not readily accessible (YMMV)(5) The DPI toggle is one way with no indicator (other than moving your mouse)I was not expecting the world from a bargain bin mouse, but with so many 4- and 5-star reviews, I thought I might have landed a big value winner. Sadly, it was has not been worth the meager amount it cost, but on the bright side, it's in my junk drawer in case I need an extra mouse for some reason.

When I first purchased this drive, I thought that I had found a functional, economical drive enclosure for my Seagate Barracuda drive to serve as a backup disk.  The setup of the Nextar CX was not very difficult despite the lack of documentation.  I just needed to fasten some screws and to connect a wire to the front portion of the enclosure before screwing the enclosure shut.  The drive enclosure did get warm, but seemed to dissipate the heat form the drive quickly enough.  I found the bright, blue light on the front of the drive enclosure to be annoying.  I also found it annoying that the power cord did not fit into the socket on the back of the drive enclosure very well.The drive worked great for a while, but then one day, after an update for my Windows XP Professional operating system, the Nextar CX drive stopped being recognized by Windows.  The first thing I thought of was to go to the Nexstar website to download the latest drivers for the enclosure.  Although there are drivers on the Nexstar website, they are for Windows 98, not for versions of Windows released in the past decade.  I did a search on the internet and discovered that others had the same problem with the Nexstar CX drive enclosure, but I could not find any solutions to the problem.  The FAQ on the Nexstar website recommended that in the event the drive was not recognized, it should be reformatted which would result in a total loss of data.  However, even if this drastic step were to be considered, it was not possible to format a drive which the computer does not recognize in the first place.I contacted Vantech Customer Support by email and received a prompt response.  I was asked if I could put a different hard drive into the enclosure to which I responded that I did not have a spare drive.  Vantech then told me to return the enclosure if it was under a year old.  Unfortunately, my drive enclosure had been purchased 13 months prior.  I was then told "You can try to find local computer store for them to check on this external hard drive enclosure."  So, basically I am left with a worthless drive enclosure and a backup disk that I may have to reformat when I buy a new enclosure.In summary, an inexpensive drive with prompt customer service for the first year.  But, you get what you pay for.  If you are looking for a reliable drive enclosure for your sensitive files, then do not purchase this drive.  It is unreliable and you risk losing your data if your operating system stops recognizing the drive.  Judging from searches on the internet, this problem has happened to others as well and it is not worth the risk to save a few dollars versus buying a more reliable drive enclosure.

By now, if you are contemplating the purchase of the iPod video, you have probably seen one and know you want it.  So I will just confirm that the product works great and is a joy to use.  The user interface is simply a breakthrough in human engineering and with 60GB you can afford to store songs in the highest quality and not worry about space.If you are a PC user and this is your first iPod, this could also be your first exposure to iTunes.  It was for me.  And here, surprisingly, I am a little disappointed.  I already had a sizable library of CDs that I had ripped using Windows Media Player in .WMA format.  While iTunes can read .WMA, the iPod will not play these files.  iTunes offers to translate all your .WMA files into iTunes format.  Don't worry it will not destroy your existing files.  However, don't make the same mistake I made: while Apple would like you to use its proprietary format, there is an option to use MP3 instead.  I did not realize that until I had translated 10GB worth of songs.  I believe using MP3 is a better choice because it is now a universal file format.  Even my cell phone can play MP3 files.It is fashionable to assume that any piece of software from Apple will be better than a corresponding product from Microsoft.  Sadly, that is not the case with iTunes.  While iTunes will translate your existing files, it will not copy album art.  Silly, since the iPod does a great job of displaying album art.  Fortunately, you can associate graphic files manually with your files.  I also find that iTunes does not offer as many sorting options as Media Player and that it runs slower.Despite my gripes with the software, I give the product 5 stars because once you have all your files in the iPod, you should not have to use iTunes very often.  With so many accessories to use an iPod in you living room, kitchen or car it can easily become your only music playing device.

The music sounds much clearer, more crisp.  It's totally awesome!  At first I thought it was just my imagination, but then I did an actual back to back sound test, using the two different cables; yup, this one produces a higher quality sound.  It comes coiled fairly tight, but can be stretched up to a total length of 6 feet; I uncoiled mine and it did stretched to just about 6 feet.  Once I released the cable it bounced back to it original length, though I cannot say if that would still be the case if I had kept it stretched for an extended period of time.  I really don't need that much length in my car, due to the location of the auxiliary output; I only purchased the 6 feet cable, as opposed to the 3 feet, in the event I ever need the longer length...such as if I were to switch cars.  In any event, I liked the coil feature as it keeps the excess cord retracted and out of the way (a problem I had once my so called retractable one stopped working.)  So overall, this cord is attractive, with it's slender profile and tight coil, and it works great.  Fits my need ideally!

I bought this router specifically to use as an 2.4 Ghz 802.11n access point. It came in a refurb Cisco box and was in very good cosmetic condition, though I could tell from a few minor scuff marks it had been used. First thing I did was update it to the latest firmware. Next I did some speed tests and soon discovered I could not connect at anything faster than 144 Mbps (rather than 300 Mbps) with both of my laptops right next to the router (both laptops have built in Intel Wi-Fi cards). I did a search on Internet and found this was not an uncommon problem (new and refurbed). I'm not sure if it is an inherent flaw, or a firmware issue, but I wasn't going to keep it to find out, so I returned it. Ended up purchasing a Linksys EA4500, which will connect to both my laptops at 300+ Mbps in the 2.4 Ghz band all day long.That said, everything else about the router was fine. In the short time I used it I didn't experience any dropped connections and it seemed quite stable. Even though I had issues with this particular model of Cisco/Linksys router, I wouldn't hesitate to purchase another Cisco refurbished unit.

My 2 year old daughter has very thick, curly hair that dries out and becomes frizzy.  I was looking for a product to use in between shampoos, as the product I normally use is pretty pricey.  The Just for Me Hair Milk Curl Smoother was decent at best.I applied an adequate amount (about the size of a quarter) to my daughter's hair after dampening it.  The first thing I noticed was how thin the product was; it made it easy to spread throughout the hair without clunking up.  Then I noticed the smell.  It had a very pleasant fragrance that was not overpowering.  Upon application, my daughter's hair looked smooth, not a bit of frizz to it.As the product began to dry, my daughter's hair was still moisturized and the curls were nice and smooth; however, throughout the day, the product began to wear off (more quickly than our usual product).  As the day ended, her hair was frizzy and the curls no longer smooth.This product was just okay.  It did not leave my daughter's hair crunchy and stiff; however, it also did not help her hair hold moisture or smooth curls.  We will use the Just for Me Hair Milk Curl Smoother until we run out, but I don't think we'll be purchasing any more bottles.

I absolutely love this turntable!  I've had it nearly a week now and have used it every day to convert a huge collection of vinyl to MP3.  I have had no problems with skipping.  The only time the arm has stopped or pulled back was when I forgot and left the Auto-Stop on while playing 45s.When I first unpacked the turntable, I had a problem with no sound coming through the speakers until I realized the internal speakers were turned off.  As others have said, the quality of the speakers is surprisingly good for such a little unit.  I've only played with the radio for a few minutes, but was surprised at how clearly so many stations came in even without the antenna extended.I love the fact it can play 78s as well as 33s and 45s.  It does come with a 45 adapter if anyone is wondering (as I was).The player is so simple to use.  Just insert an SD card or USB flash drive, press Record, play the record, and you've got an MP3 version of the record.  I then use Spin It Again to add the recordings to iTunes.

I started using Neutrogena's Clear Pore Cleanser/Mask in May 2010 when I started to suffer from (surprising) adult acne.  I had deep, painful acne cysts around my jawline and cheeks that didn't seem to go away despite the use of harsh RX cleansers and gels.  With my sensitive skin, the harsh formula of the RXs seemed to just my skin raw, chapped, and aching.  On a whim, I decided to try the Clear Pore Cleanser/Mask because of the Benzoyl Peroxide formula that was similar to the RX but without some of the other additives.  My first impressions using it were that this cleanser did not sting and burn my face as the RX had done.  After about three days of using the product, I began to notice that the size of my acne cysts had decreased considerably and were less painful and red in appearance.  By week two of using the product, I had no new breakouts and the acne I had was nearly gone.  Additionally, my skin seemed to be handling the formula well- I had smooth, normal sky.  No dry patches, redness, or peeling.  Over a year later, I rarely have breakouts and when I do, I apply the cleanser as a mask for 5 minutes twice a day and the blemish is gone within two days.  The only negative point about this cleanser is that it can be hard to wash off your hands and face.  The cleanser is very much the consistency of a mask and, therefore, it takes a little bit extra time to get off your fingers.  I recommend using a washcloth to wipe away the cleanser from your face (and also to provide a bit of exfoliation for your skin).  With the amount of money I save from not having to make a dermatology appointment and buy RX cleansers and medicine along with the beauty benefits make this an outstanding product!

I have scars from Lupus. I've tried chemical peels through doctors and then decided to do it myself since I could spend a lot less and do the same procedure--dip cotton balls in solution, swab face, wait, and rinse. I like the strength of this solution, which does create a good peeling action without being too strong (I've had peels that created more scarring because they were too strong). I gave this product four stars because I find salicylic acid to be the best peel for my skin, but I am pleased with this solution. I have found that doing a series of peels over time greatly decreases the unevenness of my skin. Unlike glycolic acid, which makes me break out, this peel does the trick without making my skin blemish. If you're looking for something that is strong without being too damaging,this peel may be a great fit.

Pros:- Fast flip out design- Pocket clip- Solid feel and design- Black anodized- Slim and serrated design- Light- Flip stub acts as a hiltCons:- Paint chips easily- Thumb stub is small and useless- Clip is too tight- Not enough gripThoughts:It definitely need to get loosed up and lubed before use. The pivot requires a 6-point star head to loosen it. Came pretty sharp from the factory. Black anodized for reduced reflection, but the finish is not as rugged as some flashlights (Surefire, fenix). An inexpensive fast operating knife for a fraction of the price of other knives. The way the clip is positioned and the oval grooves on the handle that the clip recesses into gives it a death grip on your pants, which also kills the pant seem and fabric when you pull it out. Pulling the clip away from the body while unholstering it will fix that.The material is not the best, but for this price, it usually isn't a big deal. Repeated use will have you constantly sharpening. I love the flip finger opening. Once lubed and loosened, it should only require the use of one hand (an index finger) to open. The grip could be worked on. The flip stud does act as a hilt or guard, but the body itself does not have knurling (apart from the lock) or enough friction material.A similar design to the EVO is the Boker Plus Trance. It has a more secure grip and feels right and factory sharp right out of the box. Other than that, the EVO serves its purpose and I would buy again.Bottom line:Recommended. Good buy for cheap!

I just received this product today, and after getting it setup the way I want it in my car it has been working fine for now. I will update this review should it stop working or otherwise break.I am using an iPod Touch 3rd Gen with 32gb running iOS 4.2.1. It charges the iPod with no problems, unlike other car chargers I had previously purchased that no longer work since I upgraded the iPod from iOS 3.1.3 to iOS 4.0. To me the sound quality is great; I'm no expert, but I can tell when the audio being played doesn't sound like it should, and all of the songs I tested it with sounded perfectly fine.There is static coming through the speakers either in between songs, or when the music is stopped. Once a song plays the static goes away. One thing to realize is that the static is not present when my iPod is not plugged into the device, so it is only when my iPod is connected and charging. I'll try to remember to update this review after I see if the static is only present with the iPod Touch (I have an iPod Classic I can try), or after the iPod is fully charged.The product is well made in my opinion--it doesn't seem cheap like the other chargers I've used, but I wouldn't say it has the quality of a Swiss watch either.The buttons from left to right are: (1) frequency preset one, (2) frequency preset two, (3) on/off (hold down to turn on), (4) frequency down, and (5) frequency up (just in case anyone wanted to know).The Sticky Pad seems like a nice touch so far--apparently all you have to do is wash it and it will continue to be "sticky."A note on the vendor, I ordered this on a Monday, and received it on Wednesday--they emailed me to double check the shipping address and I think it got to me pretty quick. So far I like their customer service.**Addition--Four days of use**We went on a day trip, and the FM transmitter worked really well. I think the static isn't as prevalent. The only problem we have with it is that it pops out of the plug. We had a four-hour drive round-trip and it popped out twice.I love the fact that it charges the iPod (whether it is on or off), and it has a cord to easily change music or check something like a map or address (for a passenger, not the driver, of course). The sound quality is really clear; absolutely no audible static during the song. In my opinion, it's equivalent to CD playback.

I purchased this receiver hoping I could integrate a variety of digital and analog sources into my cluster of receivers and amps that drive speakers in several rooms and outdoors.  The TX-NR509 seemed to offer everything I needed: multiple HDMI inputs and a full complement of analog inputs, subwoofer pre-out, preamp out for surround rear speakers, the highly touted "zone2" output, network capability, and the added bonus capability of using an Android smartphone for remote control and streaming.I spent the better part of two days connecting all the cables, running the setup procedures, and becoming familiar with the receiver's capabilities and limitations.  The TX-NR509 does have very sophisticated capability to optimize its output for your listening room.  It even comes with a calibrated microphone that you use to support a series of tests that the "audissey" system runs to check your room.  After completing the setup procedure I checked out audio and video performance with HDMI input from my satellite receiver--it performed beautifully.  Then I connected the ethernet cable; the TX-NR509 immediately connected to my home network, recognized all the compatible devices (basically PCs with Windows Media Player).  Using the Onkyo remote I was able to browse for media files and play them on the TX-NR509 just as easily as from any PC keyboard.  Next I installed the Onkyo application for my Android phone.  It installed in seconds and connected to the TX-NR509 immediately.  A moment later I was streaming music to the receiver, controlling volume, and many other functions right from my DroidX.  I turned on my outdoor speakers (powered by a secondary amplifier fed by the Onkyo back speaker pre-out), and walked outdoors with my Droid--it continued to function as a remote 50' away from the TX-NR509--a very nice capability for patio listening.As I continued to explore the behavior of my system I began to see the limitations of the TX-NR509.  I will list them in order as I discovered them.1) the actual number of independent sources the receiver can handle is far smaller than the number of inputs terminals on the back of the device.  There are four basic selector positions for source inputs, "BD/DVD", "VCR/DVR", "CBL/SAT", and "GAME".  Each of these has an HDMI jack and a set of analog jacks.  If you use both the HDMI input and the analog input for the same selector position, the analog input will be ignored.  There are also four digital audio input jacks that similarly compete for selector positions.  Bottom line, there are 12 possible audio inputs, but only four of them can be used.2) the TX-NR509 has no up-convert or down-convert capability for audio (digital audio cannot be converted to analog and vice versa).  As a consequence, if your source is digital (say HDMI from satellite or internet radio) there is no analog audio signal, so the much ballyhooed "Zone2" output is mute and useless.  How could Onkyo overlook such a vital capability in today's digital world?  I thought I could overcome this limitation by using the analog audio output of my HDTV (which does have down-convert capability).  This trick seemed to work as a means to feed audio from digital sources to my remote speaker systems until I discovered an even more critical flaw in the receiver's design.3) The last straw, the show-stopper for me was my discovery that enabling audio feed over HDMI cable from the TX-NR509 to the TV (an option in the setup menu) automatically disables the pre-amp outputs to the subwoofer and surround rear speakers.  Together the latter two limitations prevent me from operating all speaker systems simultaneously at full capability, even though I have plenty of power to do so.Finally, while mulling over my options but still enjoying the wireless streaming capability from my Android to the Onkyo receiver the network card in the receiver apparantly failed completely.  All attempts to connect to the network result in a stalled system with the message "initializing" ad nauseum.Tomorrow this device goes back in the box and back to Amazon.  I will look for something better.The silver lining in this dark cloud for me it that I discovered that Onkyo's free app for the Android works very well as a streaming device and remote control for Windows Media Player on any networked PC.  This opens up some interesting possiblilities.

First, it doesn't have much instructions, just short instructions on the tabs: "This sticker helps to remove the mask from the ScreenGUARD." and "Please peel off the mask with this sticker."  I figured that the two thin layers are the masks for the screenguard, then I referred to the instructions of Cbus Wireless screen protector for my son's iTouch:1.Clean the screen thoroughly.2.Holding Tab 2 with your fingers, peel Tab 1 all the way off, taking care not to touch the adhesive side. (Note from me: this adhesive side goes to the screen.)3.Place the screen protector onto your device, laying it from one end to the other.  Once in place, remove Tab 2 from the screen protector.Step 3 is the key step, it takes some patience.  I first peeled off tab 1 and 2 a little bit to check, the masks are thin and flexible layers, the middle layer(the protector) is thick and hard. Before you do this step, make sure you get a credit card ready.  Then I lined it up first with the home key, as I did it from one end to the other, I let it stick to the screen slowly while I used the credit card to get rid off any air bubbles.  I peeled off the corners a couple of times until I got rid off air bubbles, take care not to bend it when you try to peel if off, it sticks well to the screen.  At the end, there are only some minor bubbles on the black frame which I can live with.

I received one of these direct from the company to try out. They have not asked for or suggested that I write a favourable review, they have left me to write what I want good or bad. As far as I am aware this is identical to the retail item.The pump comes complete with a carry pouch, bike bracket, fixings, quick release strap plus spares, tyre levers and inflation adapters.The bike bracket is a sturdy piece of plastic that you mount on the bottle fixings. If you have a cage already fitted, this simply sandwiches in-between, so you don't have to have 2 lots of fixing points. There is a rubber strap that hooks onto the bracket, loops around and holds the pump firmly in place. When parking the bike up, the pump can be placed into the soft carry pouch for carrying with you.The tyre levers are strong enough to get a deflated tyre off the rim without risk of snapping or bending and the spoke hooks assist in holding the lever in place whilst you sit the other in. The lips are gently rounded so that you can work the lever around the tyre without it scraping the tyre rim.The adapters will inflate most sports balls and beach or gym balls. Not something I have used, but they look and feel as good as any other I have used in the past. As with all needle adapters it is advisable to moisten the needle to aid insertion.The pump is a telescopic design that is light weight, yet strong. The flexible delivery tube is tucked up inside, so you never loose it. To switch from Schrader to Presta valves, there is no messing about with swapping parts around, or turning valve assemblies inside out. You simply give the end of the delivery tube a twist, extend it out and you have converted it. When you are done, everything tucks away into the tube and reposition the dust cover.Performance of the pump is good. My bike has Hybrid tyres that need to inflate to 75 psi. I completely deflated the tyre and used the pump to inflate it again. Very quickly I was able to get the tyre back to a pressure where I could ride the bike safely. Getting to the higher end of the tyre pressure took a bit of work, but that is to be expected.On a rough ride, the pump was held firmly in place with no rattles or knocking. When I got home the pump was still in-situ, so you can be assured you are not going to lose it on a ride.A few points to note:1. When clamping the pump make sure that both halves are in the clamp area with the strap over both, just to ensure that the pump doesn't shake open as you are riding.2. In the telescopic section there is a black plastic screw cap. Check this is (finger) tight before pumping so that you don't accidentally pull the whole thing apart. I haven't had mine work loose, but it is worth checking before use.3. Periodically unscrew the aforementioned plastic screw cap and use a light oil to keep the parts moving smoothly. Don't use a petroleum based oil like WD40 as this could damage the rubber seals. A plastic / rubber friendly silicone oil is probably a good one to use.Bottom line - A high performance pump which is in keeping with the range and quality of out door products from the Friendly Swede and this is backed up with a lifetime warranty

Bought 2 of these for my kids on March 1, 2009. Today is July 22, 2009.My kids, (5 and 6 yrs.) were always wanting to play with our iPhones so we decided to let them buy themselves an iPod Touch with their money they'd saved from holidays and birthdays. Loads of educational apps, great kid video podcasts, and lots of fun games. They've been a great "edutainment" device. We don't let them use headphones and they have limits on usage.So, while they love the ipods in their stock state they are WAY too slick and easily damaged. Looked around, checked a LOT of reviews on iPodlounge and this case kept coming up so we ordered a couple.Great investment, the kids are very careful with them, but they are kids, I can't expect the same level of care from a kid as I can from myself or other adults, they WILL drop things... and use them with grubby fingers. This case is the best way to protect an ipod, all surfaces are protected behind plastic and rubber or the clear screen protector. It's not water-proof but it does offer a lot of splash resistance. The dock connector and headphone jack are covered with flaps that pop out.They've each dropped theirs at least a couple times that I'm aware of, probably more, with no damage. The sound from the built-in speaker is somewhat muted, but that's to be expected.The attached screen protector had me worried as once it wears out you either need to buy a new case or remove it and use another protector but in doing so lose that aspect of protection. I can happily report that it doesn't show any signs of wear yet. There is an issue where you can see little air bubbles where the screen protector presses against the glass of the screen, but they're mostly visible when the power is off. The kids have never mentioned it.I've had rubber cases stretch out and loosen up to the point where they are no longer useful. I was worried about the rubber on these initially, and then more so when I saw the kids would fiddle with the edges where the rubber and plastic meet. Amazingly the rubber hasn't deformed from the kids picking at it.Buttons are easily accessed through the case thanks to some well placed bumps.This is not a case you would use with a docking type speaker, it is big and bulky and the design interferes with that kind of operation. This is for people who carry theirs around with them a lot and want a grippable, beefy case to keep their toy nice and clean. It doubles the thickness of the ipod.The higher cost of this case will eventually be offset by the high resale value of a device that shows no signs of wear when the time to upgrade comes.Update - 12/2/10Cases are still going strong. They've ripped the bottom covers off, they show signs of major wear, but the ipods are still going strong.They drop them occasionally, I tell them to be careful but don't get too mad as I know their iPods are well protected.Their usage remains regular as new games are released regularly, the iPod itself was a great investment for them. I'll update again in a year or so.Update 8/9/11Nothing to note since last update.  Know of 3 other iPod Touch that have died from lack of Otterbox, my kid's are unharmed despite the occasional drop.May upgrade when the new models come out, will definitely be getting them an Otterbox again.Update 4/25/12Got the kids new iPods and Otterboxes for Christmas, gave the old ones to my granddaughters who were my inspiration for getting these cases in the first place. The iPods were in pristine condition, the cases are beat to heck but still quite functional.  After 5 months they still haven't destroyed them. Fantastic return on investment.

When I first ordered this imager, I wasn't holding out too much hope of being blown away with the image quality. I recently purchased and returned several USB equipped digital microscopes looking for one that could not only give me impressive results through the eye piece, but also high quality SHARP images on my computer that I could then save, archive and share.  Unfortunately, all of the models I purchased prior to my last had at least one fundamental FLAW in the design that made it a poor choice for what I was looking for.As I was boxing up the 5th digital microscope product for return and refund to Amazon.com, I decided that I would just focus on a quality microscope first and worry about the computer side of things later. After spending a few hour reading up on the latest brands and models of quality scopes, I purchased theCelestron 44104 500x Power Advanced Biological Microscopefrom Amazon.  Folks, this is a GREAT scope for the money and I highly recommend it if you are looking for something solid with excellent optics without spending a fortune!  You can read my review of this great product in my reviews section. After using the scope for a few sessions and being thoroughly satisfied, I found myself still longing for that computer interface option.  I then searched the web for a stand alone USB microscope camera and was pleased to see that there were such things on the market!  My first search brought up a link to this product here on Amazon.com.  The fact that it was made by Celestron was a good omen and I figured that it should mate to my scope without too much trouble.  (Turned out to be totally true!)As I said earlier in my review, I was a bit skeptical on the quality based on the price and some of the low review scores this thing had received.  But, since low reviews on a product often say more about the reviewer's skills to read and follow instructions than they do about the product's quality, I went ahead and ordered myself one. After waiting for what seemed like an eternity *, the device arrived today and within 5 minutes I had the imager installed in the adapter ring and sitting on top of my 44104 in place of the eyepiece.I am using a Macintosh computer and as usual the Mac support from Celestron was practically nonexistent. This didn't bother me though because I already owned a copy of what many consider to be the best webcam software available on ANY computer platform.  I am of course referring to the award winning "EVOcam" webcam application from EVOlogical.  I plugged in the USB cable from the imager, fired up EVOcam and held my breath! My heart was racing as I pulled into focus my first slide.In a word... WOW!!  Not only did the image look EXACTLY like it did to my eye with the conventional optics, the imager was reproducing every byte of sharpness and brilliant color contained in the specimen under the objective lens!  I really don't think you could ask for any more from an imager than this!  With my 44104 now paired up with a great piece of webcam software like EVOcam, the sky's the limit in what I can do with this rig!  How about time-lapse movies of a bacterial culture growing?  The feeding habits of an Ameba of Hydra as it goes about its day?  I kind of feel sorry for the PC folks out there that don't have a program like EVOcam to use with their scope and this imager!So there ya go... Buy yourself a quality microscope first and then pick up this imager second for the ultimate digital microscope setup!  As for the 1-Star reviewer's opinions here on this product page?  I'll go with "UTTERLY CLUELESS" on a good day and down right idiotic on a bad one.  I don't know if the scope they were trying to use this thing with was total garbage, or they simply don't know how to read an owners manual!  Either way, their low review scores say more about their own reading and comprehension skills than anything related to this product.* My only gripe is with the retailer who sold me the product. (Adorama Camera) It took WAY TOO LONG to get here!  Come on people!  10 days?  That is ridiculous!  If you have a choice in which retailer to purchase this from on Amazon, CHOOSE SOMEONE BESIDES THEM!  Unless you don't mind waiting 10 days for your imager to arrive in your mailbox.  ;o)

Love this watch. I've got a couple, one in black, one in gold, and I'm very pleased with both. This is a watch you can like even if analog-digital isn't your thing. The design has been around at least since Arnold Schwarzenegger wore it in 'Kindergarten Cop'. It also comes in a blue version with blue bezel.I call this my 'snorkel' watch. The dive bezel works smoothly but moves in both directions, so it's not for true Scuba...however I've snorkeled with this down to 20-25 feet and no problems, so it's definitely waterproof for all non-Scuba uses.Time adjustment is simple. The luminescent hands are quite bright when you first come in from the sun, they seem to last about an hour before fading, so they're for casual use but not, say, serious spelunking. That said, they're easy to read in all light levels against the black. It's a great watch at twilight because you can still clearly read the dial. The second hand is also easy to read in all light levels.This is a quietly handsome watch at a great price. Solid, reliable like every Casio, but unpretentious.I've worn mine as a general outdoors sports/beach/knockaraound watch, and it still looks great.The crystal is completely un-scratched despite some pretty careless treatment.I'm totally pleased with both of mine. Check out the white and black face versions as well. This same style series also comes in a three-dial chronograph version which is equally attractive, affordable and reliable. I have a couple of those too.There's nothing not to like about this watch. The rubber factory band is fine, but these really look good on a nylon Nato strap (if you're not familiar with those, google it).I found mine for around $30 shipped. Prices fluctuate, so do shop around.Hope this is helpful. Happy times, everybody.

There are a lot of great benefits to the jawbone UP. For me, on the rare day when I have moved less than 10,000 steps it really does motivate me to move around more. I like how it easily links with other apps such as run keeper and myfitness pal. The food tracking part of the UP sucks in my opinion, I just don't like how it presents it on the screen or how it has "popular" food items- all of which are processed foods!  It should be promoting healthier eating!  And it also makes it a little more annoying to search for items not in its popular list.  It is also pretty cool to see you sleep cycle.The cons, for me: I work nights so my sleep is all messed up- I don't need the up band to tell me I should get more sleep to be healthier...as it gives little daily health advice.  There are apps on the iphone that you can use to tell you sleep cycles, steps per day, calories burned and where you can log calorie intake- none of which will cost more than a dollar or two.  Also, the fit is not amazing.  I originally ordered a medium, going by the size chart provided on Jawbones website.  I wore it for about 2 weeks and decided it really was just too big.  I exchanged it for a small, which fits better but not perfect.  The fact that it does not clasp close in any way makes it very easy to catch on end on things.I must add, customer service reps for UP are amazing!  They sent me a knew size and were very easy to work with.  They also sent the return shipping label so it was very easy to return the old one.  So I'm still torn on whether it really worth $130 or not. It pretty cool but it also has lost its appeal after about 6 weeks of wearing it.  Also, I wish it could automatically sink to my phone!

Our primary TV is a high definition flatscreen with excellent picture quality, but sound that is mediocre, at best. The TVEE looked like an ideal way to improve the overall sound quality when watching TV, and simple enough for someone who is (admittedly) not the best in the world at connecting audio components.Initial setup is a breeze. The unit includes one optical cable that connected easily to the TV's output. One extremely minor issue (with the television, not the TVEE) arose when the instructions called for turning off the television speaker. Our television does not give that particular option; I would assume that other people with the same issue would be able to figure out that it's essentially the same as turning the volume down (not muting, as that leaves an icon on the screen of many televisions), but it seemed odd that the instructions did not address this possibility anyway.Sound from cable, Amazon streaming video, and Blu-Rays is, overall, superb. (Granted, this is coming from a "layman"; I'm not certain how well it would hold up to the scrutiny of an audiophile.) We have not experienced any of the "dropping out" issues that some other reviewers have described. However, as others have mentioned, it does seem rather odd that the TVEE does not have bass, treble, balance, or any of the other "standard" audio controls for fine tuning. It does have adjustable trim level for the Aux 1 input only, but the instructions do not explain at all what this setting means, and I had to resort to Google to learn that it deals with the input sensitivity.The only other issue we've run into is also rather minor, and deals with the inputs. There is the optical digital input, as well as two auxiliary inputs for 1/8" stereo connectors. Our TV (and I'm not certain if this is standard, or a drawback of our television) will only output sound to an external speaker if it is input via HDMI. No problem for the cable box or the Blu-Ray player, but rather a big deal for video game systems that don't have an HDMI output (like the Wii). To use such a system requires purchasing additional adaptors; though Boston Acoustics provides a few extra cables, they don't provide a way to connect the standard two RCA connectors to the 1/8" stereo input. It struck me as somewhat odd that, of three different input options, dual-RCA inputs was not one of them. (After buying about $5 worth of connectors, however, the sound from the Wii is the best I've ever heard from that system.)Overall, the TVEE is a fairly economical sound solution that should produce high enough quality results for most users, and is simple enough for anyone to set up.(As of this writing, we have not tested the remote capabilities of the TVEE. Rather than programming the remote to transmit certain signals to the TVEE, the TVEE essentially "learns" the signals from seemingly unused buttons on the remote. We currently do not have any "extra" functions on our remote, but will likely purchase a new universal remote in the near future so as not to have to deal with the separate, quite tiny, TVEE remote control exclusively for volume control. If this impacts my opinion of the TVEE, I will update this review accordingly.)

This cyclocomputer is just... meh.Does it display instantaneous speed, max spd, avg spd, ODO, time, distance travelled, etc?  Yeah sure, they all do that.Is it cheaper than others? Nope.Is it more reliable than others? Nope.Is it more user friendly than others? Nope.So what's it got going for it?  Frankly I have a hard time coming up with 1 distinguishing feature that would compel me to buy this model.  Maybe is has a backlight? Yeah, but not a very good one at that.I've been using this computer for a few weeks now since my Planet Bike Protege 9.0 was stolen with my old bike.  I miss my Protege.  Here's where the Cateye falls short compared to the Protege:-it's more confusing to program and setup.  It can be done but as with any of these devices there is a learning curve.  This one seems steeper than others.-it's wireless.  I think wireless speed-only measurement is a sham.  It is prone to interference as others have noted, the sensor on the fork is larger/heavier and prone to stealing (i.e. you need to remove the computer and sensor when you park the bike). The wireless range is small and the sensor must be installed directly under the head unit.  It chews thru batteries faster.  Wireless for a cadence sensor maybe makes sense, but speed only is less reliable than wired.-the LCD screen contrast is quite poor and directional.  To read the numbers clearly I need to tilt the nose of the computer down, so the screen is no longer perpendicular to my eyes.  Unfortunately when I'm riding the bike the tail of the computer is down b/c it is attached to the stem.  This renders the display all but unreadable except in bright sunlight.  Forget about a quick glance down at the bars, ain't gonna happen.-the backlight button is on the back.  This makes operation difficult, especially when riding, especially with gloves.  Regardless, backlight is rather useless anyways.  It is a puny light in the bottom R of the screen.Summary: 2stars only because it actually does "work" as a bike computer, just not very well.  If you're looking for a basic cyclocomputer, I think there are better choices out there.

I got this thinking that it would fit several different handguns that I have. It does not. The holster itself is pretty well made considering where it's made and the material seems strong enough for general wear but here's the problems.1. The holster is not made for compact or sub-compact handguns. It's not Universal like it says.2. Here's what I tried that did not fit, Glock 23 and 27, S&W; M&P; .40, Colt Defender.45, Ruger SR9 and a couple that I borrowed from friends. The holster is just way too wide at the mouth and all the guns go too far into the holster. The trigger guard on almost all the guns got caught inside the holster and you can't remove the weapon smoothly. The strap holding the gun in place has a double snap strap and velcro to hold the gun in place and is very hard to un-do if you need to remove the weapon quickly, it's not just a quick thumb break release. There is a small piece of material from the belt strap sewn right inside the holster that I think may cause some of the problems.3. I tried a full size 1911 .45 and a Tanfoglio .45 full size semi-auto and the holster is fine for full size full frame guns. Unfortunately I didnt need a holster for any of them.All and all if your looking for a holster for a full size semi-auto with 4 1/2 to 5" barrel then this would probably be fine for you. The holster does have alot of adjustments on it and be prepared to take quite a bit of time to get it to fit you right unless your 6'4" or something. The mag case seems fairly durable and will hold all of the mags that I tried from the weapons I have listed above.The only reason I returned this was because the holster was just too big, too wide and too deep for my purpose so I ordered another one that I know is smaller. I have several UTG products and and have found that they will last you for awhile given the type of use.

I bought this to carry water for hiking.  It's okay -- the insulation is a nice plus -- but it's really not as convenient for hiking as I had expected.You basically have two choices: wear it or hold it in your hand.  I haven't yet been able to find a place on my body or pack where it doesn't get in the way.  The angled handle doesn't help.  And if you wear it, you need to unzip and rezip every time you want a drink.  Holding it works, and here the handle helps, but you can't carry it in your hands if you're using hiking poles.So I don't really use this anymore.  I've got Nalgene holsters which are much more convenient.  They hold the bottle vertical, I can get the bottle in and out without rezipping, and they don't get in my way.I can see that for the other applications mentioned by reviewers this could be terrific.  It stands up nicely, it seems to insulate well, it compresses well.  But for hiking, where portability is key, it doesn't work well enough for me to use it.

I purchased this power conditioner/regulator recently. In testing, I found that the output voltage followed the input with no apparent attempt to regulate the voltage. In talking with the manufacturer, it turns out the unit does not regulate in the normal sense of the word - it has a huge deadband that causes it to ignore normal line voltage variations. I was seeing from 113.5 to 119 out of the unit. I would also note that the spec sheet supplied indicates that for an 87 volt to 129 volt input, the output is 120. It doesn't to that.I am planning on returning the unit ASAP.

I, too, have used Wen on and off for about a year now, but stopped using it as a total-hair cleanser; rather, I use it as a conditioner for the middle/end of my hair after I wash JUST my oily scalp with shampoo.  Wen, for me, was too pricey to use in mass quantities and for my whole head.I purchased this (L'Oreal Evercreme) after reading glowing reviews about how it's more cost-effective than Wen.  I was ultimately very excited and almost opted to buy TWO bottles.  Thankfully I didn't!  In the end, I didn't find much "benefit" with the Evercreme.For starters, L'Oreal Evercreme DOES lather.  It lathers LESS than traditional shampoo, but is nowhere near the type of consistency that Wen is.  If you've used Wen (especially for a whole-head conditioning shampoo use), you'll find it difficult to "go backwards" and use this as a Wen substitute or replacement.I attempted to use the Evercreme as a conditioner ONLY (as I ultimately did with the Wen), but that was nearly impossible:  the end of my hair was NOT sufficiently conditioned and was much more flyaway, undoubtedly due to the lather.  You have to either go all or none:  so I had to use it to clean my scalp and massage less of it into the length of my hair and let it "run through" as it rinsed out.  Ultimately, you're using a LOT of product (as you would with Wen). I rarely write bad reviews but found I was less then impressed with the SaberCut. I attempted to saw through a number of live branches, each about 3-4 inches and diameter and found the SaberCut Saw to perform poorly. It quickly became bound in the wood and required a large amount of effort to continually pull. The limbs were attached low on the tree which made for a less then ideal angle, however I expect my tools to work even in less then ideal situations. I understand the appeal of having a small, compact saw but so far have been disappointed.Pros:-Small, compact and light weight-Perfect for a survival bag-Straps are comfortable and sturdyCons:-Binds easily in wood-Requires a lot of effort to use-Is not practical for extensive useConclusion: Ultimately I think my expectations were off and that's why I rated this item a 2 instead of a 1. I was expecting that it would be as easy as a small hand saw but found it to be much more difficult to use, perhaps it is a learning curve but I suspect that it just isn't the right tool for most jobs. I can see the appeal as a survival tool or back up saw, however I just don't think its worth the money to me. I would not recommend this to others.
