I've been searching for a decent pair of headphones for the gym. I have tried the Skull Candy, Able Planet, and JVCs, and these are by far the best. When comparing them against the Skull Candy (which has good reviews), these sound much more dynamic, and the details just jump out at you. The sound is clear with strong bass. The bass is not as powerful as the Sony MDR-XB40EX, but it's much better than the other brands. The XBs are still my favorite, but they're too good for the gym.The PIIQ are better suited for the gym, because they are light weight, and have a thin wires that don't transmit sounds as they dangle. Also, they have an innovative clip that grabs on to your ear lobes, which prevents them from moving around. Regular in-ear headphones will gradually work itself loose as you work out, affecting sound quality, but these do not. They're also easy to insert, because they have soft silicone seals. There are 3 different sizes, vs 2 sizes for many other brands.I just can't believe the sound quality compared to the others I've purchased. When I first tried them, I wasn't impressed, but when I fitted the correct seal, it was an eye opener. Some songs sound a bit shrill. For example, on Dido's White Flag, you can really hear the esses. Considering the price, these can't be beat.

de-primed twenty 30-06 cartridges that had been fired through a bolt action rifle. Used 20 ounces of de-ionized reverse osmosis treated water (not unlike how some bottled water is treated) and 1/2 oz of the Hornady brass cleaning solution, no heat, ran a five minute cycle with the 90 second de-gassing function and carbon in primer pockets already gone, ran another 20 minute cycle for the heck of it and done. Prior to purchase I read some reviews that said this product was a waste of money, but I'm satisfied. Keep in mind that my brass was not tarnished, corroded or very dirty to begin with.Some quotes from the instruction manual:It may or may not produce "shiny" brass. To create bright and polished brass we recommend using the Hornady Case Tumbler. Not all carbon buildup and tarnish may be removed from cases due to the corrosive nature of some primer residue and/or deep corrosion.Most brass cartridges do not need heat to efficiently clean. Using the heating function during prolonged cleaning cycles may actually cause tarnishing to appear.The heating function does not need to be used on cartridge brass and may actually cause tarnishing to appear

Dead simple to useThese things have been around for a while; I've always thought the short movie modes available from digital cameras would be enough. This beats that (though it's another device to carry) and is easy to use. Unboxing is just like an iPod nano, and the user experience is pretty close to Apple like once you've turned the device on. Press the big red button to start recording, and use the buttons that appropriately light up to adjust the zoom. Review your movies just as easy with the arrows and other aptly lit touch sensitive buttons on the face of the device.There were no issues with the USB port; as others have mentioned, a subsequent manufacturing revision has taken care of the issue reported in early reviews. Note I'm using a newer model MacBook Pro. I downloaded the newest software from their website to save future hassles, though I'm not a big fan of all the social networking features -- they'll work well for anyone looking for that sort of stuff. The videos are in native MP4 format and can be easily downloaded like any other USB mass storage device, easily viewable by QuickTime and iMovie -- so you can use Apple's tools instead if you're so inclined. In terms of the video quality, it all looks very reasonable -- I haven't done much video in recent years, so I will say it's certainly acceptable, but don't expect broadcast quality. Realize also that sound is mono, so may not sound as clear as normal stereo audio.There are some my folks at the office that are swearing by the Kodak Playsport -- due to the fact it's waterproof, has userswappable memory (via cheap SD cards), and can support 1080p -- for a lower starting price. Unless size is super important, I would seriously consider looking at that model when doing your research for a camera like this.I was hoping for a few more features, like being able to use an external microphone, or even having an optical zoom. Though I'm hoping for too much -- it would add complexity or price, moving the device away from it's target market. They could have included a power adapter or mini-HDMI cable, but no, that's an extra (fortunately I had these items on hand, generic parts work fine.) A winner for now, but Cisco better watch out for the competition. And before I forget, watch out for the warranty -- ONLY 90 days -- that is stingy, when most devices have a standard 1 year guarantee.

My Canon A85 was stolen from my car recently - and I needed a "point and shoot" camera of compact size when my Digital Rebel and camera bag are not needed or welcome.Canon no longer makes a compact "PowerShot" camera with CF Memory cards - and since I have a large investment in AA rechargables - I opted for this model over Canon models with a more compact lithium battery power source.Following the theme of the Powershot A70 - A95 - the camera is somewhat compact - except for the battery compartment holding 4 AA batteries. IT comes with 4 Alkalines - but you can go thru those in no time. Get yourself a set of rechargable 2300-2500 Amph/hr and be done with it. Additional thickness is added to the case to accommodate the swivel LCD viewer.Memory is located in with the battery compartment - the tiny SD card is spring loaded in a slot. I chose a standard speed 1 gig card - and shoot max resolution every time. I dislike mixing batteries and memory access on the same latch - having visions of AA batteries rolling loose everywhere ... lol... The camera has a need for greater power than ever - larger LCD viewer, more powerful flash and better, faster computer...The large LCD viewfinder is twist/movable in many directions. It is bright but strong sun will wash it out. The optical viewfinder is a good backup for strong sun situations.The built in speedlight/flash on the A630 continues Cannon's tradition of redeye - even with redeye setting utilized. The flash is more powerful than ever - as is the computer in auto setting - fastest setting yet - with fewer 'hunting' delays in low light situations.Picture quality is simply outstanding - vibrant oversaturation of colors - zoom lens adequate out to 4X - continue to stay away from that digital zoom past 4X...Overall - the A630 is a grown-up version of my A85. I like the swivel LCD viewer, faster computer, and familiar controls. It enables me to utilize my investment in rechargable AA batteries - and the whopping 8MP of picture quality gives me pause to take my 'digital Rebel' dSLR on vacation.Disappointed I am with the SD card in the battery compartment and the discontinuance of CF memory in the PowerShot line. On the positive side - I find no need to purchase the 10 MP model - the 8 MP of the A630 showing virtually no difference in picture quality in my camera use.

This is an initial review of the 64" model.  Possible updates to follow.  First, ordered from Amazon, delivery was prompt and safe, price was as good as anyone I could find.  Unit is well packed for shipping from Samsung.  I replaced a 50" Samsung plasma with this set.Here are my first impressions.............PROS:-picture is bright, colors appear accurate, black levels seem good-set is lighter than the smaller set it replaced-build quality seems to be excellent-aesthetics (without the stand) of the tv are very nice-HDMI outputs run parallel with the wall instead of at a right angle to minimize "stand out" of cable-no motion blur at all so far-screen "buzz" is within acceptable levels to me-the apps section starts fast, and is fairly clean and intuitive-Netflix app looks very good, is stable, and has 5.1 out via the optical connection-I prefer the little control "nub" on the back of the bezel....saves the finger prints from the touch sensitive are of my old set-OTA ATSC tuner is on par with others-PiP looks very good-speakers sound ok.....previous reviewers had me dreading thisCONS:-screen is VERY reflective....all plasmas are, but this one more so than my last...NOT an issue 90% of the time-HDMI ports are too far "inboard" from the edge of the set....will make temporary connections difficult-A/V (component/composite), and antenna connections are right angle to the wall-the remote takes ALOT to get used to......most people will be glad to have a universal-no way to learn IR from the supplied remote as it is Bluetooth-too much emphasis is placed on the SmartTV functions, just give me a great picture!!-some of the apps are very poor-black levels don't make my jaw drop-no 2nd tuner for PiPMany of the features of this model will have very limited, if any use for me.  I could have picked up the step down model, the 5300 series, but I wanted to stay with the higher quality set.  3D, HDMI ARC, the "OnTV" functionality, PiP, wireless, 80% of the apps, the Bluetooth remote, will not get used enough by me really appreciate or give detailed info of.All in all...........I am still a fan of plasma, even with it's shortcomings as the picture quality is the ultimate driver for me.  I do wish that Samsung would have made a set with the PQ of the 8500 series without all the extras.....just give us a stripped down monitor panel with the best picture available......but I realize that I am in the minority here.Any major changes in opinion, operation, or issues will be updated.

At first I thought this was an FM transmitter. It's not. It is a charger and line level audio out device. This combines a car charger and ⅛ inch male to male cable. It appears to be of high quality materials, particularly the jacks.Keep in mind that this is not any good for the iPhone 5 or 4th generation iPad or newer, so if you plan on buying any newer products, this won't be any good anymore.The audio controls are nice for when you are using a touchscreen device, like the iPhone, iPod Touch or newer generations of iPod Nano since it is tough to skip or pause songs which driving. While the buttons aren't super easy to identify without looking, it is workable.Another nice feature of this is that it uses the line level output for the audio rather than the headphone jack. The benefits of this are that you get an output that isn't affected by volume levels. I hate using the headphone jack in the car because it works best when turned up rather high, but when I later put in headphones they blast in the ears unless I get it turned down.Monster went out of their way to put some wire management into their design, which is nice. There is a place on the back side of the audio controls to wrap wires and a clip to hold the two wires together.I tested this with an iPhone 3GS, iPhone 4 and iPad 2 in my Toyota Camry. The Camry has a cigarette lighter adapter inside a compartment under the radio (not good for this) and a vertical one near the armrest. Since it is vertical, the controls are not at the ideal angle, but still usable. The play/pause, and rewind/fast forward buttons work exactly as you would expect. Touch the rewind/FF and it will advance tracks, hold rewind/FF and it will scan through the track, Now, as for the PREV/NEXT buttons, I'm not clear when they are supposed to do. If I push NEXT, it goes to a track outside of the current playlist that I asked the iPhone to play, but if I touch fast forward, it goes to the next track within that playlist. This isn't how the instructions say it is supposed to work. (Although looking inside the front flap of the box it does say that..)I next tried on my iPod Nano 1st generation and the buttons don't work at all, but the audio does still work.I then tried on my iPod Nano 6th generation, which worked like it should.Overall, this seems to be well made and does a good job, but won't work with all iPods or iPhones, so make sure to verify yours will work and know that this 30 pin connector is on its way out.

I boiled 2 cups of water at 4300 feet ASL and it only took 50 seconds to get to a raging boil using a MSR Pocket Rocket. I was worried my Pocket Rocket wouldn't fit on the pot, but the two fit together well. It balances on the heat exchanger best when there is water in the pot. Never heat the pot without anything in it, it will ruin the heat exchanger and anodization.The second test was to cook bannock in the fry pan. It burned and stuck even though I used plenty of grease and turned the stove down. It was hard to turn the bannock as the pan is so small.I think I'll use this pan in my kit mostly for boiling water. I may just use the fry pan as a bowl.The pot is just large enough for an 8 oz fuel canister to fit inside it, with a lighter underneath, and plenty of room on the top. The pocket rocket does not fit inside with a canister. If you get the optimus crux stove it all fits inside.All in all, a great little pot set. I look forward to using them.

I've been having a challenge with thinning middle-aged lashes, and yes it is a challenge for a lifelong glamour-girly girl . I went to local beauty supply and found these Invisiband and bought two different pairs. The "natural" and these "glamour".These glamour style make the look I am going for - the look of abundant natural lashes with mascara on them. I've seen too many reviews of many products where a) the product was used incorrectly, or b) the user had expectations far beyond the advertised features.I've used hand warmers for 10 years,skiing in VT, NH and ME.  I'm a storm and powder chaser, so I'm always out during some of the worst, coldest weather, typically for 6-7 hours, with maybe a total of 1 hour for breaks.They consistently work for me.  Why?- I take them out WELL IN ADVANCE of usage.  give them 15-20 mins minimum.  They need ambient air to activate.  Once they are in your shoes or gloves, they can't activate any further.- Check the expiration date on each package. My experience tells me if they have been sitting around in a store for a while, send them back if you don't have at least 2 years ahead stamped.  They only started putting date stamps on them in the last three years, so anything older than that is not stamped, for the most part.- you can always check how effective they are going to be by taking one pair still in the wrapper and bending it.  If it bends easily you're good to go.  If it is really "stiff" when you bend it (feels like a solid mass), don't use it.- I get about 2 years of shelf life, regardless of the claim.  I used the "stiff test" (see above.)- It doesn't seem to matter where they are stored.  I've had them in the 40 degree garage, and in the heated house, with no difference in longevity.Don't cry foul until you've played fair.

Tubes allow for a HUGE variety of creative exercises and at a fraction of what weights, weight racks, weight machines, etc cost. I have free weights and have used same since I was 13. So I know a bit about how muscles respond to stress. As long as you are applying progressive resistance you will see muscle growth. Resistance can be weights, tubes or even your own body weight. The muscle doesn't consider the source, it simply responds to the stress. I said that because many people believe that tubes and bands cannot build muscle. Simply untrue. I purchased this router as a replacement for a Netgear WGR614. The setup was pretty straightforward, and I had my network up and running in approximately half an hour. The setup wizard (included in the CD that comes with the router) helps you create a basic configuration, that can be fine-tuned by using the router's web interface.The router has a good wireless range, and it works well even with several brick walls between transmitter and receiver.As a bonus, if you upgrade the router's firmware to the newest version, the Secure Easy Setup (SES) feature will be enabled (the Cisco logo in the front panel is actually a button, which will glow amber when SES is enabled). This feature enables you to easily configure a secure wireless environment, provided that your wireless card driver also supports SES. Pressing the SES button in the router and in the card's software will automatically enable WPA (WiFi Protected Access) and choose a shared key for your router and PC.

That pretty much summarizes my experience with this netbook.Disclaimer- while most reviewers seem to rave about its performance as compared to Atom-based netbooks, I actually bought it as a temporary backup for a full-blown laptop that was in repair and, needless to say, I got what I paid for. I ended up returning it back to Costco once my laptop was fixed, so this review is based on the 2 months I was using this machine.I ran Win 7 Ultimate (32-bit) on it and tweaked CPU voltage using Brazos Tweaker to reduce heat and improve battery life by under-volting the CPU (which has no impact on CPU speed). I also installed all required HW drivers.Pros-1. price (i.e. cheap)2. compact charger.3. ability to play HD content (720p is the max I tried) with almost no delays/jerks. It does require using GPU as CPU is way underpowered to play HD video. In VLC there's an option "use GPU acceleration" which has to be enabled, otherwise the video will be jerky as expected on a 1Ghz CPU.4. HDMI portThe last 2 points are perhaps the biggest selling points of this netbook, as it makes a decent HD playback machine connected to your HDTV.Cons-1. Glossy reflective screen (though a very common LCD nowadays, I find it irritating to watch everything reflecting in it)2. Very slow- even rendering a bunch of open tabs in Firefox takes rather a long time. Same goes for opening/viewing large (10 megapixel+) JPG images in Irfan- there's a significant delay while scrolling between images in the folder. It was annoying enough for me not to bother with even basic image manipulation, but just dump all my photos on the drive and wait until I can do it on a faster machine.3. Didn't recognize 802.11 N-network (it didn't see my N-network, even know N-support is stated in the product description). My unit came with Broadcom WLAN card (apparently there're at least 2 different cards Acer ships these netbooks with)4. While there's supposedly a built-in microphone I was never able to use it- didn't even see a device in Device Manager.5. Glossy cover with with ugly "ripple" design that's also a magnet for fingerprints6. Colors- in comparison with my high-end laptop, the LCD colors appeared rather dull, but I suppose for most netbook users they will be of acceptable quality, if not above average (for a netbook).

In fact the next day when I returned these to my friend, I told him I bought some on Amazon last night.I simply love them, along with great sound they basically cut you off from the outside world with its sound isolation technology.  I thought my Bose were good at that and after using these I realized that Bose didn't do anything but let outside sounds be heard.  I also like how the actual speakers separate from the cable so after extensive use they can be replaced.The only downside I have had with these is the over ear part of using them, seems to be hard for them to keep their shape once in your ear. I prefer the Microsoft 3500 mostly because the scroll wheel has the "rachet" feel that allows you to scroll precisely. Besides that it's accurate and reliable for its price range. I suggest you learn to become mouse-ambidexterous. You can do it! It takes a few days to train your fingers, but is totally worth it when using wireless mice, allowing you to switch hands at will. With heavy use, the rubber side grips will eventually wear off. I like to rub a *very* thin layer of Gorilla Glue on the side panels and let it dry. It protects the rubber and gives them some texture.The blue light works well on a variety of surfaces, you can even use it on your leg. It does tend to devour the single AA battery if you're not religious about shutting it off. I'd suggest buying some Low Self Discharge rechargables for it. While I only get about week or two out of a rechargable, with LSDs you can always keep a fresh spare on hand to pop in when it dies. They quickly pay for themselves.

These deserve a five star rating because of their value.  They're price is excellent for the sound quality.  I have been buying Etymotic brand headphones for years now.  If you're familiar with those they can come at a price.  The Etymotic's have a crisp sound and have excellent noise isolation.  The Klipsch IMAGE S4 have the same crisp sound as the Etymotic's do but where they are better than the Etymotic's is the bass response.  You don't really hear any bass in the Etymotics but with the Klipsch the bass response is just as good as my Audio-Technica Studio headphones.What I don't like is the isolation bud.  I never really liked the gel type isolation in any headphone regardless of the brand because they feel very uncomfortable.  What I did was substitute the Klipsch buds for an Etymotic foam bud found here:http://www.amazon.com/Etymotic-Research-ER38-14F-Replacement-Eartips/dp/B0078JXDRI/ref=pd_bxgy_e_text_yand they are excellent.  I think I have now found the perfect headphones.  They sound excellent and are comfy.Edit April 16, 2013... I've had the headphones for about a week now.  I've used them in different environments (home, school, buses, airplanes) and they still sound great.  I previously mentioned that they have the same crisp sound as Etymotic's but after listening to all types of music I can hear a difference (I should mention that I've had Etymotic ER6i, MC5, and MC2 for years).  The Klipsch are not as crisp but the excellent bass response more than makes up for it.  Definitely still well worth the price.

I normally do my ab workouts at the gym, but was eager to try this Ab Carver Pro since it gives you the opportunity to do the workouts at home.  The unit is easy to put together.  You just snap in each of the handles to the side (marked left and right).  The tricky thing is that it's not easy to tell which side of the wheel is left vs right, so I actually snapped them in the wrong way at first.  But after trying to use the wheel, I knew they were reversed so I easily switched them.You simply put your knees on the knee pad, and get to work.  There are recommended workouts on their website, but you basically go straight, to the left, and to the right.  They recommend sets of 10 for beginners.  You can definitely feel the abs tighten up, and they will feel sore the next day.  You can do this on carpet or hardwoods. This is my review for the Sennheiser PX 90 headphones recently purchased. I respect Sennheiser quality and have used this brand for years, but I also realize that, as with most brands, there are some losers especially in their low-end line of products. The Sennheiser PX 90's don't seem to be of this category. These are very well made and stylish looking headphones that are not only attractive but quite functional. I bought these to use with a new laptop computer for listening to music and movies and the like. A lot of people use earbuds for this, but I have always preferred over the ear headphones for a number of reasons, chief among these is the shape of my ear canal makes for a difficult fit with earbuds. I also own the Sennheiser PX 100ii that I use for my ipod. In comparing the two headphones, I will say that the sound I get from the advanced technology built into the Sennheiser PX 100ii is superior owing to the fact that it has a far greater frequency response and signal to noise ratio than the (earlier developed) Sennheiser PX 90's. The PX 90's have a frequency response of 20 Hz to 20,000 kHz, whereas the PX 100ii's have a broader, deeper, richer frequency range of 15 Hz to 27,000 kHz which is just beyond the range of most human hearing. I'm not an audiophile, but I can tell you this. You may not be able to hear this entire range, but brother you can sure FEEL it! This is the main reason I rated the PX 90's less than 5 stars. So, why did I also get the lesser sounding PX 90's? They are actually better made than later models, so in theory, they should hold up longer and even if the frequency response is lesser it is still way beyond what a lot of other brand name headphones can handle. It also cost less than the Sennheiser PX 100ii cost. So, for the use I intend for them, I feel it was a smart decision. A few things more you should know: the cord on the Sennheiser PX 100ii is 4 ft as compared to only a 3.5 ft cord with the Sennheiser PX 90's... shorter, yes but very adequate for my needs. Also, the Sennheiser PX 90 cord does not have that little sliding cincher "doo-hicky" that was later added to the Sennheiser PX 100ii. This cincher allows for a better fit around your neck. This is a very minor detail, but thought you might like to know. Also minor, but should be mentioned, is the fact that the Sennheiser PX 90's weigh slightly more than the PX 100ii's (but, both are very light at around 4 oz.) Lastly, and a bit more important is the fact that you must use a bit more power from your amp/volume to achieve the best sound from your Sennheiser PX 90's than you ordinarily would with your PX 100ii. I suppose this is due to the advanced technology used to make the PX 100ii? But, in defense of the PX 90, the sound here is a warmer tone and, in my opinion, the bass is a bit more robust. So, whether you choose the Sennheiser PX 100ii or the Sennheiser PX 90, you will find advantages to either and I feel both are a good choice.  I hope this helps.

I wanted to like this lens because I always prefer lenses with a close focusing distance, and this is a rare macro 28mm (not a "true macro" but it focuses very close). Unfortunately I was never satisfied with the image quality of the photos. The lens is mush wide open, and it never gets really sharp across the board, even when stopped down. The bokeh has an ugly swirly quality that makes the background of close-ups unpleasant looking. It might be OK for landscape shots of mountains, etc. when used close to infinity and stopped way down, but then what's the point of having a fast "macro" lens if you can't use it at the wide apertures and minimum focus distance?I used it on a full frame camera but it would make a good "normal" focal length lens on a crop sensor camera, if the optics were better. Instead I would opt for the Sigma 30mm f/1.4 lens which is actually usable wide open. Sigma has some great lenses but this isn't one of them. Unlike another reviewer, I found the build quality and autofocus performance to be just fine, but the optics to be sub-par; others have found the opposite.If you are going to buy only one wide angle lens, make it theZeiss 21mm f/2.8 Distagon T* ZE Series Lens for Canon EOS Digital SLR Cameras.Yes, it costs a small fortune, but it's worth every penny. Sell some other lenses if you have to; the Zeiss 21 can replace all of your wide angle lenses, it's so good--even if you need to crop them a bit to a 24 or 28mm field of view. There is something very special and even magical about the Zeiss 21 that has to be experienced; it makes you not want to use any other wide angle lens once you've tried it.

Well okay I'm good with her painting it in, I've seen her work so let's do it.  Um, not such a good idea!! She paints my entire head (roots) in a 6N & leaves it on waaayyyy too long & I'm looking like some old trashy gal that needs a root touch up REALLY bad!! UGHH I can't tell you how bad it was!  I was a level 9, some lowlights & BLACK roots (you pull my hair in a pony & I'm wearing a black band around my head--seriously.  My boyfriend later tells me "Wow, your roots were BLAAACCCCKKK! Leave it to men to be honest about the wrong things...anyway there's the damage, here's the repair....I go to another friend that can barely fit me in, she's good with color & is booked solid but does me a favor.  She highlights (not what I wanted in September) to break up the color but the roots are still there.  Great, thanks, tolerable but later I decide this is just not good enough for me.  I grab my Loreal in 8 natural blonde(I figure if I try to pull it too high at one time I'll go orange) & try it on my roots only even though she warns me it won't work.  Guess what?  It didn't work.  Barely put a ding in that damn dark color.  Ugh.  I know if I keep going (color, highlights, color...and so on) I will be doing some damage.  SO....I look on Amazon, I love Loreal color & consider the color oops thing & hey maybe it IS great but I see the reviews on this product here & think I should do it.  I try the shampoo method (you will see there are two ways to do it) 1/2 oz #1 bottle, 1/2 oz #2 bottle & 1 oz of shampoo.  I apply to my roots, leave in for about 20 mins. & it looks good but a little bit orangy at the roots (nothing I haven't experienced for a day or two when I've colored to light too fast before & I know it fades (at least for me) with a few washings.  But it says you can try 2-3 times so I go for it again...same method for about 10 minutes & this time all over my head.  Guess what?  I'm back to what I was before this whole fiasco, a warm level 9 that looks as natural as fake color can be. :) My hair does feel a little dry but only in the spots that I just had the highlights, I'll be deep conditioning under my dryer once a week & using the night repair from Redkin & I'll be fine.  Also, I barely went through any of the product but will keep in on hand in case I get stupid again.  Holy c**p.  Longest 4 days EVER!!!Keep in mind:*Do NOT run out & highlight hair that is too dark unless you wanted to do that anyway.  Money & damage to your hair that is unneccessary!*Do NOT let a stylist or cashier at Sally's talk you out of buying this (woman at the register said all knowingly "This will turn you orange".  I may go back there & say...REALLY?? While flipping my hair*Do NOT try to color over, you run the risk of no change but more damage, uneven color, going ashy grey-green or brassy or even better as I did once MANY years ago kind of burgandy (thank God I was young!).*Try this & if after the first time it hasn't done much you may not have left it long enough (I sat under a dryer for 10 of the 20 minutes the first use & about half the time of the second run. End result...better than I hoped!Best of Luck & Sorry this was so long, I'm running on the adrenaline of relief!

Further Update September 2006,This is a great product! With the software upgrades (I'm at 1.3.0.176 now), the performance has gotten a lot better. I've got a stream in the middle east of about 300-360kbs and its great. I would change the stars to 5 now but I can't on the Amazon system, it's definetly a 5 star purchase. I could use audio only as an emergency backup to still "catch" my favorite team, The Jacksonville Jaguars! Highly recommended for those who are traveling away from home, Expat's (like me) or those who are looking for maximum stuff to show off to their friends for only $200.00. Slingbox Rocks!!!!!Update March 2006, I'm using my Slingbox to good effect. I had to upgrade my ISP bandwidth but it was cheaper than getting a satellite (and a TV set) and I'm cheap so I went for the Slingbox. I have a cable box DVR and broadband at my American home. You will need about 350k/sec bandwidth for really smooth video, I had when I wrote the below review bandwidth that varied wildly between 20k/sec (wont work) to 150 k/sec (workable, but choppy). So your saying "so what thats easy bandwidth to get in the states"; true, I'm just commenting on the sales pitch that you can watch TV ANYWHERE. Well if your in the 3rd world you may not be able to (one ad I saw talks about using this in China, I have not been there since the internet got big but I'm not too sure about that. I did try to watch some Chinese internet TV streams recently and they did not seem to be to strong. So I doubt their infrastructure is up to western standards, and I guess it all depends on whose circuits are between you and your home). Where I am at now I've got it running on a stable 200k/sec and its a little jerky video but sound is good and its watch-able compared to the alternative (Nothing!). Thanks Slingbox for bringing America to me wherever I am. Highly recommended especially for the price! It's always cool to have the first gadget on the block too; I know myco-workers are jealous!Below is my original review from December 2005:I'm in the Middle East and the Slingbox people were really cool enough to let me "test" from their Slingbox, to see the quality. It was kind of rough but I'm going for it anyway because I miss watching sports other than soccer (yeach). The quality was totally dependent on my side and I happen to have a low end DSL transfer rate around 100kbps. I just wanted to put that out for world travelers especially outside Europe and Japan, you may not get the 5 star picture you would like. For 200.00 or so I'll buy it anyway, not that much to lose and a whole lot of entertainment to gain!

I recently switched over to an all Mac ecosystem and this router was the final piece. I have been using a MacBook Pro for three years because of my job, but I had PC's in the house. I used to stream a lot of media to my TV from my desktop, so I upgraded to a Belkin N + router so I could more easily stream HD content. It worked great with my PC's, but my Pro would lose the Internet all the time and very randomly. It was on the network, just no Internet signal. For a while I was just disconnecting and reconnecting to the network and that solved the problem (but I was constantly doing it). I just dealt with it until I replaced our other computers with Mac and they had the same problem. Our iPhones were also dropping so it was very clear the Belkin just didn't play nice with Apple.  Knowing it was the router and not the computers, I decided to get this router and the experience was great.Set up was of course very simple. I just plugged in my Xbox 360 and PS3 directly to the ports on the back, then I connected the Internet. I did have to install software for the Airport which was a little different, but it was no problem. I connected my Apple TV wirelessly as I did with our laptops. A few days later the light turned amber so I checked the software and there was a firmware update. I assumed I would have to hard wire my laptop to the router like I did when I installed it, especially considering every other router I have ever owned needed to be connected directly to the computer for the update. However, I was very surprised to learn that I could download the update wirelessly and then install it wirelessly. It was very simple and indicative of the typical "it just works" Apple experience. Neither my computers nor my iPhones have ever dropped a signal.I can't really speak to the range too much as everything is on the same floor. However, I live in an apartment and I can pick up the network from the main lobby which is a considerable distance from the router. My 2nd Gen Apple Tv takes advantage of the dual ban automatically, which frees up the network for my non N or 5 ghz network items. I would definitely recommend this to those who use Macs. However, there are cheaper options out there if you just use PC's.
Hoping not to have to use but it is well worth the reassurance that if something does happen it will be covered!
But it could be great with a little more effort in the designing of the product. Thanks, but no thanks.

I liked several things about this oil, including:The scent, which is slightly musky/nutty sweet, but not overpowering. In short, it's a lovely aroma. (Contains coconut oil and sweet almond oil, plus "fragrance", which explains the scent combo, I think.)It can be used on damp or dry skin. I prefer it on damp skin. Used this way, you can massage the oil in, then pat dry, and your skin will feel hydrated for most of the day.(On dry skin the oil "sits" too long, not absorbing quickly enough.)A little goes a long way. (I pour a small --quarter size) amount in my hand, and spread it over damp skin. This covers entire torso. Use less for your arms if you try this.)BUT...There were a couple of things I didn't care for, including:The spray applicator releases in more of a STREAM than a fine spray. So you get a "blob" of oil if you use it with the spray. (As I mentioned, I'm a "pour and massage" type, but I tried the spray.)Mineral oil is the first ingredient. And it DOES feel sort of like baby oil. In other words, it feels "heavy" and slightly oily. (Unlike, say, Neutrogena body oil, Light Sesame Formula, unscented, which I've also used, and which feels light on the skin.)Those two things were enough to remove two stars, especially because I think some users will object to the scent,  the mineral oil, the heavy feel, and the fact that it is not truly a "dry" oil.

I have had folding knives all of my life, comes from being born and raised on a farm/ranch. Since my Marine son had shown me a Tanto blade knife, I've had a hankering for one. I found his CRKT on the internet, read the reviews, and checked the price. Then I went to Amazon.com and sure enough, it was less expensive, even with shipping. The blade was sharp out of the box, but I touched up the edge with my Lansky sharpener and was able to shave hairs off of my arm within minutes. The edge lasts a long time, and touch up is quick and easy. I carry this knife all day, from sunup to sundown. I like the size and weight, it's not too heavy, but still not a featherweight to where I'm wondering if it's really there. I like a bit of heft in my tools.One footnote: The three screws that attach the pocket clip to the knife became a bit loose after a couple of months. Not "falling out and losing them" loose, just enough that I noticed the clip was moving side-to-side a bit. My fix was to remove the screws one at a time and put a drop of blue Loctite on them. This has become my favorite knife, hands down.

I bought this unit to replace a very similar Uniden model that developed problems after several years of service.  I like this style of phone and this one has a few improvements as well as one or two drawbacks versus my old Uniden.PROS:  Headset is comfortable and vocal quality is extremely good.  Has a generous talk time of up to 10 hours and the battery status is displayed when not on a call.  Lightweight and easy to carry in pocket or clipped to belt/waistband.  Orange backlight is quite bright.  Stores 70 phone numbers.  Has a groove in the back of phone for the headset cord that allows the belt clip to hold the cord snugly in place without any strain on the plug.  I love this feature because after dropping my old phone and catching it by the cord several times over the years, it was the headset jack that ultimately failed.CONS:  Does not have any speed dial - you have to do a search in the phone book to pull up a number to dial.  Mute and volume controls are on the phone.  My previous phone had a Plantronics headset where these features were built in-line on the phone cord, which was much more convenient than fumbling with the phone.  Belt clip does not open very wide and could break if forced.  Caller ID only stores each number once -- so if someone called you 5 times, only their last call would be recorded in Caller ID memory and you'd have no record of the other 4 calls.  The base unit's antenna is supposed to be a holder for the headset when not in use, but is too short for this task when using the over-the-head version - the headset gets jumbled up with the base unit.Despite the cons, overall I'm very happy with this phone.  The vocal quality is a vast improvement over my prior set and I really appreciate the long talk-time and being able to see the battery status.  I knocked one star off the rating for the lack of speed dial, the inconvenience of the mute and volume controls, and the limited amount of spring in the belt clip.

After spending an evening with the dock (and still without a working dock), I can safely say: don't waste your time or money.First off, the price of the dock is a lie. Instead of a dock, what you receive is a cheap piece of plastic and a few cheap USB & audio cables. Inorder to make this crap an actual dock, you need to purchase the following: An extra magsafe power adapter: (...) AND an official Apple Display Port to DVI (or HDMI) adapter. 3rd party plugs won't fit: (...)So, to even use the dock, you're already set back the cost of the dock plus about another $110, bring you just about to the same price point as other docks out there that are actually made of metal and not junk. I can see the need to buy those things as optional for some people, but unless you plan on leaving your macbook in the dock forever (defeating the purpose of the dock) and you have no need to have video out on the mac (and remember this dock can only be used with the mac closed), then you'd have no need for those extra cables. For everyone else - which I assume is, you know, everyone - , they are required.Ignoring the fact that this product is a NERF dockesque shell in disguise, there is still much to be hated about it. Assembly is required.  That's right, you build this thing yourself. The dock comes with the exact amount of slots for your specific macbook pro that you select ahead of time, yet for whatever reason, the manufacturer thought it would be more fun if you insert them yourself, carefully mount the machine, then screw in the plugs. They aren't special plugs either (unless you count the ethernet plug where the clip was broken off. It's "custom", hell yeah!). You're basically putting screws inside of normal USB plugs that electricity runs through. Brilliant!I could forgive all of the problems with the dock if it actually looked as slick as the pictures, but it doesn't. The overly shiny plastic just looks cheap sitting atop your desk. Don't expect any complements.So, unless you have an extra magsafe power adapter and official display port to dvi plug laying around anyway (plus 2 hours of free time), don't bother with this thing. Save yourself aggravation and stress and just get a real dock.

When it says CHILLED on the packaging, they aren't kidding.I rub one pump-full on my hand and rub it into my neck, wrists and inside my elbows. It feels a bit tacky for a moment, but then it absorbs into my skin and feels very smooth.I wish this product had a neutral scent - as it is, it smells like guys' cologne. I keep using it anyway, since it works to put a stop to the hot flashes in the moment, and for quite some time afterwards.My sense is that it is the menthol in the ingredients that provides the chill, although the gel-like texture probably also contributes to the cooling sensation, as it evaporates. I also see the formulation has cucumber extract and a few strong antioxidants like grape extract, green tea extract, vitamin E, and vitamin A, among others. So not only is it cooling, but there are anti-irritant qualities to counteract the inherent irritant qualities of the menthol, as well. It's like putting vitamins on my skin.This gel is amazing, and I am very thankful to have stumbled upon it. I think Axe could make a mint if they took out the fragrance and re-packaged it for perimenopausal women!5 stars for cooling effectiveness! This works better than the Poise roll-on product intended for hot flashes.

These are some good quality BBs, I got 20,000 of them, because I normally share and go through them a lot. I had previously bought some bio-BBs and it ended up jamming my gun a lot, so I cleaned out my guns and bought these instead, they feed properly and my guns haven't jammed yet. As far as I'm concerned, that's an A+ in my book. .2g BBs are the base line gramweight for me, I don't use anything lower than that, for many reasons like: .20-.35 BBs are usually made with better quality than other weights, .12 and .15 aren't always the right size (either exactly 6.00MM, or normally around 5.97-5.99MM diameter) and the higher weights have less imperfections, like seams. These were mildly polished. It might be good to wash or polish them yourself again, just to get any dust off that may have gotten in the bag inbetween being first polished and being sealed.

My hair is dry, frizzy, and tends towards breakage and damage. I stopped using most strongly chemical shampoos several years ago. But recently I've had some serious itchy scalp issues... I think it's partially related to a new job where the air is very dry and partially related to another product my hair didn't like.I went hunting for a product which would get rid of the problem a bit more naturally, and this makes a really nice daily cowash. I've been using the shampoo once or twice a week. I had scaly looking white flakes with an occasional yellowish tinge, and a constantly itchy scalp. The symptoms are diminishing every day, and mostly now after about a month I have smaller white flakes and occasional itching.This product contains most of the natural ingredients thought to help for serious dandruff. Salicylic Acid, lemon, and tea tree oil. It also does not smell like dog shampoo. It smells kind of like canned carrots cooking on a stove with a slight lemon cleaner undertone. It's not fresh baked cookies or anything, but it's kind of nice.The only con is that my local stores don't carry it and the shampoo comes in a two pack on amazon for $7 while this product conditioner costs a little over $5 per bottle. That seems out of whack.

I received this case as a freebie that came with my awesome Zune 80. I can't say I would have paid the $50 that it was selling for at the time (but I wouldn't pay that much for any case, period). That being said, this is a very high-quality leather case. Both inside and out, it's well-crafted and snuggles the Zune safely inside.I can access the squirkle and left/right buttons easily. While there is no plastic cover over the screen, this is of no concern due to the durable glass Microsoft smartly gave the Zune screen.It's almost perfect. There are two issues that keep it out of 5 star territory. First, you must remove the Zune in order to access the USB connection. Being that this is the Zune's lifeline to power and content, removing it is done on a frequent basis. And when attached to my home or car kits, the case must be tossed aside. So it is a carrying case in the purest form. It's only good when carrying the Zune.Secondly, since removing it must be done all too often, it's irksome that the headphones must be disconnected in order to free the player from the case. So there is a lot of disconnecting, removing, reconnecting, disconnecting, inserting, etc., etc., etc. Not very convenient.It would take only small modifications to make this case the end-all be-all of cases, but as it stands: it's a quality case with a few too many compromises. If I had to shell out cash for a case, I'd take a hit on quality for a more ergonomic design.

Check out the 2013 reports and you will find out that this did not offer protection in compare to the Target brand regardless of all their claims and all their good ingredients... I use many brands and I will not use Target's formula but there are many other brands out there with good ingredients and great protection. Target, Hawaiian Tropic, Copper tone, etc. may offer protection but offer many unwanted chemicals as well. Bottom line, what ingredients are you looking for and how well it protects you for what you pay per ounce. The Active Ingredient in this baby is a whopping 11% titanium dioxide and wow that is probably the only sunscreen that has that formula... One tube of this is all I was good for.Sunscreens are worse than processed foods (which I never consume) since there is no nutritional guide for sunscreens. Producers write whatever they want and it really consumes time to read up and compare facts. Unless you happen to be a marathon swimmer who is going to be soaked for hour and hours in the oceans' salty water without any shade, which Vaseline will act as the best sealer there is, then for a surfer like me, usually higher organic ingredients are the best. What I put on my skin is the same as consuming it through my skin since it is absorbed by my skin. I don't intend to have the sunscreen for snack but it is good to know that there are fewer chemicals in the sunscreen. Usually those SPFs will not be over 30 for any safe sunscreen ingredients and it will be for BABYs. I just happen to be a baby who tries to read ingredients. I wear a rash guard when surfing which covers my upper body so I am protected all the time. What worries me is my face. For a small investment, sunscreens may offer me the possibility of avoiding sun burn, pain, cancer, medical bills, pain killers, etc. that come with exposure to the sun. I try different brands all the time and look for the following pointers;1. I avoid water proof or water resistant. How stupid, I am going in the ocean, in salt water under the sun. Even if I put a deck coating or a roof on my skin, it will not last. Anyone looking for those wordings should avoid water,2. Natural sunscreens with FEWER chemicals. Manufacturers like to spin the facts and challenge our knowledge and intelligence by claiming it as WITHOUT any harsh chemical or chemical FREE or without chemical ABSORBERS. So stupid since a chemical is a chemical regardless,3. FRAGRANCE FREE; It must spell out on the face of the container. Majority have fragrance but baby sunscreens usually do not. After all, I am going in the ocean not on a date. Why do I need fragrance regardless, and this is from an athlete where I consume fresh garlic napping in vinegar every single day,4. EXPIRATION DATE; I never leave the sunscreen container in the sun, purchase new sunscreen all the time so I never worry about few months old, I always apply 30 minutes before the first exposure to the sun5. BROAD SPECTRUM; It must spell out on the face of the container,6. PABA free; It must spell out on the face of the container,7. I avoid OXYBENZONE which apparently disrupts human hormonal system,8. I avoid vitamin A (retinyl palmitate) which seems to boost skin cancer risk,9. I Look for ACTIVE INGREDIENTS of more zinc less titanium the best-rated mineral sun blocks. Majority have many different ingredients than zinc and titanium as ACTIVE INGREDIENTS,10. Unscented, Baby Formula, and must have the blessing of the Environmental Working Group's annual Sunscreen Guide.Sunscreen needs to protect and needs to protect for a reasonable price. Vanicream 60+ offers Titanium Dioxide (7.5%), Zinc Oxide 7.5%... Baby Blue Lizard offers Titanium Dioxide 5% and Zinc Oxide 10%. I need more zinc baby not Titanium dioxide and Blue Lizard offers more zinc, less titanium for half the cost... Vanicream 30 and 35 have less zinc so those are worthless, unless if you want to go by what Consumer Report recommends which rates protection without any consideration for ingredients in the sunscreen then in that case, I can get roofing tar for less money, so we might as well consider that... Zinc is the solution, more zinc for less money with organic ingredients that are not carcinogen or harmful to me... That is why Badger is better than most... Blue Lizard 30 is not as good of ingredients as Badger but still better than Vanicream 60+. Badger SPF 30 All Natural Baby Sunscreen Lotion 4oz This Badger is one of the best that I have ever used since it is 100% certified Natural, 87% certified organic and a whopping 12% zinc oxide that is easy to apply, rubs in quick, not greasy, offers me protection and is priced reasonably per ounce. The only draw back is that I see the whiteness on my skin. Regardless this is one of the best for my skin and surfing, one of the champs alright. Bravo Badger.

I purchased a 500GB Momentus XT Hybrid for $99.99 back in December of 2010. Whe the device arrived, I installed it and loaded Windows XP Pro, with over 17 years in the computer I.T. field, I was confident this wouldn't be a problem. The drive worked very well UNTIL early March 2011. I took a firmware update, SD25, (Recommended by the Seagate site) and within 24 hours the drive was dead. The online RMA process at the Seagate website was NOT friendly (I avoid speaking with non-technical people regarding computer problems, if not especially returns, if at all possible). You have to navigate through quite a few links to get to the actual RMA page(s). I filled out the RMA form, shipped the drive to TX, USA and waited. While it wasn't fast turn around for the replacement drive, another drive was shipped. The drive I received was NOT a new drive. Hence, I spent 99.99 PLUS return shipping for someone else's problem. (This policy in corporate America must stop). If I had wanted to adopt someone else's problematic drive, I would have purchased a used one from somewhere else. The 'REFURBISHED' drive, firmware SD24, I receieved wasn't recognized by BIOS or Windows XP (SP3) on boot. Once my computer started I ran Seagate's downloadable software called SeaTools. To my amazement, SeaTools was able to get the drive recognized, hence making it accessible to the Windows OS. However right away the drive was making the dreaded 'chirping' noise which is an indication that things are about to go down hill quickly for the drive. I went through the hassle of transfering my entire system from my old drive to the 'REFURBSIHED' drive I was sent by Seagate. Within 3 days, the 'REFURBISHED' drive was not technically dead but re-initializing every 30 seconds or so and Windows would no longer fully boot due to disk errors, registry corruption, etc. So, I once again navigated Seagate's website, paid another $10.00 shipping which brings my total investment to $129.00 for a drive I can't use and is no longer 'new'. I HIGHLY recommend you visit the forums for this or any other potential purchase before buying to get a better view of what problems you will have. I also recommend you find out what the RMA process is before hand as well as what the company's policy is regarding returns (Will you get a new drive, used drive, etc). This is a review, not an opinion, I have stated only the facts of my experience with the Seagate Momentus XT product. Decide for yourself.

*Update*  A reviewer recommended DxO software (dxo.com) for this lens, so I tried out the demo first with these raw files from the zoo.  Unreal results.  Just amazing.  Prints are simply beautiful.  You have to try to believe.What a great zoom lens.  I prefer primes and really wanted a 100/2.0 to go along with my 50/1.4 for my 5D.  However, I got a great deal on the DO zoom so jumped on it because of its versatility.It has to be one of Canon's most controversial/misunderstood zoom lens. The price is one factor and softness the other.  The 70-200/2.8 IS takes better pictures (out the box) with less computer work needed later.  However, this lens is heavy and white and needs a tripod.  But it has terrific quality.It takes time to appreciate this DO lens.  I got so many blurry images in the beginning that I almost took it back.  But I decided to take pictures all day to figure it out before my daughter's kindergarten class went to the zoo. This is what I discovered:-best sharpness  between 70-200 is f/5.6.-between 200-300 is f/11 and f/16-Image Stabilization is amazing-auto focus is lighting quick and accurate-size and weight are amazing for this focal rangeNeedless to say, the pictures I took at the zoo were terrific hand held. Because it was a sunny day, shutter speeds were high and the images were blurry free.  Shot in RAW and WOW when I opened them up.  This was "L" class all the way.  Now, I understood the price/performance advantage of this lens. After five hours at the zoo and double checking that my group of kids were still with me, I realized it was never obtrusive.  There is no way in hell one could have used the 70-200/2.8 IS for that long around their neck and shoulder!Kudos to Canon for making this technological wonder.  Just shoot RAW and the softness goes away and the images are "L" quality.  This lens will be with you all the time ready to go.  That's priceless in my book!

I tried thisDove Men+Care Hydrate + Shave Gelalong with theDove Men+Care Sensitive + Shave Geland I find that I prefer THIS variety.  They are both good products for the price and do much the same thing.  I would recommend either one to someone looking to try out a new shave gel or somebody looking for a gel that will help them get a closer hsave without irritation.  Both have a mild scent that is much less noticeable than the Axe Shave Gels.  Both types saturate the beard well too, helping you get a closer shave on the first pass with the razor.  This is nice because the closer you can shave on the first pass, the less likely you are to nick yourself or "burn" your face with repeated swipes of the razor.I liked this variety a little bit better because my face felt noticeably smoother afterwards.  Both were good at protecting my face and leaving it feeling shaved close but not tight or "burned".  This Hydrating formula seemed to do it a little bit better and therefore this is the can I would reach for when I run out of Shave Gel.  I think the razor you use makes a difference as does whether or not you use a post shave balm so when I tried them side by side, I used the same razor,Gillette Fusion Proglide Power Razor, 1-count Packageand I refrained from using any post-shave balm or moisturizer.  The Hyrdating Gel simply felt better.  I compared these every other day for two weeks before reaching that conclusion.  It could be the Sunflower Oil in the formulation that helps give you the extra smoothness.Considering that the Dove Shave Gel is priced competatively with brands I have used for years with lesser results, it makes sense to switch and never look back.  I like the Axe shave gels I have tried, such as theAxe Shave Gel, Chilled, 7 Ouncebut the Axe shave gels all have a stronger, more noticeable scent.  Even though their scent dissipates over time, the Dove scent is barely there, leaving you free to wear whatever scents you choose to without having to work around the spiciness of the Axe gels.  I never thought of Dove as a men's line before but I'll look twice at their products in the future after the good results I got here.

While this it not as heavy duty a product as a comparable bag from Maxpedition (600 Denier fabric versus Maxpedition's 1000 Denier) the stitching and welting (material used to cover exposed ends of fabric panels) is comparable and the bag is quite rugged and the perfect size for a day bag or SHTF bag.  Before you think this is a knock I assure you that it is not.  When compared to a similar Maxpedition bag (The Sabercat Versipack) it doesn't... quite... match it for quality but what it DOES have going for it is that this bag is easily 80% of the quality of the Maxpedition bag but is easily less than 30% (~$32 vs ~$125) of the cost of the Maxpedition bag.  It has PALS webbing stitched into place in strategic areas all over the bag which will allow you to customize the bag quite nicely through the addition of external pouches or clipped on gear... for example, one of the things I have clipped onto the bag is a NiteIze Pocket Pouch holding a multitool, a small set of drivers for the multitool, a very nice little flashlight, a 6" stainless steel mechanics ruler, a DPMS non-marring AR takedown tool, and a CRKT M16 folder.  And everything is being supported quite easily by the bag.  On one side of the bag is a surprisingly roomy pocket while the other side, which you can't see in the pictures, has five sewn on heavy duty PALS straps that would allow you to quickly and relatively easily secure the bag to any pack or tactical vest with tactical webbing.  The bag is quite roomy in every pocket and quite well made.  Do I have the Maxpedition Sabercat?  Yes.  But can afford to keep a Sabercat loaded with SHTF gear in every vehicle I own?  No.  My Maxpedition Sabercat cost, with the exception of my backup handgun, almost half of the total cost of the bag and gear.  BUT, I CAN easily keep one of these bags full of emergency gear (first aid kit, Eton Scorpion weather radio/flashlight, multitool, flashlight, etc...) in every vehicle my family owns.  And this bag is slightly larger and can hold more than the Sabercat.  It does NOT have the waistbelt that the Sabercat has but since I never use that particular feature (I always use the shoulder strap) that is not exactly a minus since I find the waistbelt annoying MOST of the time (I'll probably be extremely grateful for it if I ever need it... the one thing that has kept me from finding some way to remove it).  In summary, this is a well made, rugged bag with a lot of potential expansion options, a lot of storage space, and would be a great deal at twice the price.

This product is very harsh for me. For several years I have used and been happy with Rapid Lash by Promaxyl. For whatever reason, Amazon stopped carrying Rapid Lash for several months and then when it returned, it was more expensive. I decided I would try another lash product at or around the same price with comparable reviews. Unfortunately, that was a big mistake! Instead of heeding the reviews that mentioned redness and irritation, I thought I would be fine since I had been using a similar product for years with nothing more than a bit of hyperpigmentation on my upper lids. After one use, I noticed redness, itchiness and an uncomfortable burning sensation not unlike sunburn on my eyebrows and eyelashes. After the second use, I woke up in the middle of the night from a sound sleep, itching my eyes and feeling like I was having a hot flash only in the eye area. Needless to say, I have not continued and since it's an opened product, the Amazon retailer I purchased from will not work with me in any way. I am out forty dollars but I plan to send this back to the original company with my complaints, hoping that they will do a better job in the future making a product that can accommodate those with sensitivities.

I bought these based on reviews all over the net. They have such high ratings and people seem to love them, I thought, surely I need these!No, I didn't. They're  not bad. I love the flow as you line your lid. Very gel like texture and is easy to get a smooth line. It also has very intense color with one stroke, you do not need to go over the same place more than once. It smudges well if you're going smokey, and it stays super crisp if you're going more bold.What I don't like though, are two things:One, I find that two of the colors are just too dark. Demolition (Dark brown) and Empire (purple), are just black on my skin. I'm a super pale redhead with brown eyes. I would think that with such light skin you would see the slightest bit of color, but the hint you do see, goes away completely after you apply mascara, even if you have a thicker line of the eyeliner on. I put Zero (black) on one eye and Empire on the other, and couldn't see a difference once mascara was on. I was going to go over the purple with the black after my experiment, but it honestly wasn't necessary, I wore it that way all day. No one said anything and no matter what lighting I was in, I couldn't see a difference. I should mention, this was with no eyeshadow to compete with color, just eyeliner and mascara.Two, I have to curl my eyelashes and then apply the eyeliner. If I do it in my normal sequence, no matter how long I wait, the eyeliner just comes off onto the curler. A straight transfer, it doesn't smudge, it takes it off to the skin leaving a weird spotted liner on my lid and would have to redo it. I've never had this problem with any eyeliner before. So I've just learned to do it in reverse with this product.Pros:-Great texture-Intense saturation of color-Stays put all day without fading or smudging-Three of the colors, as well as the black are just lovely. Smog is my favorite, great on a brown-eyed redhead-Very easy to use/apply/smudgeCons:-Two of the colors (that aren't black) just look black-Comes off on my eyelash curler in sheetsAll in all, I wish I would have just purchased Smog in a single pencil.

My fingers go to far around to get a tight grip. It reminds me of a paint stirrer; it's a little thicker but about the width. I know this won't be much help to women reading this review; but the men who are most likely to be affected by this, will know what I'm getting at.It's a good product at a very reasonable price; only the most delicate skin would have a problem with the bristles.And I have to address the only one star rating. In that rating the person complain of rust. I'm not sure that person was reviewing the right product; there is nothing that could possibly rust. It's all plastic. Rust will almost certainly not be a problem.

I got my first flat on my new bike today - I've changed many tires and always wished my pump had a tube, a gauge, and a way to use the ground for resistance while pumping.  This pump is amazing.  It took no time at all to figure out how to use it, get the tire pumped back to 120psi, and get back on the road.  My only reasons for giving it 4 stars is the bracket can't be mounted along-side a water bottle cage, so I took the bracket from my other Topeak pump which doesn't really fit perfectly, but it does OK for now - my bike doesn't have a round top tube, so I can't use the zip ties to mount it under the top tube, and if I didn't mount it along side the bottle cage, I'd have to sacrifice a bottle cage.  It's also a very long pump - just something to keep in mind.Addendum 8/29/2010:  I called Topeak and asked if they made a side-mount bracket for the pump.  They DO make one, but it costs an extra $6 plus shipping.  I mentioned my displeasure to the person on the phone and he took off the shipping charge. Then, after I had already ordered it from Topeak, I saw a generic clip at Performance Bikes for $2 The pump itself is still a great piece of gear, so the pump gets 5-plus stars, but the bracket system gets a poor review.

I have to start off by saying that I usually have pretty cheap, old hairdryers... I will keep one until it literally will not work anymore, even if it's not that great.  So with my limited knowledge of great products I will say that I LOVE this hairdryer! Right off the bat, it looks like it is made well, it feels like it is made well (it's heavy!) and it sounds like it is made well.  The sound of the motor is smooth and definitely less jarring than other hairdryers.  The product page says that it will dry your hair 50% faster than some other dryers, but after using it and comparing it to my old, ratty machine I don't think it dried my hair all that quicker.  I do agree with the description that it helps with frizz and smooths hair.  My hair did feel smoother and looked much better after I dried it with this machine than my previous.  I didn't have to do as much styling after because it helped to sculpt it the way I wanted while drying. The two speed options are nice and the high setting is powerful.  I don't use attachments and I don't use the cooling setting, so I can't comment on their usability.  Overall, I really like this dryer and can't think of a single thing I would change about it.  It may have actually won me over into purchasing higher quality dryers in the future!

Pro's.  Runs about 10-15C cooler than previous PNY 465.  Super fine rendering in gaming.  An increase of about 15-20FPS.  Installed on a Dell XPS 8300 core I7 2600 with 6gigs of ram.Cons - $$$$$$$This review is strictly for FS9 and FSX users.  I bought this card to replace my Nvidia PNY 465.  I chose EVGA because they seemed to be the best rated card I could find.  Was considering the Nvidia 480 but reviews said this card ran cooler and used less power.  Also Passmark rated it a wee bit faster.I'm comparing this card to the Nvidia 465 and using Microsoft Flight Simulator as a gauge because it's the only game I play.  Anyway, everyone knows FSX is a resource hog and using my Nvidia 465 I could get frame rates of about 22-25 with all setting set to maximum with a screen resolution of 1920 x 1080.  Turning off ambient lighting and ground shadows increased FPS to 33-35.  Turning off airplane traffic raised that to well in the 40's.  Because I'm a fps fanatic, I wanted to play the game with ALL sliders set to max and a minimum of 30fps.  I was hoping my new card could do that.I'm happy to say that my new card is not only much sharper, but I get around 30-40fps (depending on where I'm flying) with all settings maxed.  Lighting and shading seems better and the overall image is MUCH crisper than my previous PNY Nvidia 465.  After turning off ambient lighting and ground shadows, I get around 45-50fps and turning off traffic bumps it up to almost 60fps!  That's awesome compared to my old card.  And FS9 plays around 55-60fps with all settings maxed. : )It doesn't seem like a HUGE difference, but anyone who's played MSFS knows a gain of 5-15 fps can make all the difference.  An added bonus is that my new card runs about 10-15c cooler as well.  With my old card running FSX at max settings, I saw temps around 75-77c in a matter of seconds and had to use MSI Afterburner.  That's no where near threshold, but I like my gpu temps around 55-60 under full load if possible.  Haven't had ANY heat issues with my new card.Also received it in two days by UPS so was real happy there.  They also enclose this cool little silver EVGA sticker and a big poster of a skull - lol.All in all I'm super happy with the increase in rendering and FPS.  That in itself has made this purchase worth it.  I hope this review helps other flight sim fans as there doesn't seem to be many reviews out there for us.And one last thing, make sure you purchase the model ending in AR and not KR.  AR has a lifetime warranty but you have to register it in 30 days!

This is a thin, absorbent, small towel. I have a '99 car so have depended on FM transmitters for music and podcasts since 2007. At the time of writing, this unit may be the best available for the money.In the past I've used an iRiver and a Coby, both of which worked well while they were operational (I inadvertantly yanked a wire loose on the iRiver, but the Coby stopped working after about a year of uses). In general I've had clearer sound and better FM reception with transmitters whose tuners plug in directly to the lighter socket, than ones where there was a wire going out of a separate FM frequency tuner to the plug. Given that availability of FM transmitters appears to be declining as my older favorites are no longer sold, this one works acceptably well despite a lower output volume than on the iRiver and Coby units.  There is some hiss at times, and I believe it's because the listener must compensate for the lower output volume by turning up the volume further on the car radio. But I don't hear any audio distortion or static as I have on cheaper units, so music and podcasts sound acceptably good. I really don't care about the other features except that going down to 87.7 on the dial makes tuning easy (the iRiver couldn't go this low, but the Coby could), as I've never had any issue fighting with other radio stations at this frequency.As I'll be car shopping in another year or two, my years of relying on an FM transmitter are numbered. But I'm pleased enough with this one that I'd recommend it given the shrinking market for these devices.

Unless you have a drawer full of Wahoo products (HRM, Cadence, Speed sensor),I don't see the point of this. Yes you can use this while your iPhone safely tucked in your back jersey pocket, but ~$150 is a pretty stiff price. For less than this, you can get a Garmin Edge 200 on Amazon that is designed for bike and much more accurate than the $5 GPS sensor in the iPhone.  Of course you can't operate your iPhone music player, but you shouldn't do this while ridding anyway!Set this bike computer up involved only syncing it with your iPhone. I did this in 5 minutes and can see same information displayed on both iPhone app (yes, you have to install yet another app from the app store) and ready to go. The Wahoo attached to the bike handle bar via a couple of supplied rubber bands and mounting hardware. I had devices that mounted with rubber bands and in my opinion, the rubber band breaks after a few attaches and detaches. Another item you have to worry about.There is no rechargeable option, so when the battery runs out, I guess you would have to unscrew the back and replace it. I did not bother to look inside to see what battery is used as I don't really intend to use this computer anyway.So, you must have some special purpose to buy this computer. If you want a real bike computer with GPS, speed, distance, calories count, time.. etc..buy a Garmin. Save some bucks and works better.

I perused the other reviews here before attempting my first use of "Axe Defying Dust Messy Look."  I have choppy layers and love a messy look.  Informed, I knew to carefully put the powder on to avoid dust everywhere.  I opened the (small) container and put it directly on top of my head, tapping it on my head to release the dusty contents.  It was a clean application but perhaps not the wisest.  I then needed to spread the dust amongst my roots so I ran my fingers through my hair to do so.  Well, this dust feels nasty, it is not pleasant at all on your fingers.  You nor your significant other will not be running your fingers through your hair after applying this.  It took some time to spread the dust around all and really blend it in so I didn't have a chalky white mess on the roots of my brunette head.  When I was done spreading and cringing, I happily reviewed the results in the mirror.  I was more than pleased, my fine thin hair had volume and a adorably quirky bedhead appeal.  I am a girl by the way, but my man-friend also used it to similar success.  I want to give this 5 stars for the final performance but can't because it's just to nasty to touch your hair with this in.For the curious, this washed out easily with my normal shampoo.

I'll get right to it...Pros:ComfortSound QualityLight weightLooksFlat cable from the left sideSound isolation is pretty decent.In-line mic and controls for mobile phone as a source.Cons:There is no 1/4 to 1/8 adapter included with the Hitz.Inefficient (I have to play these much louder than the CAL! HPs to get the same sound level out of the Hitz).I have a pair ofCreative Aurvana Live! Headphonesthat have impressed me since the day I got them. Unfortunately, the comfort - or lack thereof, impressed me in a negative way.The Creative Hitz MA-2400 doesn't fall too short of it's sibling in terms of sound quality. The CAL! digs a little deeper than the Hitz but it's a close call.I prefer the look, the comfort, and the single sided cable of the Hitz.The biggest differences are that the Hitz are designed for portability, while the CAL! are not and that the CAL! is an over-the-ear design, while the Hitz is an on-the-ear design.In the beginning, I found the Hitz to be very muddled with little in the way of separation between the instruments. The bass was muddy and loose and the high notes were very recessed. I let them play for about a week, while connected to my home stereo. After the week was out, these HPs improved significantly. I am not a believer in "burn-in" so, I don't know how to explain the transformation. All I know is, they sound fantastic now.For me, the Hitz were a bit of a surprise. I was expecting CREATIVE's signature sound quality, but it wasn't there in the beginning. It is now, and that is all that matters.

I did a lot of research before purchasing my tablet.  Unfortunately, one of the things I did not consider in my decision was a docking station.  The Acer A500 is a great tablet, and I am more than happy with it.  However, as good as the tablet is, that's how badly the docking station stinks.  Almost every other manufacturer which I investigated has a much better docking station than Acer.  Fortunately, the docking station was an afterthought, and I really don't NEED one--it was more of a "nice to have".  Having had one, and returning it, it's not really "nice to have".  1) No power cord.  Really?  2) No HDMI output.  Again, really?  3) Audio output doesn't work with Amazon MP3 or Google Music players.  Sad.  4) The whole thing seemed just a little unstable, as if it would be ridiculously easy to tip over.  Bought one of those leather cases that let you use it as a stand--much, much better.  Bottom line: if the docking station is very important to you, avoid Acer--you'll be sorely disappointed.
